topics.title: Decision-Making Procedure Under Certainty and Under Uncertainty - Operations Research - Probability and Decision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT757 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Decision-Making Procedure Under Certainty and Under Uncertainty - Operations Research - Probability and Decision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Decision-Making in Operations Research

**Decision-Making** is the cognitive process of selecting a logical choice from the available options to achieve a specific objective. In Operations Research (OR), decision-making is a structured, quantitative process that converts raw data, alternatives, and uncertain outcomes into an optimal course of action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> A **Decision Problem** in OR is a situation in which an individual (the **Decision Maker / DM**) must select exactly one course of action from a set of available alternatives, where each action leads to a set of possible outcomes governed by factors partially or fully beyond the DM's control (states of nature).

### 1.2 Three Pillars of a Decision-Making Framework

Every decision problem in OR is built upon three fundamental building blocks:

1. **The Decision Maker (DM)** — The person, group, or organization that must commit to a final choice.
2. **The Alternative Courses of Action ($A_i$)** — The set of feasible options $A = \{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_m\}$ available to the DM. For KTU 2024, you will typically see **2 to 5 alternatives**.
3. **The States of Nature ($\theta_j$)** — The set of future events or conditions $\Theta = \{\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n\}$ that are NOT under the control of the DM. Each state yields a specific payoff (profit, cost, utility) when paired with an alternative.

### 1.3 Classification of Decision Environments

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important classification criterion is whether the **probabilities** of the states of nature are known.

| Environment Type | Payoff Known? | State Probabilities Known? | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Certainty** | Yes (single outcome) | Yes (deterministic = 1) | Fixed deposit interest, definite contract payoffs |
| **Risk (Probabilistic)** | Yes (multi-outcome) | Yes (objective or subjective) | Insurance pricing, market launch, crop yield |
| **Uncertainty** | Yes (multi-outcome) | **No probabilities available** | New product launch in unstudied market, strategic policy decisions |
| **Conflict (Game Theory)** | Depends on opponent's move | Depends on rival's strategy | Auctions, pricing wars, military strategy |

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Farmer Choosing a Crop**
>
> Imagine **Kerala-based farmer Anitha** must choose between planting **Rice, Coconut, or Vegetables**. She cannot control the **monsoon rainfall** (the state of nature), which may be **Heavy, Normal, or Poor**.
>
> - If she knew the exact rainfall for the year, she would have a **Decision under Certainty**.
> - If historical IMD data gave her rainfall **probabilities** (e.g., 40% normal, 30% heavy, 30% poor), she would have a **Decision under Risk**.
> - If no rainfall records existed (a brand-new climate pattern), she would face a **Decision under Uncertainty** — the focus of this lecture note.

---

### 1.5 Payoff Table — The Foundational Data Structure

Every decision problem in this module is represented as a rectangular **Payoff Table (or Decision Matrix)**:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|cccc|}
\hline
\textbf{Alternatives} \downarrow \;\; / \;\; \textbf{States} \rightarrow & \theta_1 & \theta_2 & \cdots & \theta_n \\
\hline
A_1 & v_{11} & v_{12} & \cdots & v_{1n} \\
A_2 & v_{21} & v_{22} & \cdots & v_{2n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
A_m & v_{m1} & v_{m2} & \cdots & v_{mn} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Where $v_{ij}$ is the **payoff** (could be **profit**, **cost**, or **utility**) obtained when alternative $A_i$ is chosen and state $\theta_j$ occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Profit Payoff vs. Cost Payoff:**
> - If $v_{ij}$ represents **profit/revenue**, the DM seeks the **Maximum**.
> - If $v_{ij}$ represents **cost/loss**, the DM seeks the **Minimum**.
> Always check this before applying Maximin, Minimax, etc. — examiners deduct marks for misapplied direction.

---

### 1.6 Decision-Making Under Certainty — The Simplest Case

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision under Certainty** exists when each alternative leads to exactly **one** known outcome (only one state of nature has a probability of **1**). The choice reduces to a deterministic comparison of payoffs.

- The optimal alternative is simply $A^{*} = \arg\max_{i} v_{i}$ (profit case) or $A^{*} = \arg\min_{i} v_{i}$ (cost case).
- No statistical reasoning is required.
- These problems are typically solved using **Linear Programming, Assignment, Transportation, or Network Models** — covered later in this course.

**KTU Highlight:** Direct certainty-based comparison is rare in this module; however, the **value of perfect information** and **deterministic conversion factors** assume certainty as a baseline.

---

### 1.7 Decision-Making Under Uncertainty — The Core Focus of Module 1

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision under Uncertainty** exists when the DM faces multiple states of nature, but the **probabilities of these states are not known, not assigned, or not reliable**. The DM must choose a course of action using a **decision rule (criterion)** based purely on the payoff structure and personal attitude toward risk.

**The Six Classical Criteria (Must Memorize for KTU):**

| # | Criterion Name | Decision Attitude | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Maximax** (Optimistic) | Aggressive / Best-Case Seeker | High |
| 2 | **Maximin** (Pessimistic) | Conservative / Worst-Case Protector | High |
| 3 | **Minimax Regret** (Savage) | Minimizes post-hoc disappointment | High |
| 4 | **Hurwicz** (Weighted Optimism-Pessimism) | Balanced realism | Medium |
| 5 | **Laplace** (Principle of Insufficient Reason) | Rational / Equal-Likelihood | Medium |
| 6 | **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** | Risk / Probabilistic | High (Risk, not Uncertainty, but commonly tested) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Maximin and Minimax are commonly confused. **Maximin** is for **profit** tables (max of the **min**imums). **Minimax** is for **cost** tables (min of the **max**imums) — and also the criterion for **regret** tables (min of the max regrets).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Six Decision Criteria — Detailed Algorithmic Steps

Let $A_i$ be the $i$-th alternative ($i = 1, 2, \dots, m$) and $\theta_j$ be the $j$-th state of nature ($j = 1, 2, \dots, n$). Let $v_{ij}$ denote the payoff.

---

#### 2.1.1 Maximax Criterion (Optimistic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Philosophy:** "Hope for the best! Choose the alternative with the highest possible payoff."

- **Step 1:** For each alternative $A_i$, find the **best possible payoff**:

$$
B_i = \max_{j} \{v_{ij}\}
$$

- **Step 2:** Choose the alternative with the **maximum** of these best payoffs:

$$
A^{*} = \arg\max_{i} \{B_i\}
$$

- **Index:** $V_{Maximax} = \max_i \max_j v_{ij}$

> **Engineering Utility:** Used in capital budgeting when ventures have limited downside and high upside (e.g., R&D project selection, speculative engineering bids).

---

#### 2.1.2 Maximin Criterion (Pessimistic / Wald's Criterion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Philosophy:** "Prepare for the worst! Choose the alternative whose worst-case payoff is the best."

- **Step 1:** For each $A_i$, find the **worst payoff** (minimum):

$$
W_i = \min_{j} \{v_{ij}\}
$$

- **Step 2:** Choose the alternative with the **maximum** of these minimums:

$$
A^{*} = \arg\max_{i} \{W_i\}
$$

- **Index:** $V_{Maximin} = \max_i \min_j v_{ij}$

> **Engineering Utility:** Used in safety-critical engineering (nuclear shutdown systems, aerospace redundant systems, civil defense infrastructure).

---

#### 2.1.3 Minimax Regret Criterion (Savage Criterion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Philosophy:** "Minimize the regret of not having chosen the best alternative in hindsight." Converts the problem into a cost-minimization exercise via the **Regret (Opportunity Loss) Matrix**.

**Procedure:**

- **Step 1 — Build the Regret Matrix:** For each column $j$, compute the **column maximum** $M_j = \max_i \{v_{ij}\}$. Then:

$$
r_{ij} = M_j - v_{ij}
$$

This $r_{ij}$ measures how much payoff is "lost" by not picking the best alternative for that state.

- **Step 2 — For each alternative, find the maximum regret:**

$$
R_i = \max_{j} \{r_{ij}\}
$$

- **Step 3 — Choose the alternative with the minimum of these maximum regrets:**

$$
A^{*} = \arg\min_{i} \{R_i\}
$$

- **Index:** $V_{Minimax\ Regret} = \min_i \max_j r_{ij}$

> **Engineering Utility:** Supply chain selection when the DM wants to balance exploration across multiple vendor options.

---

#### 2.1.4 Hurwicz Criterion (Weighted Criterion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Philosophy:** "Be neither too optimistic nor too pessimistic. Weight optimism with a coefficient $\alpha$."

Let $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ be the **coefficient of optimism** (assigned by the DM).

- **Step 1:** For each $A_i$, compute the **Hurwicz Value:**

$$
H_i = \alpha \cdot \max_j \{v_{ij}\} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \min_j \{v_{ij}\}
$$

- **Step 2:** Choose:

$$
A^{*} = \arg\max_{i} \{H_i\}
$$

- **Special Cases:**
  - $\alpha = 1 \Rightarrow$ **Maximax** (pure optimism)
  - $\alpha = 0 \Rightarrow$ **Maximin** (pure pessimism)
  - $\alpha = 0.5 \Rightarrow$ Equal balance

> **Engineering Utility:** Project portfolio selection where management's risk appetite is known (e.g., $\alpha = 0.6$ for a moderately aggressive firm).

---

#### 2.1.5 Laplace Criterion (Principle of Insufficient Reason)

> [!NOTE]
> **Philosophy:** "When we have no information, assume all states are equally likely." Assigns equal probability $1/n$ to every state.

- **Step 1:** For each $A_i$, compute the **average payoff:**

$$
L_i = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{ij}
$$

- **Step 2:** Choose:

$$
A^{*} = \arg\max_{i} \{L_i\}
$$

> **Engineering Utility:** Used as a neutral baseline when no subjective probability is available and no risk appetite is specified.

---

#### 2.1.6 Expected Monetary Value (EMV) — Decision Under Risk

> [!NOTE]
> **Philosophy:** When probabilities $P(\theta_j) = p_j$ are available, the criterion is the mathematical expectation of payoff.

$$
\text{EMV}(A_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j \cdot v_{ij}
$$

- **Optimal:** $A^{*} = \arg\max_{i} \text{EMV}(A_i)$ (for profit tables).
- **For cost tables:** $A^{*} = \arg\min_{i} \text{EMV}(A_i)$.

> **Engineering Utility:** Insurance premium design, expected project ROI calculations, real-estate investment appraisal.

---

### 2.2 Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EVPI** is the **maximum amount a rational DM should pay** to obtain perfect (100% accurate) information about which state of nature will occur. It quantifies the value of eliminating uncertainty.

**Procedure:**

- **Step 1:** For each state $\theta_j$, identify the **best payoff**:

$$
v^{*}_j = \max_{i} \{v_{ij}\}
$$

- **Step 2:** Compute the **Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI):**

$$
\text{EVwPI} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j \cdot v^{*}_j
$$

- **Step 3:** Identify the **Expected Value without Perfect Information** (the best EMV):

$$
\text{EVwoPI} = \max_{i} \text{EMV}(A_i)
$$

- **Step 4:** Compute EVPI:

$$
\text{EVPI} = \text{EVwPI} - \text{EVwoPI}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** If EVPI is less than the cost of a market research study, **do not pay** for the study.

---

### 2.3 Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL) and EOL = EVPI

> [!NOTE]
> **EOL** uses the **regret matrix** weighted by state probabilities. It can be proven that:

$$
\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}(A_i)
$$

$$
\text{EOL}(A_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

> **KTU Note:** Many examiners ask students to verify the **EVPI = min EOL** identity numerically.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick reference for exam day — memorize these expressions:**

| Criterion | Formula (Profit Case) | Decision Rule | Key Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximax | $V_{MM} = \max_i \max_j v_{ij}$ | Take row max, then max of those | Optimistic index |
| Maximin | $V_{Mn} = \max_i \min_j v_{ij}$ | Take row min, then max of those | Pessimistic index |
| Minimax Regret | $V_{MR} = \min_i \max_j r_{ij}$ | $r_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$ | Balanced regret |
| Hurwicz | $H_i = \alpha \max_j v_{ij} + (1-\alpha) \min_j v_{ij}$ | $\max_i H_i$ | Tunable |
| Laplace | $L_i = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j} v_{ij}$ | $\max_i L_i$ | Equal-weight |
| EMV | $\text{EMV}(A_i) = \sum_j p_j v_{ij}$ | $\max_i \text{EMV}(A_i)$ | Risk-based |
| EVwPI | $\sum_j p_j \cdot \max_i v_{ij}$ | Per-state max, then weighted sum | — |
| EVPI | $\text{EVwPI} - \max_i \text{EMV}$ | Cap on info cost | — |
| EOL | $\text{EOL}(A_i) = \sum_j p_j r_{ij}$ | $\min_i \text{EOL}$ | Equals EVPI |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Decision Applications

| Application Area | Typical Decision | Criterion Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction Project Bidding** | Bid high / medium / low | Maximax / EMV |
| **Software Vendor Selection** | Choose between 3 vendors | Minimax Regret |
| **Aircraft Engine Maintenance** | Replace / repair / inspect | Maximin (safety) |
| **Crop Pattern Selection** | Rice, coconut, vegetables under rainfall | EMV + Hurwicz |
| **Pharmaceutical Drug Trials** | Invest in 3 candidate compounds | EMV with EVPI (inform sample size) |
| **Bridge Design Material** | Steel / concrete / composite | Laplace (equal-unknown loading) |
| **Kerala Flood-Resilient Housing** | Choose material grade | Maximin (catastrophic-loss avoidance) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a question states "the probabilities are not known" or "no information is available," it is asking for **uncertainty** criteria. When probabilities are given, it is **risk**, and you should use **EMV/EVPI**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Illustrative Master Problem — Setting Up the Numerical Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Comprehensive Worked Example):**
> A Kerala-based electronics manufacturer must choose between three new product lines: **Smart Speaker (A1), Smart Watch (A2), Smart Bulb (A3)**. The market demand may be **High (θ1), Medium (θ2), or Low (θ3)**. The following **profit payoff matrix** (in ₹ Lakhs) is given:

| Alternatives ↓ / States → | High Demand $\theta_1$ | Medium Demand $\theta_2$ | Low Demand $\theta_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smart Speaker $A_1$ | **20** | 10 | 4 |
| Smart Watch $A_2$ | 16 | **14** | 6 |
| Smart Bulb $A_3$ | 12 | 9 | **7** |

Apply **all six criteria** and identify the optimal decision under each. Given the company has been offered a market research study that perfectly predicts demand. State probabilities are:
$$p(\theta_1) = 0.3, \quad p(\theta_2) = 0.5, \quad p(\theta_3) = 0.2$$
Compute EMV, EVwPI, and EVPI.

---

### 3.2 Criterion 1 — Maximax (Optimistic)

**Step 1:** Find the best payoff in each row (across all states):

For $A_1$: $B_1 = \max\{20, 10, 4\} = 20$
For $A_2$: $B_2 = \max\{16, 14, 6\} = 16$
For $A_3$: $B_3 = \max\{12, 9, 7\} = 12$

**Step 2:** Choose the alternative with the maximum of $B_i$:

$$
V_{Maximax} = \max\{20, 16, 12\} = 20
$$

**Decision:** $A_1$ (Smart Speaker), with optimistic payoff of **₹20 Lakhs**.

> **Interpretation:** If the company is fully optimistic and trusts the best-case scenario, it should launch the Smart Speaker.

---

### 3.3 Criterion 2 — Maximin (Pessimistic / Wald)

**Step 1:** Find the worst payoff in each row:

For $A_1$: $W_1 = \min\{20, 10, 4\} = 4$
For $A_2$: $W_2 = \min\{16, 14, 6\} = 6$
For $A_3$: $W_3 = \min\{12, 9, 7\} = 7$

**Step 2:** Choose the alternative with the maximum of $W_i$:

$$
V_{Maximin} = \max\{4, 6, 7\} = 7
$$

**Decision:** $A_3$ (Smart Bulb), with guaranteed floor of **₹7 Lakhs**.

> **Interpretation:** A risk-averse DM selects Smart Bulb because its worst-case (₹7 L) is the best worst-case among the three.

---

### 3.4 Criterion 3 — Minimax Regret (Savage)

**Step 1:** Compute column maxima of the original payoff table:

$\max$ in column $\theta_1 = \max\{20, 16, 12\} = 20$
$\max$ in column $\theta_2 = \max\{10, 14, 9\} = 14$
$\max$ in column $\theta_3 = \max\{4, 6, 7\} = 7$

**Step 2:** Construct the Regret Matrix $r_{ij} = M_j - v_{ij}$:

$$
r_{11} = 20 - 20 = 0, \quad r_{12} = 14 - 10 = 4, \quad r_{13} = 7 - 4 = 3
$$
$$
r_{21} = 20 - 16 = 4, \quad r_{22} = 14 - 14 = 0, \quad r_{23} = 7 - 6 = 1
$$
$$
r_{31} = 20 - 12 = 8, \quad r_{32} = 14 - 9 = 5, \quad r_{33} = 7 - 7 = 0
$$

The regret table is:

| Regret $r_{ij}$ | $\theta_1$ | $\theta_2$ | $\theta_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ | 0 | 4 | 3 |
| $A_2$ | 4 | 0 | 1 |
| $A_3$ | 8 | 5 | 0 |

**Step 3:** Find the maximum regret per row:

$R_1 = \max\{0, 4, 3\} = 4$
$R_2 = \max\{4, 0, 1\} = 4$
$R_3 = \max\{8, 5, 0\} = 8$

**Step 4:** Choose the minimum of the maximum regrets:

$$
V_{Minimax\ Regret} = \min\{4, 4, 8\} = 4
$$

**Decision:** **Tie between $A_1$ and $A_2$**. Report both as candidates.

---

### 3.5 Criterion 4 — Hurwicz Criterion (with $\alpha = 0.6$)

Using the **coefficient of optimism** $\alpha = 0.6$ (assume DM is moderately optimistic).

For $A_1$:

$$
H_1 = 0.6 \times \max\{20,10,4\} + 0.4 \times \min\{20,10,4\}
$$
$$
H_1 = 0.6 \times 20 + 0.4 \times 4 = 12 + 1.6 = 13.6
$$

For $A_2$:

$$
H_2 = 0.6 \times \max\{16,14,6\} + 0.4 \times \min\{16,14,6\}
$$
$$
H_2 = 0.6 \times 16 + 0.4 \times 6 = 9.6 + 2.4 = 12.0
$$

For $A_3$:

$$
H_3 = 0.6 \times \max\{12,9,7\} + 0.4 \times \min\{12,9,7\}
$$
$$
H_3 = 0.6 \times 12 + 0.4 \times 7 = 7.2 + 2.8 = 10.0
$$

**Decision:** $\max\{13.6, 12.0, 10.0\} = 13.6$ → **$A_1$ (Smart Speaker)**.

> **Sanity Check:** $\alpha = 1$ would yield $A_1$ (Maximax), $\alpha = 0$ would yield $A_3$ (Maximin). The Hurwicz result $A_1$ at $\alpha = 0.6$ is consistent.

---

### 3.6 Criterion 5 — Laplace Criterion (Equal Likelihood)

Since there are 3 states, assign $p_j = 1/3$ to each.

For $A_1$:

$$
L_1 = \frac{20 + 10 + 4}{3} = \frac{34}{3} \approx 11.33
$$

For $A_2$:

$$
L_2 = \frac{16 + 14 + 6}{3} = \frac{36}{3} = 12.00
$$

For $A_3$:

$$
L_3 = \frac{12 + 9 + 7}{3} = \frac{28}{3} \approx 9.33
$$

**Decision:** $\max\{11.33, 12.00, 9.33\} = 12.00$ → **$A_2$ (Smart Watch)**.

---

### 3.7 Criterion 6 — Expected Monetary Value (EMV) with Probabilities

Given $p_1 = 0.3, \; p_2 = 0.5, \; p_3 = 0.2$.

For $A_1$:

$$
\text{EMV}(A_1) = (0.3)(20) + (0.5)(10) + (0.2)(4)
$$
$$
\text{EMV}(A_1) = 6.0 + 5.0 + 0.8 = 11.8
$$

For $A_2$:

$$
\text{EMV}(A_2) = (0.3)(16) + (0.5)(14) + (0.2)(6)
$$
$$
\text{EMV}(A_2) = 4.8 + 7.0 + 1.2 = 13.0
$$

For $A_3$:

$$
\text{EMV}(A_3) = (0.3)(12) + (0.5)(9) + (0.2)(7)
$$
$$
\text{EMV}(A_3) = 3.6 + 4.5 + 1.4 = 9.5
$$

**Decision:** $\max\{11.8, 13.0, 9.5\} = 13.0$ → **$A_2$ (Smart Watch)**.

---

### 3.8 Computing EVwPI and EVPI

**Step 1:** Column maxima of the original table:
$v^*_1 = 20, \; v^*_2 = 14, \; v^*_3 = 7$ (already computed in 3.4).

**Step 2:** Expected Value with Perfect Information:

$$
\text{EVwPI} = (0.3)(20) + (0.5)(14) + (0.2)(7)
$$
$$
\text{EVwPI} = 6.0 + 7.0 + 1.4 = 14.4
$$

**Step 3:** EVwoPI = best EMV from Section 3.7:

$$
\text{EVwoPI} = \max\{11.8, 13.0, 9.5\} = 13.0
$$

**Step 4:** EVPI:

$$
\text{EVPI} = \text{EVwPI} - \text{EVwoPI} = 14.4 - 13.0 = 1.4
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The firm should pay **at most ₹1.4 Lakhs** for the perfect-demand market study. If the consulting firm quotes more than this, the study is not worth it.

---

### 3.9 Verification via EOL = EVPI

Using the regret table from 3.4 and the probabilities:

For $A_1$:

$$
\text{EOL}(A_1) = (0.3)(0) + (0.5)(4) + (0.2)(3) = 0 + 2.0 + 0.6 = 2.6
$$

For $A_2$:

$$
\text{EOL}(A_2) = (0.3)(4) + (0.5)(0) + (0.2)(1) = 1.2 + 0 + 0.2 = 1.4
$$

For $A_3$:

$$
\text{EOL}(A_3) = (0.3)(8) + (0.5)(5) + (0.2)(0) = 2.4 + 2.5 + 0 = 4.9
$$

Minimum EOL = $\min\{2.6, 1.4, 4.9\} = 1.4$ ✓

> **Identity confirmed:** $\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}(A_i) = 1.4$ **Lakhs** ✓

---

### 3.10 Decision Under Certainty — Sub-Problem Illustration

Suppose the firm commissions a perfect market study and learns that demand will be **High** with certainty. Then:

- Payoff column reduces to $\theta_1$: $\{20, 16, 12\}$.
- Optimal: $A_1$ (Smart Speaker) with profit **₹20 Lakhs**.
- This is a **decision under certainty** — a single state, single optimal alternative.

If perfect information revealed **Medium** demand:

- Payoff column: $\{10, 14, 9\}$ → $A_2$ (Smart Watch), **₹14 Lakhs**.

If **Low** demand:

- Payoff column: $\{4, 6, 7\}$ → $A_3$ (Smart Bulb), **₹7 Lakhs**.

> **Consistency check:** $A_1$ is chosen for high demand, $A_2$ for medium, $A_3$ for low — this is exactly what the column maxima $v^*_j$ used in EVwPI reflect.

---

### 3.11 Comprehensive Results Table — At a Glance

| Criterion | Decision | Index Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximax | $A_1$ | 20 | Optimistic |
| Maximin | $A_3$ | 7 | Pessimistic |
| Minimax Regret | $A_1$ or $A_2$ (tie) | 4 | Regret-balanced |
| Hurwicz ($\alpha = 0.6$) | $A_1$ | 13.6 | Moderately optimistic |
| Laplace | $A_2$ | 12.00 | Equal-weight |
| EMV | $A_2$ | 13.0 | Risk-based |
| EVPI | — | 1.4 | Value of information |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer-Writing Tip:** Always conclude each criterion with a one-line justification. E.g., "Since $\alpha = 0.6$ reflects moderate optimism, and $H_1 = 13.6$ is the highest, $A_1$ is recommended under Hurwicz."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Decision-Making Environment Classification Tree

The following block diagram maps the **classification logic** of decision environments, which is the foundational concept of this module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Decision Problem Encountered"] --> B{"Are State\nProbabilities Known?"}
    B -- "Yes: Deterministic" --> C["Decision Under Certainty\nSingle outcome per action"]
    B -- "Yes: Stochastic" --> D["Decision Under Risk\nEMV, EVPI, EOL"]
    B -- "No Probabilities" --> E["Decision Under Uncertainty\nMaximax, Maximin, etc."]
    C --> F1["Linear / Integer Programming\nNetwork Models"]
    D --> F2["Bayesian Decision Trees\nExpected Utility"]
    E --> F3["Savage, Hurwicz, Laplace\nWald Criteria"]
    E --> G1{"Multiple Adversarial\nDecision Makers?"}
    G1 -- "Yes" --> G2["Game Theory / Conflict Models"]
    G1 -- "No" --> G3["Single DM Uncertainty Models"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above clarifies the **placement** of each criterion. The "uncertainty" path is the focus of Module 1. The "risk" path is also heavily tested because EVPI / EOL calculations require explicit probabilities.

---

### 4.2 Decision Criterion Selection Flowchart

This functional block diagram helps students **choose the right criterion** for a given KTU problem statement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Start: Payoff Table + Question Type"] --> P1{"Is the Problem\nProfit or Cost?"}
    P1 -- "Profit" --> P2["Use Maximizing\nCriteria"]
    P1 -- "Cost" --> P3["Use Minimizing\nCriteria (Maximin becomes Minimin)"]
    P2 --> P4{"Are State\nProbabilities Given?"}
    P4 -- "No" --> P5["Apply Maximax, Maximin,\nMinimax Regret, Hurwicz, Laplace"]
    P4 -- "Yes" --> P6["Apply EMV, EVPI, EOL\nBayesian Methods"]
    P5 --> P7["Identify DM Risk Attitude\nOptimistic / Pessimistic / Neutral"]
    P7 --> P8["Pick Matching Criterion:\nMaximax / Maximin / Hurwicz"]
    P6 --> P9["Compute EMV per alternative\nPick highest = optimal"]
    P9 --> P10["Compute EVwPI, EVPI\nDetermine info value"]
    P3 --> P5
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree Schematic for EMV / EVPI Computation

This block-level architecture visualizes how state probabilities flow into the decision tree, leading to EMV and EVPI calculations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0["Decision Node:\nChoose Ai"] --> D1["Alternative A1"]
    D0 --> D2["Alternative A2"]
    D0 --> D3["Alternative A3"]
    D1 --> S1A["State theta1\nP = 0.3\nPayoff = v11"]
    D1 --> S1B["State theta2\nP = 0.5\nPayoff = v12"]
    D1 --> S1C["State theta3\nP = 0.2\nPayoff = v13"]
    D2 --> S2A["State theta1\nP = 0.3\nPayoff = v21"]
    D2 --> S2B["State theta2\nP = 0.5\nPayoff = v22"]
    D2 --> S2C["State theta3\nP = 0.2\nPayoff = v23"]
    D3 --> S3A["State theta1\nP = 0.3\nPayoff = v31"]
    D3 --> S3B["State theta2\nP = 0.5\nPayoff = v32"]
    D3 --> S3C["State theta3\nP = 0.2\nPayoff = v33"]
    S1A --> R1["Roll-back: Sum P times v\n= EMV A1 = 11.8"]
    S1B --> R1
    S1C --> R1
    S2A --> R2["EMV A2 = 13.0"]
    S2B --> R2
    S2C --> R2
    S3A --> R3["EMV A3 = 9.5"]
    S3B --> R3
    S3C --> R3
    R1 --> FB["Best EMV = 13.0\nSelected: A2"]
    R2 --> FB
    R3 --> FB
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When the question specifies a decision tree, you can reference this schematic. The **roll-back** technique is essential — compute payoffs from right to left, multiplying by probabilities at each chance node.

---

### 4.4 Regret Matrix Construction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["Original Payoff Table v_ij"] --> B1["Step 1: Compute Column Max\nM_j = max_i v_ij"]
    B1 --> B2["Step 2: Regret = M_j minus v_ij\nfor every cell"]
    B2 --> B3["Step 3: Row Max of Regret\nR_i = max_j r_ij"]
    B3 --> B4["Step 4: Min of Row Max\nV = min_i R_i"]
    B4 --> B5["Final: A* with min V"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Regret Matrix workflow is a **favourite KTU sub-question** worth 7 marks. Practice the four steps until they are second nature.

---

### 4.5 Hurwicz Criterion Sensitivity Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0["Hurwicz Analysis"] --> H1{"Coefficient of\nOptimism alpha?"}
    H1 -- "alpha = 1" --> H2["Reduces to Maximax"]
    H1 -- "alpha = 0" --> H3["Reduces to Maximin"]
    H1 -- "alpha between 0 and 1" --> H4["Compute H_i for each Ai\nPick max H_i"]
    H4 --> H5["Sensitivity:\nIf alpha changes, optimal may change\nPlot H_i vs alpha to find break-even"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Some students forget that **alpha is given in the problem** — never assume. Read the problem statement carefully.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question Paper, 3 Marks]**

> Define the terms **"State of Nature"** and **"Payoff"** in the context of decision-making under uncertainty. Briefly distinguish between decision-making under **risk** and decision-making under **uncertainty**.

**Model Answer:**

A **State of Nature** $\theta_j$ refers to any future event or condition that is **not under the control of the decision maker** but which influences the payoff of the chosen alternative. Examples include market demand, rainfall, and equipment failure.

A **Payoff** $v_{ij}$ is the **consequence** (profit, cost, or utility) resulting from selecting alternative $A_i$ when state $\theta_j$ materializes.

**Distinction:**

- **Decision under Risk:** Probabilities of the states of nature are **known or estimable**, and the criterion is typically the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)**.
- **Decision under Uncertainty:** Probabilities of the states are **not known**, and the criterion is based on heuristics such as **Maximax, Maximin, Minimax Regret, Hurwicz, or Laplace**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2022, 3 Marks]**

> State and explain the **Minimax Regret Criterion**. What type of decision maker attitude does it represent?

**Model Answer:**

The **Minimax Regret (Savage) Criterion** is a decision rule that:

1. Converts the payoff table into a **Regret (Opportunity Loss) Table** using $r_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$.
2. For each alternative, finds the **maximum regret** $R_i = \max_j r_{ij}$.
3. Selects the alternative that **minimizes** this maximum regret: $A^{*} = \arg\min_i R_i$.

**Attitude:** It represents a **risk-averse and hindsight-oriented** decision maker who wishes to **minimize the worst possible post-decision disappointment**, regardless of which state actually occurs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

> A construction company in Kerala must decide whether to build a **Small (S)**, **Medium (M)**, or **Large (L)** commercial complex. The future demand may be **High (H), Medium (M'), or Low (L')**. The following profit payoffs (in ₹ Crores) are estimated:

| Alternatives ↓ / States → | High $H$ | Medium $M'$ | Low $L'$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small $S$ | 50 | 30 | 20 |
| Medium $M$ | 80 | 60 | 30 |
| Large $L$ | 120 | 70 | 10 |

>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Apply the following decision criteria and recommend the best alternative:
> (i) Maximax, (ii) Maximin, (iii) Minimax Regret, (iv) Hurwicz with $\alpha = 0.7$.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] The company has conducted a market study that estimates $P(H) = 0.4$, $P(M') = 0.4$, $P(L') = 0.2$. Compute the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** of each alternative, find the **EVwPI**, **EVPI**, and **EOL**. Also, verify that $\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}(A_i)$. State whether the company should pay ₹10 Lakhs for a perfect-information study.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**(i) Maximax:**

Row maxima: $S \to 50$, $M \to 80$, $L \to 120$.

$V_{Maximax} = \max\{50, 80, 120\} = 120$ → **Choose $L$ (Large)**.

**(ii) Maximin:**

Row minima: $S \to 20$, $M \to 30$, $L \to 10$.

$V_{Maximin} = \max\{20, 30, 10\} = 30$ → **Choose $M$ (Medium)**.

**(iii) Minimax Regret:**

Column maxima: $H \to 120$, $M' \to 70$, $L' \to 30$.

Regret matrix:

| Regret | $H$ | $M'$ | $L'$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | $120-50 = 70$ | $70-30 = 40$ | $30-20 = 10$ |
| $M$ | $120-80 = 40$ | $70-60 = 10$ | $30-30 = 0$ |
| $L$ | $120-120 = 0$ | $70-70 = 0$ | $30-10 = 20$ |

Row max of regret: $S \to 70$, $M \to 40$, $L \to 20$.

$V_{Minimax\ Regret} = \min\{70, 40, 20\} = 20$ → **Choose $L$ (Large)**.

**(iv) Hurwicz with $\alpha = 0.7$:**

For $S$:

$$
H_S = 0.7 \times 50 + 0.3 \times 20 = 35 + 6 = 41
$$

For $M$:

$$
H_M = 0.7 \times 80 + 0.3 \times 30 = 56 + 9 = 65
$$

For $L$:

$$
H_L = 0.7 \times 120 + 0.3 \times 10 = 84 + 3 = 87
$$

$V_{Hurwicz} = \max\{41, 65, 87\} = 87$ → **Choose $L$ (Large)**.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**EMV Calculations:**

For $S$:

$$
\text{EMV}(S) = (0.4)(50) + (0.4)(30) + (0.2)(20) = 20 + 12 + 4 = 36
$$

For $M$:

$$
\text{EMV}(M) = (0.4)(80) + (0.4)(60) + (0.2)(30) = 32 + 24 + 6 = 62
$$

For $L$:

$$
\text{EMV}(L) = (0.4)(120) + (0.4)(70) + (0.2)(10) = 48 + 28 + 2 = 78
$$

**Best EMV:** $\max\{36, 62, 78\} = 78$ → **Choose $L$** with **EMV = ₹78 Crores**.

**EVwPI** (column maxima weighted by probabilities):

$$
\text{EVwPI} = (0.4)(120) + (0.4)(70) + (0.2)(30) = 48 + 28 + 6 = 82
$$

**EVPI:**

$$
\text{EVPI} = 82 - 78 = 4 \;\; \text{Crores} = 400 \;\; \text{Lakhs}
$$

**EOL Calculations (using regret matrix and probabilities):**

For $S$:

$$
\text{EOL}(S) = (0.4)(70) + (0.4)(40) + (0.2)(10) = 28 + 16 + 2 = 46
$$

For $M$:

$$
\text{EOL}(M) = (0.4)(40) + (0.4)(10) + (0.2)(0) = 16 + 4 + 0 = 20
$$

For $L$:

$$
\text{EOL}(L) = (0.4)(0) + (0.4)(0) + (0.2)(20) = 0 + 0 + 4 = 4
$$

$\min_i \text{EOL}(A_i) = \min\{46, 20, 4\} = 4$ → **confirms EVPI = 4 Crores ✓**

**Decision on Perfect-Information Study:**

The EVPI is **₹4 Crores = ₹400 Lakhs**. The company should be willing to pay up to **₹400 Lakhs** for the study. The quoted price of **₹10 Lakhs is well below ₹400 Lakhs**, so **the company SHOULD buy the study**.

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Model Paper Style]**

> A pharmaceutical company is testing three drug compounds — **Compound X, Y, Z** — to launch in the Kerala market. The market response could be **Strong (S), Moderate (M), or Weak (W)**. The estimated **costs (in ₹ Lakhs)** are:

| Alternatives ↓ / States → | Strong $S$ | Moderate $M$ | Weak $W$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compound X | 20 | 35 | 60 |
| Compound Y | 30 | 25 | 50 |
| Compound Z | 45 | 30 | 20 |

>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Since the table represents **costs**, reformulate the analysis using the appropriate minimization criteria:
> (i) **Minimax** (min of the max cost),
> (ii) **Minimin** (min of the min cost, optimistic),
> (iii) **Minimax Regret**,
> (iv) **Hurwicz** with $\alpha = 0.3$ (interpret as cost-optimism).
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Subjective probability estimates are $P(S) = 0.5$, $P(M) = 0.3$, $P(W) = 0.2$. Compute the **Expected Cost** of each compound, find the minimum expected cost, and compute **EVPI**. Should the company invest ₹2 Lakhs in a regulatory prediction study?

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**(i) Minimax (Cost case — min of max cost):**

Row maxima: $X \to 60$, $Y \to 50$, $Z \to 45$.

$V_{Minimax} = \min\{60, 50, 45\} = 45$ → **Choose $Z$**.

**(ii) Minimin (Optimistic cost minimization):**

Row minima: $X \to 20$, $Y \to 25$, $Z \to 20$.

$V_{Minimin} = \min\{20, 25, 20\} = 20$ → **Tie: $X$ or $Z$**.

**(iii) Minimax Regret (Cost case — convert using $r_{ij} = v_{ij} - \min_k v_{kj}$):**

Column minima: $S \to 20$, $M \to 25$, $W \to 20$.

Regret matrix (cost regret = actual cost - column min):

| Regret | $S$ | $M$ | $W$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $X$ | $20-20 = 0$ | $35-25 = 10$ | $60-20 = 40$ |
| $Y$ | $30-20 = 10$ | $25-25 = 0$ | $50-20 = 30$ |
| $Z$ | $45-20 = 25$ | $30-25 = 5$ | $20-20 = 0$ |

Row max regrets: $X \to 40$, $Y \to 30$, $Z \to 25$.

$V_{Minimax\ Regret} = \min\{40, 30, 25\} = 25$ → **Choose $Z$**.

**(iv) Hurwicz (cost version, $\alpha = 0.3$ = coefficient of optimism on cost):**

For $X$:

$$
H_X = 0.3 \times \min + 0.7 \times \max = 0.3 \times 20 + 0.7 \times 60 = 6 + 42 = 48
$$

For $Y$:

$$
H_Y = 0.3 \times 25 + 0.7 \times 50 = 7.5 + 35 = 42.5
$$

For $Z$:

$$
H_Z = 0.3 \times 20 + 0.7 \times 45 = 6 + 31.5 = 37.5
$$

$V_{Hurwicz} = \min\{48, 42.5, 37.5\} = 37.5$ → **Choose $Z$**.

**Recommendation:** Compound $Z$ is selected by 3 out of 4 criteria (Minimax, Minimax Regret, Hurwicz). Compound $X$ and $Z$ tie on Minimin. **Choose $Z$**.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Expected Cost Calculations:**

For $X$:

$$
E(X) = (0.5)(20) + (0.3)(35) + (0.2)(60) = 10 + 10.5 + 12 = 32.5
$$

For $Y$:

$$
E(Y) = (0.5)(30) + (0.3)(25) + (0.2)(50) = 15 + 7.5 + 10 = 32.5
$$

For $Z$:

$$
E(Z) = (0.5)(45) + (0.3)(30) + (0.2)(20) = 22.5 + 9 + 4 = 35.5
$$

**Minimum Expected Cost:** $\min\{32.5, 32.5, 35.5\} = 32.5$ → **Tie: $X$ or $Y$**.

**EVwPI (column minima weighted by probabilities):**

$$
\text{EVwPI} = (0.5)(20) + (0.3)(25) + (0.2)(20) = 10 + 7.5 + 4 = 21.5
$$

**EVPI:**

$$
\text{EVPI} = 32.5 - 21.5 = 11 \;\; \text{Lakhs}
$$

**Decision on Prediction Study:**

The EVPI is **₹11 Lakhs**. The quoted study cost is only **₹2 Lakhs**, which is far below ₹11 Lakhs. **The company SHOULD pay ₹2 Lakhs** for the study because the information value (₹11 Lakhs) is more than five times the study cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Maximax vs. Maximin:** Maximax = max of max payoffs (optimistic). Maximin = max of min payoffs (pessimistic). Mixing them up is a guaranteed 7-mark loss.
> 2. **Profit vs. Cost Direction:** For **cost** tables, the criterion becomes **Minimax / Minimin / Minimax Regret**. Forgetting this inversion loses full marks.
> 3. **Regret Computation Errors:** For profit tables, $r_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$. For cost tables, $r_{ij} = v_{ij} - \min_k v_{kj}$. Wrong sign loses 3-4 marks.
> 4. **Hurwicz α Interpretation:** $\alpha$ is the **coefficient of optimism** (closer to 1 = more optimistic). It is GIVEN in the problem — never assume.
> 5. **EVPI vs. EOL Identity:** Examiners may ask to **verify** that $\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}(A_i)$. Always perform this cross-check.
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency:** Payoffs may be in Lakhs/Crores; EVPI inherits the same unit. Note the conversion in the final answer.
> 7. **Ties in Optimal Decision:** When two alternatives give the same index, mention both. Do not arbitrarily pick one.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

#### Conceptual Anchors
- **Decision under Certainty:** Single state of nature, deterministic, solved via LP/NLP/Networks.
- **Decision under Risk:** Multiple states, **known probabilities**, use **EMV / EVPI / EOL / Bayesian**.
- **Decision under Uncertainty:** Multiple states, **unknown probabilities**, use **Maximax, Maximin, Minimax Regret, Hurwicz, Laplace**.

#### Formula Anchors (Memorize)
- **Maximax:** $V = \max_i \max_j v_{ij}$
- **Maximin:** $V = \max_i \min_j v_{ij}$
- **Minimax Regret:** $r_{ij} = M_j - v_{ij}$ (profit) or $r_{ij} = v_{ij} - m_j$ (cost); $V = \min_i \max_j r_{ij}$
- **Hurwicz:** $H_i = \alpha \max_j v_{ij} + (1-\alpha) \min_j v_{ij}$ (profit); flip order for cost.
- **Laplace:** $L_i = \frac{1}{n} \sum_j v_{ij}$
- **EMV:** $E(A_i) = \sum_j p_j v_{ij}$
- **EVwPI:** $\sum_j p_j \max_i v_{ij}$
- **EVPI:** $\text{EVwPI} - \max_i \text{EMV}(A_i) = \min_i \text{EOL}(A_i)$

#### DM Attitude Mapping
- **Maximax** ↔ Optimistic / Risk-seeker
- **Maximin** ↔ Pessimistic / Risk-averter / Conservative
- **Hurwicz** ↔ Tunable / Balanced realism
- **Minimax Regret** ↔ Hindsight-minimizer / Opportunity-loss averse
- **Laplace** ↔ Rational-equalist / Information-agnostic
- **EMV** ↔ Risk-based / Long-run expected value

#### Process Checklist (Solve in this order)
1. Identify the **type of problem**: profit or cost.
2. Identify **probabilities** (given or not).
3. Build the **regret matrix** if criterion requires it.
4. Compute the criterion-specific index per alternative.
5. Select the alternative with the **best index** (max for profit, min for cost).
6. For EMV problems, also compute **EVwPI, EVPI, EOL**.
7. Cross-verify $\text{EVPI} = \min \text{EOL}$.
8. State the **decision** and the **value** clearly.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module 1 typically carries a **2-mark** Part A question and a **14-mark** Part B question (often with a 7+7 sub-part structure). Master the **Minimax Regret matrix construction** and the **EVPI/EOL identity** — these are the most commonly repeated high-value topics.

> **Module 1 Recap Status:** ✅ Completed. Proceed to **Module 2** only after solving at least 3 distinct end-of-module problems covering Maximax, EMV, and EVPI jointly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Making - Queuing or Waiting Line Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT757 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Making - Queuing or Waiting Line Theory # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Queuing Theory** (also called **Waiting Line Theory**) is the mathematical study of queues (waiting lines) and the stochastic processes governing the arrival of customers, their wait time in the queue, the service mechanism, and their eventual departure. It is a branch of **Operations Research** that uses **probability theory** to analyze the dynamic behavior of waiting-line systems and to optimize system performance by balancing customer wait costs with service-providing costs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Importance):** A queuing system is one where customers arrive at a service facility, wait their turn for service, receive service, and then depart. The primary objective of queuing analysis is to determine the **optimal service level** that minimizes the total expected cost (waiting cost + service cost). ## 1.2 Components of a Queuing System A queuing model is built upon six fundamental components: | # | Component | Description | |---|-----------|-------------| | 1 | **Arrival Pattern** | The statistical distribution governing the inter-arrival time of customers (e.g., Poisson, Exponential). | | 2 | **Service Pattern** | The statistical distribution governing how long service takes for each customer. | | 3 | **Queue Discipline** | The order in which customers are served — **FCFS** (First-Come-First-Served), **LCFS**, **SIRO** (Service-In-Random-Order), or **Priority**. | | 4 | **Number of Servers (c)** | The number of parallel service channels available. | | 5 | **System Capacity (K)** | The maximum number of customers allowed in the system (queue + service). | | 6 | **Population Size** | The total size of the calling source (finite or infinite). | ## 1.3 The Real-World Analogy 🏦 > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — A Day at the Bank Counter:** > > Imagine walking into a Kerala Gramin Bank at 10 AM. You join a line (**queue**) behind 4 other people. There is **one teller** (**single server**) who helps each customer in the order they arrived (**FCFS**). Customers walk in at random intervals (**Poisson arrivals**), and each transaction takes a different amount of time (**Exponential service**). > > The manager wants to know: *Should I open a second counter?* If yes, my operating cost goes up, but the customers wait less. Queuing Theory provides the **mathematical answer** to this exact trade-off. The same logic applies to **hospital OPDs, toll booths, ATM machines, call centers, and CPU process scheduling** in operating systems. ## 1.4 Why is Queuing Theory Important? - **Engineering Applications:** Teletraffic engineering, computer networks, manufacturing flow lines. - **Service Industry:** Hospital queue management, airport runway scheduling, restaurant design. - **Decision-Making:** Helps determine the **optimal number of servers (c)** to minimize the sum of service cost and customer wait cost. - **Performance Metrics:** Quantifies **waiting time, queue length, server utilization, and system throughput** mathematically. > [!TIP] > **KTU Key Insight:** In Operations Research, a "customer" is **not necessarily a human** — it can be a **packet in a router, a job in a CPU, a vehicle at a toll booth, or a part on an assembly line**. The mathematics remains identical. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Kendall's Notation (The Universal Language of Queues) D.G. Kendall introduced a compact six-symbol notation to describe any queuing system: $$(a/b/c):(d/e/f)$$ Where: - **a** = Inter-arrival time distribution - **b** = Service time distribution - **c** = Number of parallel servers - **d** = Queue discipline - **e** = Maximum system capacity - **f** = Population size > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Symbols Used:** > - **M** = Markovian (Poisson arrival / Exponential service) > - **D** = Deterministic (constant time) > - **Eₖ** = Erlang-k distribution > - **G** = General (any distribution) > - **FCFS** = First Come First Served (default if omitted) **The Most Common Model:** **(M/M/1):(FCFS/∞/∞)** — Poisson arrivals, exponential service, 1 server, infinite capacity, infinite population. ## 2.2 Performance Measures (Operating Characteristics) For any queuing system in **steady-state**, the following metrics are evaluated: | Symbol | Meaning | |--------|---------| | **λ** | Mean arrival rate (customers/unit time) | | **μ** | Mean service rate (customers/unit time) per server | | **ρ** | Traffic Intensity / Server Utilization = λ/μ (single server) or λ/(cμ) (c servers) | | **Pₙ** | Steady-state probability of exactly *n* customers in the system | | **L** | Expected (average) number of customers in the system | | **Lq** | Expected number of customers in the queue (excluding the one in service) | | **W** | Expected time a customer spends in the system (waiting + service) | | **Wq** | Expected time a customer waits in the queue | | **Lₛ** | Expected number of customers being served | > [!IMPORTANT] > **STABILITY CONDITION:** For a steady-state solution to exist, the traffic intensity must be **ρ < 1**, i.e., the service rate must exceed the arrival rate. Otherwise, the queue grows indefinitely. ## 2.3 Fundamental Relationships (Little's Laws) These two equations are **universal** and apply to **any** queuing model: $$L = \lambda W \quad \text{and} \quad L_q = \lambda W_q$$ The difference relations are: $$L = L_q + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \quad \text{and} \quad W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$$ ## 2.4 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Queuing Model | Key Formulas (Steady-State) | |---------------|-----------------------------| | **M/M/1** (Single Server) | $P_0 = 1 - \rho; \quad P_n = (1-\rho)\rho^n$ | | | $L = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$ | | | $L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{\mu(\mu-\lambda)}$ | | | $W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}; \quad W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu-\lambda)}$ | | **M/M/c** (Multi-Server, c channels) | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c\mu}$ | | | $P_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!} + \dfrac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)}}$ | | | $L_q = \dfrac{P_0 \cdot (\lambda/\mu)^c \cdot \rho}{c!\,(1-\rho)^2}$ | | | $W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda}; \quad L = L_q + \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | | **M/M/1/K** (Finite Capacity, K max) | $P_0 = \dfrac{1-\rho}{1-\rho^{K+1}}$ for $\rho \neq 1$; $P_0 = \dfrac{1}{K+1}$ for $\rho = 1$ | | | $P_n = P_0 \cdot \rho^n$ for $n = 0, 1, \dots, K$ | | | $L = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \dfrac{(K+1)\rho^{K+1}}{1-\rho^{K+1}}$ | | | Effective arrival rate: $\lambda_{eff} = \lambda(1-P_K)$ | | | $W = \dfrac{L}{\lambda_{eff}}; \quad W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda_{eff}}$ | | **M/M/∞** (Infinite Servers) | $P_n = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda/\mu}(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!}; \quad L = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of M/M/1 Steady-State Probabilities ### Setup For an M/M/1 system, the arrival rate and service rate are constants: - $\lambda_n = \lambda$ for all $n \geq 0$ (Poisson arrivals) - $\mu_n = \mu$ for all $n \geq 1$ (Exponential service) This is a **birth-death process** with constant rates. ### Derivation of $P_n$ At steady state, the **flow-balance equation** states that the rate of flow into any state equals the rate of flow out: $$\mu_{n+1} P_{n+1} - \lambda_n P_n = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{n+1} = \frac{\lambda_n}{\mu_{n+1}} P_n$$ Substituting $\lambda_n = \lambda$ and $\mu_{n+1} = \mu$: $$P_{n+1} = \left(\frac{\lambda}{\mu}\right) P_n = \rho \cdot P_n$$ Applying this recurrence successively from $P_0$: $$P_1 = \rho P_0, \quad P_2 = \rho^2 P_0, \quad P_3 = \rho^3 P_0, \quad \ldots, \quad P_n = \rho^n P_0$$ ### Derivation of $P_0$ (Normalization) Since probabilities must sum to 1: $$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n = 1$$ The geometric series converges to $\dfrac{1}{1-\rho}$ only if $\rho < 1$: $$P_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_0 = 1 - \rho$$ Therefore, the **steady-state probability** is: $$P_n = (1-\rho)\rho^n \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$ ### Derivation of L (Expected Queue Length in System) $$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot P_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n (1-\rho)\rho^n = (1-\rho) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n \rho^n$$ Using the identity $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n x^n = \dfrac{x}{(1-x)^2}$ for $0 \leq x < 1$: $$L = (1-\rho) \cdot \frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$$ ### Derivation of $L_q$ (Expected Queue Length in Waiting Line) $$L_q = L - \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda} - \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{\lambda \mu - \lambda(\mu - \lambda)}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{\lambda^2}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$$ ### Derivation of W and Wq (Using Little's Law) $$W = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} \quad ; \quad W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$$ ## 3.2 📊 Complete Worked Example (M/M/1 Model) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** At a single-window ration shop, customers arrive at a Poisson rate of **10 per hour**. The service time is **exponential with mean 5 minutes**. Find: **(a)** Traffic intensity, **(b)** $P_0$, $P_1$, $P_2$, **(c)** Average number in system and queue, **(d)** Average waiting time in system and queue, **(e)** Probability that more than 2 customers are present. **Given Data:** - $\lambda = 10$ customers/hour - Mean service time = 5 min = 1/12 hour - $\mu = 12$ customers/hour ### Solution **Step 1 — Traffic Intensity:** $$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{10}{12} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.8333$$ Since $\rho < 1$, the system is **stable** ✅. **Step 2 — Steady-State Probabilities:** $$P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - \frac{5}{6} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$ $$P_1 = (1-\rho)\rho = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{5}{6} = \frac{5}{36} \approx 0.1389$$ $$P_2 = (1-\rho)\rho^2 = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\frac{5}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{25}{216} \approx 0.1157$$ **Step 3 — Expected Number in System and Queue:** $$L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{5/6}{1/6} = 5 \text{ customers}$$ $$L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} = \frac{(5/6)^2}{1/6} = \frac{25/36}{1/6} = \frac{25}{6} \approx 4.1667 \text{ customers}$$ **Step 4 — Expected Times:** $$W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{12 - 10} = 0.5 \text{ hour} = 30 \text{ minutes}$$ $$W_q = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{5/6}{2} = \frac{5}{12} \text{ hour} \approx 25 \text{ minutes}$$ **Step 5 — Probability of more than 2 customers:** $$P(n > 2) = 1 - P_0 - P_1 - P_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{6} - \frac{5}{36} - \frac{25}{216} = \frac{216 - 36 - 30 - 25}{216} = \frac{125}{216} \approx 0.5787$$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** The shop is busy **83.3%** of the time. The average customer waits about **25 minutes in queue** before getting ration — this is a strong argument for opening a second counter. ## 3.3 Derivation of M/M/c — Waiting Probability (Erlang-C Formula) For a multi-server system, $P_0$ is derived by applying the flow-balance equations separately for $n \leq c$ and $n > c$: $$\frac{dP_n}{dt} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Flow in} = \text{Flow out}$$ For $n < c$: $\lambda P_{n-1} - (\lambda + n\mu)P_n + (n+1)\mu P_{n+1} = 0$ For $n \geq c$: $\lambda P_{n-1} - (\lambda + c\mu)P_n + c\mu P_{n+1} = 0$ Solving these and applying normalization $\sum P_n = 1$: $$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!} + \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)} \right]^{-1}, \quad \text{where } \rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu}$$ The **probability that an arriving customer must wait** (Erlang-C formula): $$P(\text{wait}) = \frac{P_0 \cdot (\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)}$$ And the expected queue length: $$L_q = P(\text{wait}) \cdot \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{P_0 \cdot (\lambda/\mu)^c \cdot \rho}{c!\,(1-\rho)^2}$$ # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Block Diagram of a Queuing System The following **functional architecture** illustrates the flow of customers through any queuing system: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Population
Finite or Infinite] -->|Arrivals at rate lambda| B[Queue
Waiting Line
FIFO LIFO Priority] B -->|Select next customer| C[Service Facility
Number of servers c
Service rate mu] C -->|After service| D[Departures
Exit Population] C -.->|Blocked if K full| E[Turned Away
Lost Customers] A -.->|Cannot join if full| E C -->|Feedback| F[Performance Metrics
L Lq W Wq Pn] B -->|Feedback| F styleA fill:FFF2CC stroke:BF9000 styleB fill:DEEBF7 stroke:2E75B6 styleC fill:E2EFDA stroke:548235 styleD fill:FCE4D6 stroke:C55A11 styleE fill:F8CBAD stroke:843C0C styleF fill:EDEDED stroke:7F7F7F ``` ## 4.2 State Transition Diagram for M/M/1 (Birth-Death Process) The following diagram shows the **state probabilities** of an M/M/1 queue, where each state *n* represents *n* customers in the system. Transitions occur at rate $\lambda$ (arrival) upward and $\mu$ (service) downward: ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 direction LR state0: STATE 0
Empty system
P0 state1: STATE 1
One customer
P1 state2: STATE 2
Two customers
P2 state3: STATE 3
Three customers
P3 state4: STATE N
N customers
Pn state0 --> state1 : arrival rate lambda state1 --> state0 : service rate mu state1 --> state2 : arrival rate lambda state2 --> state1 : service rate mu state2 --> state3 : arrival rate lambda state3 --> state2 : service rate mu state3 --> state4 : arrival rate lambda state4 --> state3 : service rate mu ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram of a Multi-Server (M/M/c) System ```mermaid flowchart TB Q[Queue
FCFS Discipline] -->|Customer 1| S1[Server 1
rate mu] Q -->|Customer 2| S2[Server 2
rate mu] Q -->|Customer c| SC[Server c
rate mu] S1 --> OUT[Departure] S2 --> OUT SC --> OUT Q -.->|If all busy| WT[Customer Waits] WT --> Q styleQ fill:DEEBF7 stroke:2E75B6 styleS1 fill:E2EFDA stroke:548235 styleS2 fill:E2EFDA stroke:548235 styleSC fill:E2EFDA stroke:548235 styleOUT fill:FCE4D6 stroke:C55A11 styleWT fill:FFF2CC stroke:BF9000 ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart for Choosing a Queuing Model ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Identify the Queuing Problem] --> S1{Inter-arrival
Time Type} S1 -->|Exponential| S2{Service Time
Type} S1 -->|Deterministic| S3[Use M/D/1 Model] S1 -->|General| S4[Use G/G/1 Approx] S2 -->|Exponential| S5{Number of
Servers} S2 -->|Deterministic| S6[Use M/D/c Model] S2 -->|General| S7[Use M/G/1 Pollaczek-Khinchine] S5 -->|One server| S8{System
Capacity} S5 -->|Multiple c| S9{System
Capacity} S8 -->|Infinite| S10[M/M/1 Model] S8 -->|Finite K| S11[M/M/1/K Model] S9 -->|Infinite| S12[M/M/c Model] S9 -->|Finite K| S13[M/M/c/K Model] ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagrams:** The arrows indicate the *direction of customer flow*. The labels $\lambda$ and $\mu$ on the state diagram represent the **transition rates** between consecutive states, and the **steady-state probability** of being in state *n* is the well-known geometric distribution. # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### ❓ Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Define Queuing Theory. List any four characteristics of a queuing system.** **Model Answer:** Queuing Theory is the mathematical study of waiting lines. It is a branch of Operations Research that uses probability and stochastic processes to model systems where customers arrive, wait for service, get served, and depart. Its goal is to optimize the trade-off between **waiting cost** and **service cost**. The four characteristics (components) of a queuing system are: 1. **Arrival pattern** (how customers arrive — Poisson, deterministic, etc.) 2. **Service pattern** (how service times are distributed) 3. **Queue discipline** (FCFS, LCFS, Priority, etc.) 4. **Number of servers (c)** and **System capacity (K)** > [!TIP] > Examiner's tip: Always mention *Kendall's notation* and the *stability condition* $\rho < 1$ in your definition for full marks. --- ### ❓ Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What do you mean by (i) Traffic Intensity and (ii) Steady-State Probability in a queuing system? Why is the steady-state condition $\rho < 1$ essential?** **Model Answer:** **(i) Traffic Intensity ($\rho$):** It is the ratio of the mean arrival rate to the mean service rate, i.e., $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ for a single-server system. It also represents the **fraction of time the server is busy**. For an *M/M/c* system, $\rho = \lambda / (c\mu)$. **(ii) Steady-State Probability ($P_n$):** It is the long-run probability that exactly *n* customers are present in the system. For an M/M/1 queue, $P_n = (1-\rho)\rho^n$. **Why $\rho < 1$ is essential:** If $\rho \geq 1$, arrivals occur at a rate equal to or faster than service. The queue length grows without bound, and the geometric series $\sum \rho^n$ does not converge, making the steady-state solution mathematically **impossible**. --- ## 5.2 📝 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### ❓ Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Customers arrive at a railway reservation counter at a **Poisson rate of 24 per hour**. The service time follows an **exponential distribution with a mean of 2 minutes**. Determine the following for steady-state: > **(i)** Probability that the system is empty ($P_0$) > **(ii)** Expected number of customers in the queue ($L_q$) > **(iii)** Expected waiting time in the system ($W$) > **(iv)** Expected number of customers in the system ($L$) > > **(b)** With reference to queuing systems, explain **Kendall's notation** and discuss the assumptions of the M/M/1 model in detail. --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question A #### **Part (a) — Numerical [7 Marks]** **Given Data:** - Arrival rate: $\lambda = 24$/hour - Mean service time = 2 min = 1/30 hour - Service rate: $\mu = 30$/hour - Model: M/M/1 (single server, Poisson arrival, exponential service) **Step 1 — Compute Traffic Intensity:** $$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{24}{30} = 0.8$$ Since $\rho < 1$, the system is **stable**. ✅ **Step 2 — (i) Probability of Empty System:** $$P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2$$ **Step 3 — (ii) Expected Number in Queue:** $$L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho} = \frac{(0.8)^2}{1 - 0.8} = \frac{0.64}{0.2} = 3.2 \text{ customers}$$ **Step 4 — (iii) Expected Waiting Time in System:** $$W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{30 - 24} = \frac{1}{6} \text{ hour} = 10 \text{ minutes}$$ **Step 5 — (iv) Expected Number in System:** $$L = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \frac{0.8}{0.2} = 4 \text{ customers}$$ > **Verification (Little's Law):** $L = \lambda W = 24 \times (1/6) = 4$ ✅ --- #### **Part (b) — Theory [7 Marks]** **Kendall's Notation:** D.G. Kendall (1953) introduced a six-symbol notation to describe any queuing system in a compact form: $$(a/b/c):(d/e/f)$$ | Position | Symbol | Meaning | Common Value | |----------|--------|---------|--------------| | 1 | a | Inter-arrival time distribution | M (Poisson) | | 2 | b | Service time distribution | M (Exponential) | | 3 | c | Number of parallel servers | 1, 2, ..., c | | 4 | d | Queue discipline | FCFS, LCFS, SIRO | | 5 | e | Maximum system capacity | $\infty$ or K | | 6 | f | Size of calling population | $\infty$ or N | For example, the standard M/M/1 queue is written as **(M/M/1):(FCFS/∞/∞)**. **Assumptions of the M/M/1 Model:** 1. **Poisson Arrivals:** Customers arrive at a constant mean rate $\lambda$ following a Poisson distribution, implying exponential inter-arrival times with mean $1/\lambda$. 2. **Exponential Service Times:** Service times are independent and exponentially distributed with mean $1/\mu$. 3. **Single Server:** There is exactly one service channel (c = 1). 4. **FCFS Discipline:** Customers are served in the order of their arrival. 5. **Infinite Capacity:** The system can hold an unlimited number of customers. 6. **Infinite Population:** The source of customers is infinite; arrival rate is independent of system state. 7. **Steady-State:** The system has reached statistical equilibrium with $\rho < 1$. --- ### ❓ Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]** > **(a)** A two-server (M/M/2) system handles **Poisson arrivals at the rate of 12 customers per hour**. Service time is **exponential with a mean of 6 minutes per customer**. Calculate: > **(i)** Traffic intensity ($\rho$) > **(ii)** Probability that the system is empty ($P_0$) > **(iii)** Expected number of customers in the queue ($L_q$) > **(iv)** Probability that a newly arriving customer has to wait. > > **(b)** A small **canteen has a single counter** where customers arrive at the rate of **20 per hour** (Poisson) and are served at the rate of **30 per hour** (exponential). The manager is considering a **new automated machine** that will double the service rate but cost **₹50 per hour** extra. Each customer's waiting cost is estimated at **₹10 per hour of waiting**. Should the manager install the machine? (Assume total cost = service cost + waiting cost.) --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question B #### **Part (a) — Numerical [7 Marks]** **Given:** M/M/2 system with $\lambda = 12$/hr, mean service time = 6 min ⇒ $\mu = 10$/hr, $c = 2$. **Step 1 — (i) Traffic Intensity:** $$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu} = \frac{12}{2 \times 10} = 0.6$$ Since $\rho < 1$, the system is **stable** ✅. **Step 2 — (ii) Probability of Empty System:** $$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!} + \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)} \right]^{-1}$$ With $c = 2$ and $\lambda/\mu = 12/10 = 1.2$: $$\sum_{n=0}^{1} \frac{(1.2)^n}{n!} = \frac{(1.2)^0}{0!} + \frac{(1.2)^1}{1!} = 1 + 1.2 = 2.2$$ $$\frac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)} = \frac{(1.2)^2}{2!\,(1-0.6)} = \frac{1.44}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{1.44}{0.8} = 1.8$$ $$P_0 = \frac{1}{2.2 + 1.8} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$ **Step 3 — (iii) Expected Number in Queue:** $$L_q = \frac{P_0 \cdot (\lambda/\mu)^c \cdot \rho}{c!\,(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{0.25 \times 1.44 \times 0.6}{2 \times (0.4)^2} = \frac{0.216}{0.32} = 0.675 \text{ customers}$$ **Step 4 — (iv) Probability of Waiting (Erlang-C):** $$P(\text{wait}) = \frac{P_0 \cdot (\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)} = \frac{0.25 \times 1.44}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{0.36}{0.8} = 0.45$$ > **Interpretation:** There is a **45% chance** that any new arrival will have to wait before getting service. --- #### **Part (b) — Cost Analysis [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Current System (M/M/1, $\lambda = 20$/hr, $\mu = 30$/hr):** $$\rho = \frac{20}{30} = 0.6667$$ $$L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho} = \frac{(0.6667)^2}{1 - 0.6667} = \frac{0.4444}{0.3333} = 1.3333 \text{ customers}$$ **Total cost of current system:** $$\text{Waiting cost} = L_q \times \text{₹10/hr} = 1.3333 \times 10 = \text{₹}13.33/\text{hr}$$ $$\text{Service cost} = \text{₹}0 \text{ (assumed base)}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Total Cost (Current)} = \text{₹}13.33/\text{hr}}$$ **Step 2 — New System (M/M/1 with $\mu$ doubled = 60/hr, $\lambda = 20$/hr):** $$\rho_{\text{new}} = \frac{20}{60} = 0.3333$$ $$L_{q,\text{new}} = \frac{(0.3333)^2}{1 - 0.3333} = \frac{0.1111}{0.6667} = 0.1667 \text{ customers}$$ **Total cost of new system:** $$\text{Waiting cost} = 0.1667 \times 10 = \text{₹}1.67/\text{hr}$$ $$\text{Service cost} = \text{₹}50/\text{hr (machine cost)}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Total Cost (New)} = 1.67 + 50 = \text{₹}51.67/\text{hr}}$$ **Step 3 — Comparison & Decision:** | System | Waiting Cost | Service Cost | Total Cost | |--------|--------------|--------------|------------| | Current (manual) | ₹13.33 | ₹0 | **₹13.33/hr** | | New (automated) | ₹1.67 | ₹50 | **₹51.67/hr** | > **Conclusion:** The new machine **should NOT be installed** because the total cost (₹51.67/hr) is far higher than the current cost (₹13.33/hr). The savings in waiting cost do not justify the additional ₹50/hr service cost. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes to Avoid:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Arrival rates in per-hour and service time in minutes — **ALWAYS convert to the same unit** before computing $\rho$. > 2. **Not checking the stability condition $\rho < 1$:** If a student forgets this, the examiner may deduct **1 full mark** for not justifying the existence of a steady-state. > 3. **Confusing $L$ and $L_q$:** $L$ is the total number **in the system** (queue + service), $L_q$ is only those **in the queue**. Mixing these is the most common error. > 4. **Wrong $P_0$ formula in M/M/c:** Many students use the M/M/1 formula. Remember that M/M/c has a *piece-wise* expression for $P_n$ with a different sum in the denominator. > 5. **Cost Analysis:** Always report both the service cost AND waiting cost. Comparing only waiting cost is incomplete. --- ## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Queuing Theory** ### 🔑 Core Definitions - ✅ **Queuing System:** Customers arriving, waiting, getting served, and leaving. - ✅ **Customer in OR context:** Generic term for any unit requiring service (humans, packets, jobs). - ✅ **Steady State:** Long-run equilibrium where probabilities are constant in time. ### 🔑 Kendall's Notation Essentials - ✅ **(M/M/1):(FCFS/∞/∞)** is the default model unless specified otherwise. - ✅ M = Markovian (Poisson/Exponential), D = Deterministic, G = General. - ✅ The first 3 symbols **(a/b/c)** are mandatory; the rest are optional. ### 🔑 Must-Memorize Formulas - ✅ M/M/1: $L = \rho/(1-\rho)$, $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho)$, $W = 1/(\mu-\lambda)$. - ✅ M/M/c: $P_0$ uses **two terms** — finite sum up to $c-1$ plus an infinite tail. - ✅ Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$ and $L_q = \lambda W_q$ — **always check** using this identity. ### 🔑 Stability & Validity - ✅ A steady-state exists **only if** $\rho < 1$ (for M/M/1) or $\rho < 1$ (for M/M/c with $\rho = \lambda/c\mu$). - ✅ If $\rho \geq 1$, the system is **unstable** and the queue grows to infinity. ### 🔑 Practical Interpretation - ✅ $\rho$ = fraction of time server is busy (e.g., $\rho = 0.8$ means 80% utilization). - ✅ $1/\mu$ = mean service time per customer. - ✅ A high $W_q$ suggests **adding more servers** (cost analysis justifies it). ### 🔑 Exam Strategy (KTU Board Pattern) - ✅ In **Part A (3 marks):** Focus on definitions, notation, and brief reasoning. - ✅ In **Part B (14 marks):** Always present formulas first, then numerical computation, then a one-line **interpretation**. - ✅ **Show all unit conversions** explicitly — the examiner specifically looks for this. - ✅ Conclude every numerical with a **Little's Law verification** to earn bonus marks. - ✅ When in doubt about the model (M/M/1 vs M/M/c), **read the problem statement carefully** for "single channel," "one server," or "multiple servers."

topics.title: Simulation and Monte
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT757 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simulation and Monte Carlo Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simulation** is the process of designing a mathematical or logical model of a real-world system and conducting experiments on this model (typically using a computer) to understand the behavior of the system, evaluate alternative strategies, or optimize system performance over simulated time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monte Carlo Method** is a broad class of computational algorithms that rely on repeated random sampling to obtain numerical estimates of deterministic quantities, using the law of large numbers to ensure convergence of stochastic estimates to true values. In optimization, it is used to evaluate complex integrals, explore large solution spaces, and handle stochastic/probabilistic objective functions where classical deterministic methods fail.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a civil engineer trying to estimate the **probability of flooding** in a city over the next 50 years. The flood depends on many uncertain factors — rainfall, river flow, soil saturation, dam release timings. You cannot solve a single deterministic equation that captures all this randomness.

**The Monte Carlo Intuition — The "Throwing Darts" Analogy:**

> [!NOTE]
> Think of estimating the area of an irregular pond. You cannot integrate its shape directly. So you throw **100,000 darts** randomly onto a rectangular field that contains the pond. You count how many land inside the pond versus the total. The ratio (times the rectangle's area) gives you the pond's area. As you throw more darts, your answer becomes more accurate.

In engineering terms: instead of solving one giant complex equation, you **simulate thousands of possible scenarios**, each driven by random inputs drawn from known probability distributions, and average the results. This is the heart of Monte Carlo.

### 1.3 Why Simulation & Monte Carlo in Optimization?

Many real engineering optimization problems are:
- **Stochastic** (involve random variables, e.g., demand, failure rates, weather).
- **Too complex for closed-form solutions** (multi-modal, non-differentiable, discrete).
- **High-dimensional** (many variables make grid-search infeasible).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For deterministic and stochastic systems where analytical optimization fails, **Simulation-Based Optimization** and **Monte Carlo Sampling** provide flexible, scalable alternatives. The course treats Monte Carlo as a numerical integration/optimization tool rooted in probability theory.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Monte Carlo

- **Expected Value Estimator:** $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i$
- **Standard Error:** $SE = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$
- **Confidence Interval (95%):** $\bar{X} \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** $MSE = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}$
- **Number of iterations for ε-accuracy:** $N = \left(\dfrac{1.96 \cdot \sigma}{\epsilon}\right)^2$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Simulation

A simulation experiment follows this structured logic:

- **Step 1 — System Definition:** Identify the boundary, components, and interactions of the real system.
- **Step 2 — Model Formulation:** Translate the system into mathematical/logical relationships (deterministic + stochastic).
- **Step 3 — Data Collection:** Gather probability distributions of random inputs (uniform, exponential, normal, Poisson, etc.).
- **Step 4 — Random Number Generation (RNG):** Produce Uniform(0,1) numbers using pseudo-random generators (LCG, Mersenne Twister).
- **Step 5 — Random Variate Generation:** Transform U(0,1) into samples from desired distributions (Inverse Transform, Acceptance-Rejection, Composition).
- **Step 6 — Experiment Execution:** Run the simulation for N replications, advancing simulated time.
- **Step 7 — Statistical Analysis:** Compute mean, variance, confidence intervals, perform hypothesis testing.
- **Step 8 — Validation & Verification:** Ensure the model behaves as the real system (validation) and is implemented correctly (verification).

### 2.2 Theoretical Foundation of Monte Carlo

**The Core Idea — Numerical Integration via Sampling:**

Suppose we need to evaluate the integral:

$$I = \int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx$$

Monte Carlo estimates this as:

$$\hat{I} = (b-a) \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} f(X_i), \quad X_i \sim U(a,b)$$

By the **Law of Large Numbers**, $\hat{I} \to I$ as $N \to \infty$.

**Why does this work?**
- The expected value of $f(X_i)$ under uniform distribution is $E[f(X)] = \dfrac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$.
- So the sample mean $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum f(X_i)$ is an unbiased estimator of $E[f(X)]$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **must-memorize cheat sheet** for KTU exams. Cover all rows.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Sample Mean | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i$ | Unbiased estimator of $E[X]$ |
| 2 | Sample Variance | $S^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ | Bessel's correction |
| 3 | Standard Error | $SE = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Quantifies estimator precision |
| 4 | 95% Confidence Interval | $\bar{X} \pm 1.96 \cdot SE$ | Asymptotic normal approximation |
| 5 | Monte Carlo Integration | $\hat{I} = (b-a)\cdot\dfrac{1}{N}\sum f(X_i)$ | $X_i \sim U(a,b)$ |
| 6 | Required sample size | $N = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\cdot \sigma}{\epsilon}\right)^2$ | For $\epsilon$ accuracy |
| 7 | Inverse Transform Method | $X = F^{-1}(U)$, $U \sim U(0,1)$ | $F$ = target CDF |
| 8 | Mean of exponential samples | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum X_i$ | For rate $\lambda$: $E[X]=1/\lambda$ |
| 9 | Expected cost estimator | $E[C] = \sum_{i} c_i \cdot P_i$ | Discrete probability |
| 10 | Convergence rate (MC) | $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ | Independent of dimension! |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Simulating traffic flow, hydrological flood estimation, structural reliability under random loads.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Tolerance stack-up analysis, thermal/heat-transfer stochastic modeling, fatigue life prediction.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Communication channel noise simulation, semiconductor yield analysis, power system reliability.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Queue modeling in manufacturing, inventory simulation, supply chain stochastic optimization.
- **Computer Science/AI:** Reinforcement learning policy evaluation, Bayesian inference, hyperparameter tuning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Unbiasedness of Monte Carlo Estimator

**Goal:** Prove that the sample mean $\bar{X}$ is an unbiased estimator of the true mean $\mu = E[X]$.

**Given:** Independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) samples $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_N$ with $E[X_i] = \mu$ and $Var(X_i) = \sigma^2$.

**Step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\bar{X}] &= E\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} E[X_i] &&\text{(Linearity of Expectation)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu &&\text{(Each } X_i \text{ has mean } \mu\text{)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{N} \cdot N \cdot \mu \\[4pt]
&= \mu
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, $E[\bar{X}] = \mu$, so $\bar{X}$ is an **unbiased estimator**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variance of the Monte Carlo Estimator

**Goal:** Find $Var(\bar{X})$ to quantify estimation precision.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Var(\bar{X}) &= Var\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{N^2}\,Var\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i\right) &&\text{(Variance of constant scaling)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} Var(X_i) &&\text{(Independence of } X_i\text{)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{N^2} \cdot N \cdot \sigma^2 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\sigma^2}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Standard error is $SE = \sqrt{Var(\bar{X})} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$. To halve the standard error, we must **quadruple** the number of samples.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Samples Required for Given Accuracy

**Goal:** Derive $N$ such that $P(|\bar{X} - \mu| \leq \epsilon) \geq 1 - \alpha$.

By the **Central Limit Theorem**, $\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \dfrac{\sigma^2}{N}\right)$ for large $N$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(|\bar{X} - \mu| \leq \epsilon) &= P\left(\left|\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{N}}\right| \leq \frac{\epsilon\sqrt{N}}{\sigma}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 2\Phi\left(\frac{\epsilon\sqrt{N}}{\sigma}\right) - 1 \geq 1 - \alpha \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \Phi\left(\frac{\epsilon\sqrt{N}}{\sigma}\right) &\geq 1 - \frac{\alpha}{2} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \frac{\epsilon\sqrt{N}}{\sigma} &\geq z_{\alpha/2} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow N &\geq \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2}\cdot \sigma}{\epsilon}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

For 95% confidence ($\alpha = 0.05$), $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, giving $N = \left(\dfrac{1.96\,\sigma}{\epsilon}\right)^2$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Monte Carlo Estimation of a Definite Integral

**Problem:** Estimate $I = \int_0^1 e^{-x^2}\, dx$ using Monte Carlo with $N = 5$ samples (hypothetical).

**Step 1:** Draw $U(0,1)$ samples. Suppose we obtain: $U = [0.12,\ 0.47,\ 0.83,\ 0.29,\ 0.66]$.

**Step 2:** Evaluate $f(x) = e^{-x^2}$ at each point.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(0.12) &= e^{-0.0144} \approx 0.9857 \\
f(0.47) &= e^{-0.2209} \approx 0.8019 \\
f(0.83) &= e^{-0.6889} \approx 0.5021 \\
f(0.29) &= e^{-0.0841} \approx 0.9193 \\
f(0.66) &= e^{-0.4356} \approx 0.6467
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute sample mean.

$$
\bar{f} = \frac{1}{5}(0.9857 + 0.8019 + 0.5021 + 0.9193 + 0.6467) = \frac{3.8557}{5} = 0.7711
$$

**Step 4:** Estimate the integral. Since the integration limits are $(0,1)$ with width $b-a = 1$:

$$
\hat{I} = (1-0) \cdot \bar{f} = 0.7711
$$

The exact value is $I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,\text{erf}(1) \approx 0.7468$. With $N=5$ the error is $\sim 3.3\%$. With $N=10^6$, the error would drop below $0.1\%$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Table — Monte Carlo vs. Deterministic Quadrature

| Property | Deterministic (Trapezoidal/Simpson) | Monte Carlo |
|----------|------------------------------------|-------------|
| Convergence rate (1-D) | $O(1/N^2)$ | $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ |
| Convergence rate (d-D) | $O(1/N^{2/d})$ (curse of dimensionality) | $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ (dimension-independent) |
| Error estimate | Easy to bound | Requires statistical estimation |
| Best suited for | Low-dimensional smooth integrands | High-dimensional, complex, stochastic integrands |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Monte Carlo Simulation — End-to-End Workflow

The diagram below captures the complete Monte Carlo pipeline as used in optimization and engineering simulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Define Problem and Objective"] --> B["Identify Random Inputs and Their Probability Distributions"]
    B --> C["Generate Uniform Random Numbers U 0 to 1 via Pseudo RNG"]
    C --> D["Apply Inverse Transform or Acceptance Rejection to Get Random Variates"]
    D --> E["Run Simulation Model for One Replication"]
    E --> F["Record Output Response of Interest"]
    F --> G{"Replications N Complete?"}
    G -->|"No"| C
    G -->|"Yes"| H["Compute Sample Mean and Sample Variance"]
    H --> I["Construct Confidence Interval for Estimator"]
    I --> J["Check Convergence within Epsilon Tolerance"]
    J -->|"Not Converged"| C
    J -->|"Converged"| K["Report Estimated Solution and Statistical Error"]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Simulation Study

This architecture maps the functional interactions between components in a typical simulation-based optimization project.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["User and Decision Maker"] --> B["Problem Specification Module"]
    B --> C["Mathematical Model Builder"]
    C --> D["Random Number Generator Engine"]
    D --> E["Random Variate Transformation Unit"]
    E --> F["Simulation Engine Core"]
    F --> G["Output Response Collector"]
    G --> H["Statistical Analyzer and Confidence Interval Builder"]
    H --> I["Optimization Decision Module"]
    I --> A
    C -->|"Validation Data"| J["Real World System"]
    J -->|"Feedback and Calibration"| C
```

---

### 4.3 Random Variate Generation Methods — Decision Tree

The following flowchart guides the choice of variate generation technique based on the probability distribution type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Target Random Variable X with known CDF F"] --> B{"Is F Invertible in Closed Form?"}
    B -->|"Yes"| C["Use Inverse Transform Method: X equals F inverse of U"]
    B -->|"No"| D{"Can you find a Simple Envelope g that Dominates f?"}
    D -->|"Yes"| E["Use Acceptance Rejection Method"]
    D -->|"No"| F{"Distribution is a Mixture of Simpler Distributions?"}
    F -->|"Yes"| G["Use Composition Method"]
    F -->|"No"| H["Use Numerical Inversion or Table Lookup"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define the Monte Carlo simulation method. List any two advantages of Monte Carlo simulation over deterministic numerical methods.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Monte Carlo simulation is a computational technique that uses repeated random sampling of inputs from specified probability distributions to obtain the statistical distribution of outputs.
