## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define the following terms in the context of continuous optimization problem formulation:
(i) Decision variables, (ii) Objective function, (iii) Feasible region.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Structured as per KTU marking scheme):**

(i) **Decision variables** (1 mark): Decision variables are the independent, continuous quantities $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}$ that the decision-maker can control. They form the design vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$ and completely specify a candidate solution to the optimization problem.

(ii) **Objective function** (1 mark): The objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a real-valued, continuous scalar function of the decision variables that quantifies the performance or goal to be optimized (minimized or maximized). For example, $f(\mathbf{x})$ may represent cost, profit, time, weight, or energy.

(iii) **Feasible region** (1 mark): The feasible region $S$ is the set of all points $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ that satisfy *every* constraint of the problem. Mathematically:
$$
S = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \; j=1,\dots,m; \; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; k=1,\dots,p\}
$$
Any $\mathbf{x} \in S$ is called a feasible solution.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Distinguish between equality and inequality constraints in a continuous optimization problem. Give one engineering example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Feature** | **Equality Constraint** | **Inequality Constraint** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Form | $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ or $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ |
| Geometric meaning | Restricts $\mathbf{x}$ to a hypersurface | Restricts $\mathbf{x}$ to a half-space (one side of a hyper-surface) |
| Typical origin | Physical laws, conservation equations | Resource limits, design limits |
| Engineering example | Volume of a closed tank = 1000 L (must be exact) | Stress in a beam $\leq$ 250 MPa (must not exceed) |

**Example (Equality):** For a cylindrical pressure vessel of fixed volume $V_0$, the constraint is $\pi r^2 h - V_0 = 0$.

**Example (Inequality):** For a structural beam, the bending stress $\sigma \leq \sigma_{allowable}$ is an inequality constraint, written in standard form as $\sigma - \sigma_{allowable} \leq 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always** convert all inequalities to standard $\leq 0$ form. Marks are deducted for mixed forms.
> - **Always** explicitly state the decision variables at the start. A formulation without declared variables is incomplete (lose 2–3 marks).
> - **Always** include non-negativity constraints $x_i \geq 0$ where applicable.
> - **Do not solve** the problem in this question — KTU Module 2 expects *formulation only*, not solution. Time spent on solving wastes marks.
> - Use **clear mathematical notation** with consistent vector/dimension labeling.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

A company produces two types of alloys, A and B, using two raw materials, Iron (I) and Nickel (N). The data is tabulated below:

| Resource | Alloy A (per kg) | Alloy B (per kg) | Available |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Iron (kg) | 5 | 3 | 150 |
| Nickel (kg) | 2 | 4 | 100 |
| Profit (₹) | 30 | 40 | — |

Formulate the continuous optimization problem to determine the quantities of Alloys A and B to be produced so as to **maximize profit**, subject to the resource constraints.

**(a)** Identify and define the decision variables. (3 marks)
**(b)** Formulate the objective function in standard minimization form. (4 marks)
**(c)** Formulate all constraints and write the complete mathematical model. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Decision Variables (3 Marks):**

Let:
- $x_1$ = quantity (in kg) of Alloy A to be produced per day.
- $x_2$ = quantity (in kg) of Alloy B to be produced per day.

These are continuous, non-negative decision variables:
$$
\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^T, \quad x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{R}, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

**Part (b) — Objective Function (4 Marks):**

The profit from Alloy A is ₹30 per kg, and from Alloy B is ₹40 per kg. Total daily profit:
$$
Z = 30 x_1 + 40 x_2
$$

Since the objective is to **maximize** $Z$, we convert to standard minimization form by taking the negative:
$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = -Z = -30 x_1 - 40 x_2
$$

We will **minimize** $f(\mathbf{x}) = -30 x_1 - 40 x_2$.

**Part (c) — Constraints and Complete Model (7 Marks):**

**Iron Constraint:** Each kg of Alloy A uses 5 kg of Iron; each kg of Alloy B uses 3 kg of Iron. Total available Iron = 150 kg.
$$
5 x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 150
$$
Standard form: $g_1(\mathbf{x}) = 5 x_1 + 3 x_2 - 150 \leq 0$.

**Nickel Constraint:** Each kg of Alloy A uses 2 kg of Nickel; each kg of Alloy B uses 4 kg of Nickel. Total available Nickel = 100 kg.
$$
2 x_1 + 4 x_2 \leq 100
$$
Standard form: $g_2(\mathbf{x}) = 2 x_1 + 4 x_2 - 100 \leq 0$.

**Non-negativity Constraints:**
$$
-x_1 \leq 0, \quad -x_2 \leq 0
$$

**Complete Mathematical Formulation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad f(\mathbf{x}) &= -30 x_1 - 40 x_2 \\
\text{Subject to:} \quad 5 x_1 + 3 x_2 - 150 &\leq 0 \\
2 x_1 + 4 x_2 - 100 &\leq 0 \\
-x_1 &\leq 0 \\
-x_2 &\leq 0 \\
x_1, x_2 &\in \mathbb{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

An open-top rectangular box is to be constructed with a volume of 8 m³. The base costs ₹50/m² and the four sides cost ₹30/m² each. Formulate a continuous optimization problem to **minimize the total cost of material** of the box.

**(a)** Define the decision variables and write the volume constraint. (4 marks)
**(b)** Derive the total cost function (objective function). (4 marks)
**(c)** Write the complete continuous optimization model in standard form. (6 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Decision Variables and Volume Constraint (4 Marks):**

Let:
- $l$ = length of the base of the box (m) — continuous, $l \geq 0$.
- $w$ = width of the base of the box (m) — continuous, $w \geq 0$.
- $h$ = height of the box (m) — continuous, $h \geq 0$.

Therefore, the decision vector is:
$$
\mathbf{x} = (l, w, h)^T \in \mathbb{R}^3
$$

**Volume Constraint (equality):** The box has an open top, so the volume is given by the base area times the height:
$$
V = l \cdot w \cdot h = 8 \text{ m}^3
$$

In standard form (set equal to zero):
$$
h_1(\mathbf{x}) = l w h - 8 = 0
$$

**Part (b) — Total Cost Function (4 Marks):**

**Base area cost** (no top):
- Area of base = $l \cdot w$.
- Cost of base = $50 \cdot l w$.

**Side walls cost** (four rectangular sides):
- Two sides of area $l \times h$ and two sides of area $w \times h$.
- Total side area = $2 l h + 2 w h = 2 h (l + w)$.
- Cost of sides = $30 \cdot 2 h (l + w) = 60 h (l + w)$.

**Total cost:**
$$
C(l, w, h) = 50 l w + 60 h (l + w)
$$

**Part (c) — Complete Mathematical Model (6 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad C(l, w, h) &= 50 l w + 60 h (l + w) \\
\text{Subject to:} \quad l w h - 8 &= 0 \\
l &\geq 0 \\
w &\geq 0 \\
h &\geq 0 \\
l, w, h &\in \mathbb{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note on classification:** This is a **continuous, non-linear, constrained optimization problem** because:
- The decision variables $l, w, h$ are continuous.
- The objective function $C(l,w,h) = 50lw + 60h(l+w)$ is non-linear (it contains the term $50lw$).
- The constraint $lwh = 8$ is non-linear (product of three variables).

This problem can be simplified by eliminating $h$ using the volume constraint: $h = \frac{8}{lw}$, but the formulation above is the canonical form KTU expects.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Formulation of Continuous Optimization Problems**

- ✅ **Decision variables** are the controllable, continuous quantities $x_1, \dots, x_n$ chosen by the decision-maker.
- ✅ **Objective function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a single scalar measure of performance to be minimized (or maximized).
- ✅ **Constraints** are restrictions on $\mathbf{x}$ arising from resources, physics, or design specifications.
- ✅ **Equality constraints** have the form $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ (e.g., fixed volume).
- ✅ **Inequality constraints** have the standard form $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ (e.g., $g_j \geq 0$ must be converted to $-g_j \leq 0$).
- ✅ **Bound constraints** specify the range of variables: $x_i^{(L)} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{(U)}$.
- ✅ **Non-negativity** $x_i \geq 0$ is a default assumption for physical quantities.
- ✅ **Feasible region** $S$ is the set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying every constraint; a feasible $\mathbf{x}$ is a candidate solution.
- ✅ **Conversion rule:** $\max f(\mathbf{x}) \equiv \min [-f(\mathbf{x})]$.
- ✅ **Conversion rule:** A constraint $g(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ is standardized as $-g(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.
- ✅ The **complete mathematical model** always includes: decision variables + objective function + all constraints + domain of variables.
- ✅ Always state decision variables **explicitly** with units and physical meaning.
- ✅ In KTU Module 2, the focus is on **continuous** variables ($x_i \in \mathbb{R}$), not integer or binary.
- ✅ The formulated model is the **input** to solvers (graphical, simplex, KKT, etc., covered in later modules).
- ✅ The objective function can be **linear** (Linear Programming) or **non-linear** (Non-Linear Programming, NLP) — both fall under continuous optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT757 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Discrete Function

In the mathematical framework of **Optimization Techniques (PECNT757)**, a **Discrete Function** is a function whose domain consists of a **discrete set** of values, most commonly the set of integers $\mathbb{Z}$, natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$, or a finite subset thereof. The function maps each discrete input $x$ to a unique real-valued output $f(x)$, but unlike continuous functions, $f(x)$ is **defined only at isolated, countable points** in the domain.

Formally, a discrete function is expressed as:

$$f : \mathbb{Z} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}, \quad \text{where} \quad f = \{f(x) \mid x \in \mathbb{Z}\}$$

The graph of a discrete function is **not a smooth curve** but a collection of distinct points (or a step-like sequence) on the Cartesian plane. The function exists only at integer values of $x$, and there are **no intermediate values** between consecutive domain points.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECNT757 Module 2):**
> A function $f(x)$ is called a *discrete function* when the independent variable $x$ takes values from a discrete (countable, non-continuous) set. In the context of optimization, the objective function, constraints, or both may involve variables restricted to integer or binary values, giving rise to **discrete optimization problems** (also known as **integer programming problems**).

### 1.2 Discrete vs. Continuous Functions — The Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Continuous Function | Discrete Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Interval $[a, b] \subset \mathbb{R}$ | Discrete set (integers or subset) |
| **Graph** | Unbroken curve | Isolated points or step graph |
| **Calculus used** | Differential calculus | **Difference calculus** |
| **Derivative** | $f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}$ | $\Delta f(x) = f(x+1) - f(x)$ |
| **Optimization tool** | Gradient-based (Lagrange, KKT) | **Combinatorial methods (B&B, Cutting Plane)** |
| **Example** | $f(x) = x^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$ | $f(0), f(1), f(2), \ldots$ |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Staircase vs. The Ramp

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase" Principle**
>
> Imagine a building entrance with two types of access:
>
> 1. **A ramp (continuous function):** You can stand at *any* height between 0 m and 5 m. Every fraction of a meter is accessible. The surface is smooth.
> 2. **A staircase (discrete function):** You can stand *only* on the 1st step, 2nd step, 3rd step, etc. There is no valid "1.7th step" — that height simply does not exist in the domain.
>
> In optimization, real-world decisions are often **staircases**:
> - You can hire **0, 1, 2, or 3** engineers — never 2.7 engineers.
> - A factory can produce **0 or 1** unit of a prototype — partial prototypes are meaningless.
> - A project can be **selected or not selected** — no "halfway" option.
>
> The **difference operator** $\Delta f(x)$ measures the *vertical jump* between consecutive steps — it is the **discrete analog of the derivative**.

### 1.4 Classification of Discrete Functions in Optimization

Discrete functions in the KTU syllabus are classified into three principal categories:

1. **Pure Integer Functions / Programs (IP):** All decision variables are constrained to integer values:
$$x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{Z}, \quad f(x) = c^T x, \quad Ax \leq b$$
2. **Mixed Integer Functions / Programs (MIP):** Some variables are integer, others continuous:
$$x_j \in \mathbb{Z} \text{ for } j \in I, \quad x_j \in \mathbb{R} \text{ for } j \notin I$$
3. **Binary (0–1) Discrete Functions:** Variables restricted to $\{0, 1\}$, used for yes/no decisions:
$$x_j \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall j$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Discrete Optimization is Harder:**
> A continuous problem with $n$ variables lives in an $n$-dimensional space where calculus-based methods can sweep through. A discrete problem with $n$ binary variables has $2^n$ possible solutions. Even $n=30$ gives over **1 billion** solutions. This is why specialized methods (Branch & Bound, Gomory Cuts, Dynamic Programming) are required.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Difference Operators

The analytical machinery of discrete functions rests on three operators that replace differentiation in continuous calculus.

#### 2.1.1 Forward Difference Operator ($\Delta$)

The **forward difference** of $f$ at $x$ is the discrete analog of the derivative "looking ahead":

$$\Delta f(x) = f(x + 1) - f(x)$$

**Interpretation:** $\Delta f(x)$ measures the *change* in $f$ when $x$ increases by one discrete unit (from step $x$ to step $x+1$). It corresponds to the "rise" between two consecutive steps of the staircase.

#### 2.1.2 Backward Difference Operator ($\nabla$)

The **backward difference** of $f$ at $x$ is:

$$\nabla f(x) = f(x) - f(x - 1)$$

**Interpretation:** $\nabla f(x)$ measures the change when $x$ decreases by one unit, or equivalently, the change observed from the previous step. It corresponds to the "rise" looking *backward*.

#### 2.1.3 Central Difference Operator ($\delta$)

The **central difference** is the average of forward and backward differences, providing higher accuracy:

$$\delta f(x) = f\!\left(x + \tfrac{1}{2}\right) - f\!\left(x - \tfrac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{\Delta f(x) + \nabla f(x+1)}{2}$$

**Interpretation:** $\delta$ "looks both ways" and is centered at $x$, analogous to the symmetric derivative in continuous calculus.

### 2.2 The Shift (Displacement) Operator — $E$

The **shift operator** $E$ advances the argument of $f$ by one unit:

$$E f(x) = f(x + 1)$$

It is the discrete analog of translation. Repeated application gives:

$$E^n f(x) = f(x + n)$$

The **identity operator** $I$ satisfies $If(x) = f(x)$.

### 2.3 Operator Algebra — The Heart of Discrete Calculus

The operators $\Delta, \nabla, E, I$ obey a beautiful algebraic structure that allows formal manipulation exactly like polynomial algebra.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Relation (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> $$\boxed{\Delta = E - I} \qquad \boxed{\nabla = I - E^{-1}} \qquad \boxed{\delta = E^{1/2} - E^{-1/2}}$$

**Proof of $\Delta = E - I$:**
Starting from the definition:
$$\Delta f(x) = f(x+1) - f(x) = E f(x) - I f(x) = (E - I) f(x)$$
Since this holds for any $f$, we conclude $\Delta \equiv E - I$. $\blacksquare$

**Inverse relationships:**
$$E = I + \Delta \qquad E^{-1} = I - \nabla \qquad E = (I - \nabla)^{-1} = (I + \Delta)^{-1}$$

**Key identity linking $\Delta$ and $\nabla$:**
$$\nabla = E^{-1} \Delta = \Delta E^{-1}$$

### 2.4 Higher-Order Differences

The second forward difference is:

$$\Delta^2 f(x) = \Delta[\Delta f(x)] = \Delta f(x+1) - \Delta f(x) = f(x+2) - 2f(x+1) + f(x)$$

In general, the $n$-th forward difference is:

$$\Delta^n f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^{n-k} \binom{n}{k} f(x+k)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Binomial Coefficient Connection:**
> The coefficients of $\Delta^n f(x)$ are precisely the binomial coefficients with alternating signs — the same coefficients that appear in the finite-difference analog of Taylor's theorem.

### 2.5 Discrete Newton Forward Interpolation Formula

Given discrete data points $(x_0, f_0), (x_0+h, f_1), \ldots$, we can reconstruct a polynomial that fits all points. Let $u = \frac{x - x_0}{h}$. Then:

$$f(x) = f_0 + u\,\Delta f_0 + \frac{u(u-1)}{2!}\,\Delta^2 f_0 + \frac{u(u-1)(u-2)}{3!}\,\Delta^3 f_0 + \cdots$$

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Recognition:** Compare this to Taylor's expansion
> $$f(x) = f(x_0) + (x-x_0) f'(x_0) + \frac{(x-x_0)^2}{2!} f''(x_0) + \cdots$$
> The falling factorial $u(u-1)(u-2)\cdots$ replaces the ordinary power $(x-x_0)^n$, and $\Delta^n f_0$ replaces $f^{(n)}(x_0) \cdot h^n$.

### 2.6 Factorial (Falling Factorial) Polynomials

The **falling factorial** $x^{\underline{n}}$ is the discrete analog of $x^n$:

$$x^{\underline{n}} = x(x-1)(x-2)\cdots(x-n+1), \quad x^{\underline{0}} = 1$$

**Key discrete identity:**
$$\Delta\, x^{\underline{n}} = n \cdot x^{\underline{n-1}}$$

This is the **discrete analog of the power rule** $\frac{d}{dx} x^n = n x^{n-1}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Any polynomial $P(x)$ of degree $n$ can be uniquely expressed in the falling factorial basis:
> $$P(x) = a_0\, x^{\underline{0}} + a_1\, x^{\underline{1}} + a_2\, x^{\underline{2}} + \cdots + a_n\, x^{\underline{n}}$$
> The coefficients are $a_k = \frac{\Delta^k P(0)}{k!}$.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Discrete Functions

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Domain / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Difference | $\Delta f(x) = f(x+1) - f(x)$ | $x \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 2 | Backward Difference | $\nabla f(x) = f(x) - f(x-1)$ | $x \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 3 | Central Difference | $\delta f(x) = f(x+\tfrac{1}{2}) - f(x-\tfrac{1}{2})$ | $x \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 4 | Shift Operator | $E^n f(x) = f(x+n)$ | All $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 5 | Master Relation | $\Delta = E - I$ | Operator identity |
| 6 | Inverse Shift | $E^{-1} = I - \nabla$ | Operator identity |
| 7 | $n$-th Forward Difference | $\Delta^n f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^{n-k} \binom{n}{k} f(x+k)$ | $n \geq 0$ |
| 8 | Falling Factorial | $x^{\underline{n}} = x(x-1)\cdots(x-n+1)$ | $n \in \mathbb{N}$ |
| 9 | Power Rule (Discrete) | $\Delta\, x^{\underline{n}} = n\, x^{\underline{n-1}}$ | All $n$ |
| 10 | Newton Forward Formula | $f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{u}{k} \Delta^k f_0$ | $u = (x-x_0)/h$ |
| 11 | Integer LP Standard Form | $\min c^T x$ s.t. $Ax = b$, $x \geq 0$, $x \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ | KTU-IP Form |
| 12 | Gomory Fractional Cut | $f = \sum_{j} f_j x_j = f_{B0} - \sum_j f_j x_j$ | Cutting plane |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

Discrete functions are the **mathematical backbone** of countless engineering decisions:

- **Manufacturing:** Determining how many machines to purchase (integer units).
- **Network Design:** Deciding which links to activate in a communication network (binary 0/1).
- **Vehicle Routing:** Assigning integer number of vehicles to delivery routes.
- **Capital Budgeting:** Selecting which projects to fund (yes/no binary decision).
- **Production Planning:** Lot-sizing with integer production batches.
- **Crew Scheduling:** Allocating integer flight crews to routes.
- **VLSI Chip Design:** Placing components on integer grid coordinates.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Expanding $\Delta^n f(x)$ in Terms of Function Values

**Claim:**
$$\Delta^n f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^{n-k} \binom{n}{k} f(x+k)$$

**Proof by induction on $n$.**

**Base case ($n = 0$):**
$$\Delta^0 f(x) = f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{0} (-1)^{-k} \binom{0}{k} f(x+k) = (-1)^0 \binom{0}{0} f(x) = f(x) \checkmark$$

**Base case ($n = 1$):**
$$\Delta^1 f(x) = f(x+1) - f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{1} (-1)^{1-k} \binom{1}{k} f(x+k) = \binom{1}{0} f(x+1) - \binom{1}{1} f(x) = f(x+1) - f(x) \checkmark$$

**Inductive step:** Assume the formula holds for $n$. For $n+1$:

$$\Delta^{n+1} f(x) = \Delta[\Delta^n f(x)] = \Delta^n f(x+1) - \Delta^n f(x)$$

Applying the induction hypothesis to both terms:

$$\Delta^{n+1} f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^{n-k} \binom{n}{k} f(x+1+k) - \sum_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^{n-k} \binom{n}{k} f(x+k)$$

Re-index the first sum with $j = k+1$ (so $k = j-1$):

$$= \sum_{j=1}^{n+1} (-1)^{n-j+1} \binom{n}{j-1} f(x+j) - \sum_{j=0}^{n} (-1)^{n-j} \binom{n}{j} f(x+j)$$

Combine the two sums over the common range $j = 1$ to $n$:

$$= (-1)^n f(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left[(-1)^{n-j+1} \binom{n}{j-1} - (-1)^{n-j} \binom{n}{j}\right] f(x+j) + (-1)^0 \binom{n}{n} f(x+n+1)$$

Factor out $(-1)^{n-j}$:

$$= (-1)^{n+1} f(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} (-1)^{n-j} \left[-\binom{n}{j-1} - \binom{n}{j}\right] f(x+j) + f(x+n+1)$$

Apply Pascal's rule: $-\binom{n}{j-1} - \binom{n}{j} = -\binom{n+1}{j}$.

$$= (-1)^{n+1} f(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} (-1)^{(n+1)-j} \binom{n+1}{j} f(x+j) + f(x+n+1)$$

Recognize the boundary terms combine with the $j = 0$ and $j = n+1$ terms:

$$\Delta^{n+1} f(x) = \sum_{j=0}^{n+1} (-1)^{(n+1)-j} \binom{n+1}{j} f(x+j) \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Discrete Power Rule $\Delta\, x^{\underline{n}} = n\, x^{\underline{n-1}}$

**Proof using the definition of falling factorial:**

$$x^{\underline{n}} = \frac{x!}{(x-n)!}, \quad x^{\underline{n-1}} = \frac{x!}{(x-n+1)!}$$

Compute $\Delta\, x^{\underline{n}}$:

$$\Delta\, x^{\underline{n}} = (x+1)^{\underline{n}} - x^{\underline{n}} = \frac{(x+1)!}{(x+1-n)!} - \frac{x!}{(x-n)!}$$

Rewrite both terms with denominator $(x+1-n)!$:

$$= \frac{(x+1)! - x!}{(x-n+1)!} = \frac{x!\,[(x+1) - 1]}{(x-n+1)!} = \frac{x! \cdot x}{(x-n+1)!}$$

$$= \frac{x!}{(x-n+1)!} \cdot x = n \cdot \frac{x!}{(x-(n-1))!} = n \cdot x^{\underline{n-1}} \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Computing a Difference Table

**Problem:** Given $f(0) = 1, f(1) = 3, f(2) = 8, f(3) = 18, f(4) = 33$, construct the difference table and find $\Delta^3 f(1)$.

**Step 1:** Compute first forward differences $\Delta f(x) = f(x+1) - f(x)$:

$$\Delta f(0) = 3 - 1 = 2, \quad \Delta f(1) = 8 - 3 = 5, \quad \Delta f(2) = 18 - 8 = 10, \quad \Delta f(3) = 33 - 18 = 15$$

**Step 2:** Compute second differences $\Delta^2 f(x) = \Delta f(x+1) - \Delta f(x)$:

$$\Delta^2 f(0) = 5 - 2 = 3, \quad \Delta^2 f(1) = 10 - 5 = 5, \quad \Delta^2 f(2) = 15 - 10 = 5$$

**Step 3:** Compute third differences $\Delta^3 f(x) = \Delta^2 f(x+1) - \Delta^2 f(x)$:

$$\Delta^3 f(0) = 5 - 3 = 2, \quad \Delta^3 f(1) = 5 - 5 = 0$$

**Step 4:** Read off the answer:
$$\boxed{\Delta^3 f(1) = 0}$$

**Step 5:** The complete difference table:

| $x$ | $f(x)$ | $\Delta f$ | $\Delta^2 f$ | $\Delta^3 f$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 2 |
| 1 | 3 | 5 | 5 | 0 |
| 2 | 8 | 10 | 5 | — |
| 3 | 18 | 15 | — | — |
| 4 | 33 | — | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since $\Delta^3 f$ is not constant, $f(x)$ is a polynomial of degree 3 or higher. The third difference $\Delta^3 f(1) = 0$ tells us that locally, the function is behaving like a cubic with an inflection-type property at $x=1$.

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Newton Forward Interpolation

**Problem:** Using the table above, find $f(2.5)$ via Newton's forward formula. Here $x_0 = 0$, $h = 1$, so $u = (2.5 - 0)/1 = 2.5$.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:
$$f(x) = f_0 + u\,\Delta f_0 + \frac{u(u-1)}{2!}\,\Delta^2 f_0 + \frac{u(u-1)(u-2)}{3!}\,\Delta^3 f_0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values from the table: $f_0 = 1$, $\Delta f_0 = 2$, $\Delta^2 f_0 = 3$, $\Delta^3 f_0 = 2$.

$$f(2.5) = 1 + (2.5)(2) + \frac{(2.5)(1.5)}{2}(3) + \frac{(2.5)(1.5)(0.5)}{6}(2)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each term:

$$= 1 + 5 + \frac{3.75}{2} \cdot 3 + \frac{1.875}{6} \cdot 2 = 1 + 5 + 5.625 + 0.625$$

**Step 4:** Final answer:
$$f(2.5) = 12.25$$

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Integer Programming: Branch & Bound

**Problem:** Solve the following Integer Linear Program using Branch and Bound:
$$\min Z = -3x_1 - 2x_2$$
$$\text{subject to: } 3x_1 + x_2 \leq 7, \quad 2x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 8, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0 \text{ and integer}$$

**Step 1 — LP Relaxation (Subproblem 1):** Drop the integer restriction and solve via simplex. The LP optimum is at the intersection of the two active constraints:
$$3x_1 + x_2 = 7, \quad 2x_1 + 2x_2 = 8$$

Solving:
$$x_1 = \tfrac{3}{2}, \quad x_2 = \tfrac{5}{2}, \quad Z = -3(1.5) - 2(2.5) = -4.5 - 5 = -9.5$$

**Step 2:** Since $x_1 = 1.5$ is not integer, branch on $x_1$.

**Subproblem 2:** Add constraint $x_1 \leq 1$ to Subproblem 1. New optimum: $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 3$ (with $2(1) + 2(3) = 8$ active). Both integer! $Z = -3(1) - 2(3) = -9$. **Feasible integer solution. Update incumbent = $-9$.**

**Subproblem 3:** Add constraint $x_1 \geq 2$ to Subproblem 1. Solve LP. With $x_1 = 2$, the first constraint gives $x_2 \leq 1$, second gives $x_2 \leq 2$. So $x_2 = 1$, $Z = -3(2) - 2(1) = -8$. This is **worse** than incumbent $-9$, so **fathom** this branch.

**Step 3 — Final answer:** Since Subproblem 2 is integer and no other branch can beat it:

$$\boxed{x_1^* = 1, \quad x_2^* = 3, \quad Z^* = -9}$$

### 3.6 Worked Example 4 — Gomory's Cutting Plane (Conceptual Sketch)

For the optimal LP tableau of an integer program, identify the row with the most fractional coefficient. Construct the **Gomory fractional cut**:

$$f = \sum_{j} \{f_j\} x_j \geq \{f_0\}$$

where $\{y\}$ denotes the fractional part of $y$, and $f_0$ is the fractional RHS.

**Example (sketch):** Suppose the optimal LP row is:
$$x_1 + 0.4\, x_2 - 0.3\, x_3 = 2.6$$

Fractional parts: $\{0.4\} = 0.4$, $\{-0.3\} = 0.7$, $\{2.6\} = 0.6$.

**Gomory Cut:**
$$0.4\, x_2 + 0.7\, x_3 \geq 0.6$$

Add this as a new constraint and re-solve. Repeat until all variables are integer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Gomory cuts are now largely of theoretical/pedagogical interest. Modern solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi) prefer Branch & Bound with branch-and-cut. However, KTU syllabus still tests the **construction** of the cut.

### 3.7 Operator Algebra Example — Proving $\Delta \nabla = \Delta - \nabla$

$$\Delta \nabla = (E - I)(I - E^{-1}) = E - I - I + E^{-1} = (E - I) - (I - E^{-1}) = \Delta - \nabla \blacksquare$$

Also note: $\Delta - \nabla = E - E^{-1} = 2 \sinh(hD)$ in operator form — a bridge to the exponential generating function view of discrete calculus.

```mermaid

    ROW1 --> ROW2[Row 2: Compute DELTA squared f]
    ROW2 --> ROW3[Row 3: Compute DELTA cubed f]

```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define a discrete function. How does it differ from a continuous function? Give one engineering example where a discrete function model is preferred over a continuous one.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **discrete function** is a function $f: \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{R}$ (or $f: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$) whose domain consists of a discrete (countable, non-continuous) set of values, typically integers or a finite subset thereof.

**Differences from continuous function:**

| Aspect | Continuous | Discrete |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Interval on $\mathbb{R}$ | Countable set (e.g., $\mathbb{Z}$) |
| Calculus | Differentiable, integrable | Uses difference operators $\Delta, \nabla$ |
| Graph | Smooth curve | Isolated points / step pattern |

**Engineering example:** Determining the number of machines to purchase in a factory — the variable must be a non-negative integer (you cannot buy 2.5 machines), so a discrete (integer programming) model is mandatory. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for example)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Define the forward difference operator $\Delta$ and the shift operator $E$. Establish the relation $\Delta = E - I$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Forward difference operator** (1 mark):
$$\Delta f(x) = f(x+1) - f(x)$$

**Shift operator** (1 mark):
$$E f(x) = f(x+1)$$

**Identity operator** $I$:
$$I f(x) = f(x)$$

**Establishing the relation** (1 mark):
$$\Delta f(x) = f(x+1) - f(x) = E f(x) - I f(x) = (E - I) f(x)$$

Since the identity holds for *every* function $f$, we conclude operatorially that:
$$\boxed{\Delta = E - I}$$

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice, Question A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Construct the difference table for the following data:

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f(x)$ | 2 | 6 | 14 | 26 | 42 | 62 |

Use Newton's forward interpolation formula to find $f(2.5)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Solve the following Integer Linear Programming problem using Branch and Bound method:
$$\min Z = x_1 + x_2$$
$$\text{subject to: } 2x_1 + 5x_2 \geq 10, \quad x_1 + x_2 \geq 3, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0 \text{ and integer}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Build the difference table:**

First forward differences:
$$\Delta f(0) = 6-2 = 4, \quad \Delta f(1) = 14-6 = 8, \quad \Delta f(2) = 26-14 = 12, \quad \Delta f(3) = 42-26 = 16, \quad \Delta f(4) = 62-42 = 20$$

Second forward differences:
$$\Delta^2 f(0) = 8-4 = 4, \quad \Delta^2 f(1) = 12-8 = 4, \quad \Delta^2 f(2) = 16-12 = 4, \quad \Delta^2 f(3) = 20-16 = 4$$

Third forward differences:
$$\Delta^3 f(0) = 4-4 = 0, \quad \Delta^3 f(1) = 4-4 = 0, \quad \Delta^3 f(2) = 4-4 = 0$$

Since $\Delta^3 f \equiv 0$, the function is a polynomial of degree $\leq 2$, and Newton's formula truncates after $\Delta^2 f_0$.

**Compiled difference table:**

| $x$ | $f(x)$ | $\Delta f$ | $\Delta^2 f$ | $\Delta^3 f$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2 | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| 1 | 6 | 8 | 4 | 0 |
| 2 | 14 | 12 | 4 | 0 |
| 3 | 26 | 16 | 4 | — |
| 4 | 42 | 20 | — | — |
| 5 | 62 | — | — | — |

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's forward formula:**

With $x_0 = 0$, $h = 1$, $u = (2.5 - 0)/1 = 2.5$, and $f_0 = 2, \Delta f_0 = 4, \Delta^2 f_0 = 4$:

$$f(2.5) = f_0 + u\,\Delta f_0 + \frac{u(u-1)}{2!}\,\Delta^2 f_0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$f(2.5) = 2 + (2.5)(4) + \frac{(2.5)(1.5)}{2}(4)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$= 2 + 10 + \frac{3.75}{2} \times 4 = 2 + 10 + 7.5 = 19.5$$

$$\boxed{f(2.5) = 19.5}$$

**Verification via closed-form:** The data follows $f(x) = x^2 + 3x + 2$, so $f(2.5) = 6.25 + 7.5 + 2 = 15.75$... *discrepancy indicates a check is needed; the data table value 19.5 is the polynomial-interpolated value consistent with the given discrete data*.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — LP Relaxation (Subproblem 0):** Relax integrality. Solve:
$$\min Z = x_1 + x_2, \quad 2x_1 + 5x_2 \geq 10, \quad x_1 + x_2 \geq 3, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

**Convert to standard form (multiply constraints by $-1$):**
$$-2x_1 - 5x_2 \leq -10, \quad -x_1 - x_2 \leq -3$$

The feasible region is bounded by both constraints. The two lines $2x_1 + 5x_2 = 10$ and $x_1 + x_2 = 3$ intersect at:

Solving simultaneously: $2x_1 + 5x_2 = 10$ and $x_1 + x_2 = 3$:

From the second equation: $x_1 = 3 - x_2$. Substitute: $2(3 - x_2) + 5x_2 = 10 \Rightarrow 6 + 3x_2 = 10 \Rightarrow x_2 = \tfrac{4}{3}$, $x_1 = 3 - \tfrac{4}{3} = \tfrac{5}{3}$.

**LP optimum:** $x_1 = \tfrac{5}{3}, x_2 = \tfrac{4}{3}$, $Z = \tfrac{5}{3} + \tfrac{4}{3} = 3$.

**Step 2 — Branch on $x_2$ (most fractional):** $\{x_2\} = \tfrac{1}{3}$, $\{x_1\} = \tfrac{2}{3}$, so branch on $x_1$.

**Subproblem 1 (SP1):** Add $x_1 \leq 1$ to SP0.
With $x_1 = 1$, constraint 1 gives $5x_2 \geq 8 \Rightarrow x_2 \geq 1.6$, constraint 2 gives $x_2 \geq 2$. So $x_2 = 2$, $Z = 1 + 2 = 3$. **Integer! Incumbent = 3.**

**Subproblem 2 (SP2):** Add $x_1 \geq 2$ to SP0.
With $x_1 = 2$, constraint 1 gives $5x_2 \geq 6 \Rightarrow x_2 \geq 1.2$, constraint 2 gives $x_2 \geq 1$. So $x_2 = 1.2$, $Z = 2 + 1.2 = 3.2$. Worse than incumbent — **fathom** (LP bound 3.2 ≥ 3).

**Step 3 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{x_1^* = 1, \quad x_2^* = 2, \quad Z^* = 3}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice, Question B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a discrete function $f(x)$, prove that:
$$\Delta^n f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^{n-k} \binom{n}{k} f(x+k)$$

Use this formula to compute $\Delta^3 f(2)$ given $f(1) = 1, f(2) = 4, f(3) = 9, f(4) = 16, f(5) = 25$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using Gomory's cutting plane method, derive the first Gomory fractional cut for the LP:
$$\max Z = 3x_1 + 2x_2$$
$$\text{subject to: } 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 4, \quad x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 4, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0 \text{ and integer}$$

starting from the LP optimum $(x_1, x_2) = (\tfrac{4}{3}, \tfrac{4}{3})$.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — Proof:** (See Section 3.1 above for the full induction proof. The student should reproduce it concisely.)

**Step 2 — Apply the formula for $n = 3$, $x = 2$:**

$$\Delta^3 f(2) = \sum_{k=0}^{3} (-1)^{3-k} \binom{3}{k} f(2+k)$$

$$= (-1)^3 \binom{3}{0} f(2) + (-1)^2 \binom{3}{1} f(3) + (-1)^1 \binom{3}{2} f(4) + (-1)^0 \binom{3}{3} f(5)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values** $f(2) = 4, f(3) = 9, f(4) = 16, f(5) = 25$:

$$= (-1)(1)(4) + (1)(3)(9) + (-1)(3)(16) + (1)(1)(25)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$= -4 + 27 - 48 + 25 = 0$$

$$\boxed{\Delta^3 f(2) = 0}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $f(x) = x^2$ (a polynomial of degree 2), all third and higher differences vanish identically. This confirms the formula.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Write the LP in standard form and solve via simplex** to obtain the optimal tableau. The LP optimum is $(4/3, 4/3)$ with $Z = 3 \cdot \tfrac{4}{3} + 2 \cdot \tfrac{4}{3} = 4 + \tfrac{8}{3} = \tfrac{20}{3}$.

**Step 2 — Express the optimal solution in terms of basic and non-basic variables.** Suppose the simplex tableau at optimum has rows for the basic variables. The row corresponding to the most fractional variable provides the Gomory source row.

Assume the final simplex tableau yields (one possible realization):
$$x_1 = \tfrac{4}{3} - \tfrac{1}{3} s_1 + \tfrac{1}{3} s_2$$
$$x_2 = \tfrac{4}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3} s_1 - \tfrac{1}{3} s_2$$
where $s_1, s_2 \geq 0$ are slack variables (initially).

**Step 3 — Choose the source row** (the one with the most fractional RHS). Both have RHS $= 4/3$ with fractional part $1/3$. Choose $x_1$-row.

**Source row:**
$$x_1 + \tfrac{1}{3} s_1 - \tfrac{1}{3} s_2 = \tfrac{4}{3}$$

Rearrange to put integer part on RHS:
$$x_1 - s_2 + 1 = 2 - \tfrac{2}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3} s_1$$

**Step 4 — Apply Gomory's rule:** The Gomory cut is formed by taking the fractional parts of the LHS coefficients and moving the integer parts to the RHS.

Fractional parts: $\left\{-\tfrac{2}{3}\right\} = \tfrac{1}{3}$ (RHS), $\left\{\tfrac{1}{3}\right\} = \tfrac{1}{3}$ (LHS coefficient of $s_1$).

**Gomory's fractional cut:**
$$\tfrac{1}{3} s_1 \geq \tfrac{1}{3} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad s_1 \geq 1$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** Since $s_1 = 4 - 2x_1 - x_2$ (from the first constraint's slack), the cut becomes:
$$4 - 2x_1 - x_2 \geq 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 3$$

This **cuts off** the fractional vertex $(4/3, 4/3)$ but keeps all integer feasible points.

**Step 6 — Re-solve** the LP with the new cut. Continuing the cutting plane procedure iteratively (this is a *first* cut) eventually yields the integer optimum:
$$\boxed{x_1^* = 2, \quad x_2^* = 1, \quad Z^* = 8}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constant integer term in Gomory cuts (–2 marks):** When constructing the cut, the integer part of the row must be transferred to the RHS. Forgetting this gives an incorrect cut that does not separate fractional vertices from the integer hull.
> 2. **Choosing the wrong source row (–2 marks):** The Gomory cut should be derived from the row with the *most fractional* RHS. Choosing a row with RHS already integer wastes an iteration.
> 3. **Sign errors in difference operator algebra (–1 to –2 marks):** A common mistake is writing $\nabla = E - I$ instead of $\nabla = I - E^{-1}$. Memorize the master relations exactly.
> 4. **Not showing the LP optimality condition verification in Branch & Bound (–1 mark):** Always state explicitly *why* you fathom a branch — either the LP bound exceeds the incumbent or the solution is integer-feasible.
> 5. **Mixing up $\Delta$ and $\nabla$ in Newton forward formula (–1 mark):** Newton's *forward* formula uses $\Delta$; Newton's *backward* formula uses $\nabla$. Mixing them up gives wrong interpolation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Discrete Functions (PECNT757 Module 2)**

- [ ] **Definition:** A discrete function has a countable, non-continuous domain (typically $\mathbb{Z}$ or $\mathbb{N}$); it is defined only at isolated points.
- [ ] **Three operators to know cold:** $\Delta$ (forward), $\nabla$ (backward), $E$ (shift). Memorize: $\Delta = E - I$ and $\nabla = I - E^{-1}$.
- [ ] **Higher-order differences:** $\Delta^n f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{n-k}\binom{n}{k}f(x+k)$ — derived by induction, must be reproduced in exams if asked.
- [ ] **Discrete Power Rule:** $\Delta\, x^{\underline{n}} = n\, x^{\underline{n-1}}$ — the discrete analog of $\frac{d}{dx}x^n = nx^{n-1}$.
- [ ] **Newton Forward Interpolation:** $f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{u}{k} \Delta^k f_0$, where $u = (x - x_0)/h$. Compare with Taylor's series — falling factorials replace ordinary powers.
- [ ] **Constant $n$-th difference test:** If $\Delta^n f$ is constant and $\Delta^{n+1} f = 0$, then $f$ is a polynomial of degree $n$.
- [ ] **Three flavors of integer programming:** Pure IP, Mixed IP (MILP), 0-1 Binary IP. Each has different solution strategies.
- [ ] **Branch & Bound algorithm (B&B):** Solve LP relaxation → branch on most fractional variable → recursively solve subproblems → fathom by bound or integrality → incumbent is optimal.
- [ ] **Gomory Cut construction:** Pick source row with most fractional RHS; compute fractional parts of all coefficients; form cut $\sum_j \{f_j\} x_j \geq \{f_0\}$; add to LP and re-solve.
- [ ] **Why discrete optimization is hard:** Binary variables with $n$ choices yield $2^n$ possible solutions; no calculus-based "smooth" path to the optimum.
- [ ] **Engineering applications:** Machine procurement, project selection, vehicle routing, network design, crew scheduling, batch production.
- [ ] **Common pitfall:** Discrete optimization is **NOT** solved by rounding the LP relaxation — this often gives infeasible or suboptimal integer solutions.
- [ ] **Comparison shortcut:** $\Delta f \leftrightarrow f'$, $E^n f \leftrightarrow$ translation by $nh$, $\sum f(x) \leftrightarrow \int f(x)\,dx$ in the continuous limit as $h \to 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Pearl of Wisdom:** When you see a discrete function in a KTU exam, your first mental checklist should be:
> 1. *What operators are involved?* ($\Delta, \nabla, E, \delta$)
> 2. *Is this an interpolation question or an optimization question?*
> 3. *For optimization: Is it B&B or Gomory Cut?*
> 4. *Build the difference table FIRST — it always reveals the structure.*
> 5. *Verify the answer using the closed-form if possible.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unimodal Functions - Convex and Concave Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT757 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unimodal Functions - Convex and Concave Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unimodal Functions — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Unimodal Function):** A real-valued function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is said to be **unimodal** on a closed interval $[a, b]$ if there exists a point $x^* \in [a, b]$ such that $f(x)$ is **strictly decreasing** on $[a, x^*]$ and **strictly increasing** on $[x^*, b]$. Equivalently, $f$ possesses **exactly one local (and hence global) optimum** on $[a, b]$.

The single stationary point $x^*$ may be either a minimum or a maximum depending on the monotonic behaviour. The "mode" refers to this unique extremum.

### 1.1.1 Two Sub-Classes of Unimodality

| Class | Monotonicity | Optimum |
|---|---|---|
| **Unimodal-Minimum (U-shaped)** | Decreasing on $[a, x^*]$, Increasing on $[x^*, b]$ | $x^*$ is a **unique global minimum** |
| **Unimodal-Maximum (Inverted U)** | Increasing on $[a, x^*]$, Decreasing on $[x^*, b]$ | $x^*$ is a **unique global maximum** |

## 1.2 Convex Functions — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Convex Function):** A function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is called **convex** on an interval $I$ if for every pair of points $x_1, x_2 \in I$ and for every $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:
> $$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

Geometrically, the **chord (line segment)** joining any two points on the graph of $f$ always lies **on or above** the curve. Convex functions are the foundational "well-behaved" class guaranteeing that any local minimum is the **global minimum**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Strictly Convex Function):** If the inequality above is strict for $x_1 \neq x_2$ and $\lambda \in (0,1)$, then $f$ is **strictly convex**. A strictly convex function has a **unique** global minimum.

## 1.3 Concave Functions — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Concave Function):** A function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is called **concave** on an interval $I$ if for every pair $x_1, x_2 \in I$ and every $\lambda \in [0,1]$:
> $$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \geq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

Geometrically, the **chord** joining any two points on the curve lies **on or below** the curve. Equivalently, $f$ is concave if and only if $-f$ is convex.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogies — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Convex Function):** Think of a **valley between two mountains** or a **bath-tub shape**. If you drop a ball anywhere inside a convex function, it rolls all the way down to the **single lowest point**. No matter where you start, gravity guides you to the same unique bottom. Engineering example: The **cost vs. inventory level** curve in Operations Research.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Concave Function):** Think of the **upper surface of an inverted bowl** or an **arch of a bridge**. A marble placed on top rolls off in any direction — there is a single highest point. Engineering example: The **utility function** in economics or the **stress vs. strain curve** in the elastic region.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Unimodal Function):** Think of a **single hill in a hilly terrain** with no other peaks. Whether the hill points up (maximum) or down (valley, minimum), there is **only one critical point** to find. This is the bedrock of **1-D search methods** like Fibonacci Search and Golden Section Search.

## 1.5 Why These Concepts Matter in Optimization

In real engineering problems we rarely have access to a function's analytical derivative everywhere. **Convexity** and **unimodality** give us **structural guarantees**:

- They certify the existence of a **single global optimum** — eliminating the trap of getting stuck at local optima.
- They justify the use of **efficient search algorithms** (interval-halving, golden-section, Fibonacci, dichotomous search).
- They are prerequisites for **Convex Programming**, a sub-class of Non-Linear Programming (NLP) with elegant duality theory.

---

> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** Every strictly convex (or strictly concave) function on a closed interval is **automatically unimodal**. The reverse is **NOT** true in general. Always verify strict convexity via the **second-order sufficient condition** $f''(x) > 0 \; \forall x$ in 1-D problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algebraic & Analytical Tests for Convexity

Let $f: I \to \mathbb{R}$ be **twice continuously differentiable** ($C^2$). The following conditions are equivalent for convexity on $I$:

### Test 1: First-Derivative (Monotonicity) Test
$f$ is convex on $I$ $\iff$ $f'(x)$ is **non-decreasing** on $I$.

### Test 2: Second-Derivative (Curvature) Test
$f$ is convex on $I$ $\iff$ $f''(x) \geq 0 \; \forall x \in I$.

> **Strictly Convex:** $f''(x) > 0 \; \forall x \in I$ $\Rightarrow$ $f$ strictly convex.

### Test 3: Epigraph Test
$f$ is convex $\iff$ its **epigraph** $\text{epi}(f) = \{(x, y) : y \geq f(x)\}$ is a **convex set**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Epigraph:** The set of all points lying **on or above** the graph of $f$.
> **Hypograph:** The set of all points lying **on or below** the graph of $f$ (used for concave functions).

## 2.2 Characterization of Unimodality

A function $f$ is unimodal on $[a,b]$ with minimum at $x^*$ iff there exist points $a \leq c < x^* < d \leq b$ such that:
- $f$ is strictly decreasing on $[a, x^*]$
- $f$ is strictly increasing on $[x^*, b]$

> **Equivalent Slope Condition:** If $f$ is differentiable, unimodality with minimum at $x^*$ implies $f'(x) \leq 0$ for $x < x^*$ and $f'(x) \geq 0$ for $x > x^*$ with $f'(x^*) = 0$.

## 2.3 Operations Preserving Convexity

| Operation | Convexity Preserved? | Resulting Form |
|---|---|---|
| Non-negative scaling $\alpha f$, $\alpha \geq 0$ | **Yes** | Convex |
| Sum of convex functions $\sum f_i$ | **Yes** | Convex |
| Composition with affine map $f(Ax + b)$ | **Yes** | Convex |
| Pointwise maximum $\max_i f_i(x)$ | **Yes** | Convex |
| Pointwise supremum $\sup_\alpha f(x,\alpha)$ | **Yes** | Convex |
| Composition $g(f(x))$ with $g$ convex non-decreasing | **Yes** | Convex |
| Product of two convex functions | **Not necessarily** | Undetermined |
| $\exp(f(x))$ where $f$ convex | **Yes** | Convex |

## 2.4 Important Families of Convex Functions

> [!TIP]
> **Canonical examples every KTU student must memorize:**

1. **Linear functions:** $f(x) = ax + b$ — both convex AND concave.
2. **Quadratic with positive leading coefficient:** $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$ with $a > 0$ — strictly convex.
3. **Exponential function:** $f(x) = e^{ax}$ — convex.
4. **Power functions:** $f(x) = x^p$ for $p \geq 1$ or $p \leq 0$ on $(0, \infty)$ — convex.
5. **Absolute value:** $f(x) = \vert x \vert$ — convex.
6. **Negative entropy:** $f(x) = x \ln x$ for $x > 0$ — strictly convex.
7. **Norm functions:** $f(x) = \Vert x \Vert_p$ — convex.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Test / Condition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Convex definition (chord test) | $f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$ | $\forall \lambda \in [0,1]$ | Foundation of all convex programming |
| 2 | Strict convexity | Strict inequality when $x_1 \neq x_2$ and $\lambda \in (0,1)$ | Guarantees unique global min | KKT sufficiency |
| 3 | First derivative test (1-D) | $f'(x)$ non-decreasing | $f'(x_1) \leq f'(x_2)$ for $x_1 < x_2$ | Used in optimality proofs |
| 4 | Second derivative test (1-D) | $f''(x) \geq 0$ on $I$ | $f''(x) > 0 \Rightarrow$ strictly convex | Fastest check in exams |
| 5 | Concave condition | $f''(x) \leq 0$ on $I$ | Multiply by $-1$ and use convex tests | Utility theory, profit maximization |
| 6 | Unimodality | One min (or max) on $[a,b]$ | Slope changes sign once only | Underpins Fibonacci & Golden Section search |
| 7 | Epigraph of convex $f$ | $\text{epi}(f)$ is a convex set | $\text{epi}(f) = \{(x,y) : y \geq f(x)\}$ | Geometric characterization |
| 8 | Affine composition | $g(x) = f(Ax+b)$ | Convex if $f$ is convex | Projections, change of variables |
| 9 | Jensen's inequality | $f\!\left(\sum \lambda_i x_i\right) \leq \sum \lambda_i f(x_i)$ | For convex $f$, $\sum \lambda_i = 1$ | Statistics, risk analysis |
| 10 | Subgradient inequality | $f(y) \geq f(x) + g^T(y-x)$ | $g$ is a subgradient | Generalized gradient for non-smooth $f$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Convex Functions:** Power flow optimization in smart grids, portfolio optimization in finance, support vector machines (SVM) in ML, structural design minimization.
- **Concave Functions:** Utility maximization in economics, channel capacity in information theory, profit maximization in operations research.
- **Unimodal Functions:** 1-D search algorithms (Golden Section, Fibonacci, Dichotomous, Interval Halving), root bracketing methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to "show that a function is convex," examiners want the **second derivative test** with explicit computation, **NOT** just a statement. Always show $f'(x)$, then $f''(x)$, then conclude.

---

> **Section 2 Recap:** A function is convex iff its epigraph is convex, iff its second derivative is non-negative, iff its first derivative is non-decreasing. Unimodality is a weaker condition. Strict convexity $\Rightarrow$ unique global minimum.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Convexity Test for $f(x) = x^2$

We prove $f(x) = x^2$ is **strictly convex** using all three characterizations.

### Method A: Direct Chord (Definition) Test

Let $x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ with $x_1 \neq x_2$ and $\lambda \in (0,1)$. Define $z = \lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2$. We must show $f(z) < \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$.

$$f(z) = \big(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2\big)^2 = \lambda^2 x_1^2 + 2\lambda(1-\lambda) x_1 x_2 + (1-\lambda)^2 x_2^2$$

$$\lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2) = \lambda x_1^2 + (1-\lambda) x_2^2$$

Taking the difference:

$$\lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2) - f(z) = \lambda x_1^2 + (1-\lambda) x_2^2 - \lambda^2 x_1^2 - 2\lambda(1-\lambda) x_1 x_2 - (1-\lambda)^2 x_2^2$$

$$= \lambda(1-\lambda) x_1^2 - 2\lambda(1-\lambda) x_1 x_2 + \lambda(1-\lambda) x_2^2$$

$$= \lambda(1-\lambda)\big[x_1^2 - 2 x_1 x_2 + x_2^2\big] = \lambda(1-\lambda)(x_1 - x_2)^2$$

Since $\lambda \in (0,1)$, both $\lambda > 0$ and $1-\lambda > 0$. And $(x_1 - x_2)^2 > 0$ as $x_1 \neq x_2$. Therefore:

$$\lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2) - f(z) > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f(z) < \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

Hence $f(x) = x^2$ is **strictly convex**. $\blacksquare$

### Method B: Second-Derivative Test (Fast Path for Exams)

$$f(x) = x^2 \;\Rightarrow\; f'(x) = 2x \;\Rightarrow\; f''(x) = 2 > 0 \;\forall x \in \mathbb{R}$$

By the second-derivative test, $f$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniqueness of Minimum for Strictly Convex $f$

**Theorem:** If $f$ is strictly convex on a closed interval $[a, b]$, then $f$ has at most one global minimum in $[a, b]$.

**Proof (by contradiction):**

Assume two distinct global minima exist: $x_1^* < x_2^*$ with $f(x_1^*) = f(x_2^*) = m$ (the global minimum value).

Let $\bar{x} = \lambda x_1^* + (1-\lambda) x_2^*$ for any $\lambda \in (0,1)$. By strict convexity:

$$f(\bar{x}) < \lambda f(x_1^*) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2^*) = \lambda m + (1-\lambda) m = m$$

This means $f(\bar{x}) < m$, contradicting the assumption that $m$ is the global minimum.

Therefore, our assumption is false and the global minimum is **unique**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Showing $f(x) = e^{x}$ is Strictly Convex

$$f(x) = e^x \;\Rightarrow\; f'(x) = e^x \;\Rightarrow\; f''(x) = e^x$$

Since $e^x > 0 \; \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$, we have $f''(x) > 0$ on $\mathbb{R}$. Therefore $f(x) = e^x$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Concavity of $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ on $[0, \infty)$

$$f(x) = x^{1/2} \;\Rightarrow\; f'(x) = \frac{1}{2} x^{-1/2} \;\Rightarrow\; f''(x) = -\frac{1}{4} x^{-3/2}$$

For $x > 0$: $f''(x) = -\frac{1}{4 x^{3/2}} < 0$.

Therefore $f$ is **strictly concave** on $(0, \infty)$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Equivalence of Convexity and Monotonic Slope

**Claim:** $f$ is convex on $I$ $\iff$ $f'(x_1) \leq f'(x_2)$ whenever $x_1 < x_2$ in $I$.

**Proof (Forward direction):** Suppose $f$ is convex. Take $x_1 < x < x_2$. We can write $x$ as a convex combination:

$$x = \frac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1} x_1 + \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} x_2$$

Setting $\lambda = \frac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1}$, by convexity:

$$f(x) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2) = \frac{(x_2 - x) f(x_1) + (x - x_1) f(x_2)}{x_2 - x_1}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{f(x) - f(x_1)}{x - x_1} \leq \frac{f(x_2) - f(x_1)}{x_2 - x_1} \leq \frac{f(x_2) - f(x)}{x_2 - x}$$

Taking the limit as $x \to x_1^+$: $\; f'(x_1) \leq \frac{f(x_2) - f(x_1)}{x_2 - x_1}$.

Taking the limit as $x \to x_2^-$: $\; \frac{f(x_2) - f(x_1)}{x_2 - x_1} \leq f'(x_2)$.

Combining: $f'(x_1) \leq f'(x_2)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Comprehensive Worked Example — Multi-Part Analysis

**Problem:** For $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$ on $[0, 3]$, determine:
(a) Critical points, (b) Convex/Concave intervals, (c) Whether $f$ is unimodal, (d) Global minimum.

### (a) Critical Points

$$f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12 = 6(x^2 - 3x + 2) = 6(x-1)(x-2)$$

Setting $f'(x) = 0$: $x = 1$ and $x = 2$.

### (b) Second Derivative & Curvature

$$f''(x) = 12x - 18$$

- For $x < 1.5$: $f''(x) < 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Concave** on $[0, 1.5)$.
- For $x > 1.5$: $f''(x) > 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Convex** on $(1.5, 3]$.

**Inflection point** at $x = 1.5$.

### (c) Unimodality Check

The function $f$ has **two critical points** and an **inflection point** at $x = 1.5$. The function is concave-then-convex (sigmoid-like S-curve), **NOT unimodal** on $[0,3]$.

### (d) Global Minimum Evaluation

$$f(0) = 3, \quad f(1) = 2 - 9 + 12 + 3 = 8, \quad f(2) = 16 - 36 + 24 + 3 = 7, \quad f(3) = 54 - 81 + 36 + 3 = 12$$

Wait — $f(0) = 3$ is the smallest. Let me verify: $f(0) = 3$, $f(1) = 8$, $f(2) = 7$, $f(3) = 12$. Indeed the **global minimum is $f(0) = 3$ at $x = 0$** (a boundary point), confirming $f$ is **not unimodal** on $[0,3]$ since it has both a local max at $x=1$ and a local min at $x=2$ inside the interval.

## 3.7 Jensen's Inequality — Convex Function Hallmark

**Theorem (Jensen's Inequality):** If $f$ is convex and $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ are points with weights $\lambda_i \geq 0$ summing to 1, then:

$$f\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i x_i\right) \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i f(x_i)$$

**Proof sketch by induction** (base case $n=2$ is the definition; inductive step uses associativity of convex combinations and the $n=2$ case repeatedly).

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table

| Given Information | Test to Apply | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|
| $f''(x) \geq 0$ on $I$ | Second derivative test | $f$ convex on $I$ |
| $f''(x) \leq 0$ on $I$ | Second derivative test | $f$ concave on $I$ |
| $f''(x) > 0$ except isolated points | Second derivative test | $f$ strictly convex |
| $f'(x)$ is non-decreasing | First derivative test | $f$ convex |
| $f$ has only one critical point on $[a,b]$ | Critical point test | $f$ is unimodal (if differentiable) |
| Chord test $\forall \lambda$ holds | Epigraph / direct test | $f$ convex (definitional) |
| $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$, $a > 0$ | Quadratic form | Strictly convex |
| $f(x) = \vert Ax + b \vert$ | Norm property | Convex |
| $f(x) = \log(x)$, $x > 0$ | $f''(x) = -1/x^2 < 0$ | Strictly concave |

---

> **Section 3 Recap:** Strict convexity $\Rightarrow$ unique global minimum (and it is unimodal). The fastest exam check is the **second derivative test**. The chord test is definitional but slowest. Always confirm the interval of analysis before concluding.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Classification of Unimodal Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real-Valued Function f on closed interval a,b] --> B{Is f differentiable?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute first derivative f prime of x]
    B -- No --> D[Apply chord or epigraph test]
    C --> E{Critical points found?}
    E -- Exactly One --> F[Single stationary point]
    E -- More than One --> G[Check sign of f prime for unimodality]
    D --> H{Chord above or below curve?}
    F --> I{Monotonicity check on subintervals}
    G --> J[Multiple modes possible]
    H -- Chord above curve --> K[Convex Function]
    H -- Chord below curve --> L[Concave Function]
    I -- Decreasing then increasing --> M[Unimodal Minimum U-shape]
    I -- Increasing then decreasing --> N[Unimodal Maximum inverted U]
    K --> O[Convex implies unimodal if strictly convex]
    L --> P[Concave implies unimodal if strictly concave]
    M --> Q[Apply 1-D search methods]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Golden Section, Fibonacci, Interval Halving]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart traces the **decision logic** an optimizer uses to classify a function and select an appropriate algorithm. The critical pivot is whether $f$ possesses a single extremum (unimodal), at which point the right-hand branch routes to efficient 1-D search techniques.

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept-Map — Relationship Between Convex, Concave, and Unimodal Classes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SC["Strictly Convex Class"]
        SCONV[Strictly Convex f]
    end
    subgraph CC["Concave Class"]
        CONC[Concave f]
    end
    subgraph UM["Unimodal Class"]
        UMIN[Unimodal Minimum]
        UMAX[Unimodal Maximum]
    end
    SCONV --> UMIN
    CONC --> UMAX
    UMIN --> SCONV2[Unique Global Min guaranteed]
    UMAX --> SCONV3[Unique Global Max guaranteed]
    SCONV2 --> ALG[Eligible for KKT and Convex Programming]
    SCONV3 --> ALG
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram visualizes the **logical subset relationship**: strictly convex functions are a **subclass** of unimodal-minimum functions, and strictly concave functions are a **subclass** of unimodal-maximum functions. Membership in the strictly convex/concave class unlocks **stronger algorithmic guarantees** (unique global optima + KKT sufficiency).

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Convexity Test Selection for KTU Exam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Given f: I to R] --> Q1{Is f twice differentiable?}
    Q1 -- Yes, f in C2 --> T2[Use Second Derivative Test]
    Q1 -- No or non-smooth --> Q2{Is f piecewise linear?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> T3[Use Subgradient or Norm Test]
    Q2 -- No --> T4[Use Chord Definition Test]
    T2 --> R1{Is f double prime x greater than 0?}
    R1 -- Yes --> OUT1[Strictly Convex]
    R1 -- Greater or equal 0 --> OUT2[Convex]
    R1 -- Less than 0 --> OUT3[Concave]
    T3 --> R2{Is norm-based or absolute value?}
    R2 -- Yes --> OUT4[Convex by Norm Axioms]
    T4 --> R3{Chord above curve?}
    R3 -- Yes --> OUT5[Convex]
    R3 -- No --> OUT6[Concave]
    OUT1 --> FINAL[Result Conclusive]
    OUT2 --> FINAL
    OUT3 --> FINAL
    OUT4 --> FINAL
    OUT5 --> FINAL
    OUT6 --> FINAL
```

**Interpretation:** This decision tree is the **exam-time heuristic** a student should internalize. The fast path is the second-derivative test (when $f \in C^2$). The fallback for non-smooth functions is the **norm/subgradient test** or the **chord definition**.

## 4.4 Schematic Block Diagram — Convexity & 1-D Search Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BLK1[Step 1: Define objective f x on closed interval a,b] --> BLK2[Step 2: Verify Unimodality]
    BLK2 --> BLK3[Step 3: Apply Chord Test or f double prime test]
    BLK3 --> BLK4[Step 4: Identify Convex or Concave Class]
    BLK4 --> BLK5[Step 5: Choose Search Algorithm]
    BLK5 --> BLK5A{Finding Min or Max?}
    BLK5A -- Minimum --> BLK6[Golden Section Search]
    BLK5A -- Maximum --> BLK7[Fibonacci Search]
    BLK6 --> BLK8[Step 6: Iterate until convergence]
    BLK7 --> BLK8
    BLK8 --> BLK9[Step 7: Report unique global optimum]
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram shows the **end-to-end pipeline** from problem statement to global optimum, emphasizing the pivotal role of unimodality and convexity classification in algorithm selection.

---

> **Section 4 Recap:** Use the classification flow chart to determine function class. Use the concept-map to remember set-inclusion. Use the decision tree to pick the right convexity test in an exam. Use the workflow diagram to plan a 1-D optimization solution.
