> **The optimal TSP tour for this matrix is** $(1\text{-}2\text{-}5\text{-}3\text{-}4\text{-}1)$ with $f = 12+2+4+6+19 = 43$? **Recheck:** actually $1\text{-}5\text{-}2\text{-}3\text{-}4\text{-}1 = 8+2+3+6+19 = 38$, while $1\text{-}3\text{-}4\text{-}2\text{-}5\text{-}1 = 10+6+7+2+8 = 33$. The search illustrates *trajectory improvement*, not guarantee of global optimum — which is the honest trade-off of all metaheuristics.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tabu Search vs Other Metaheuristics

| Property | Tabu Search | Simulated Annealing | Genetic Algorithm |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Memory** | Explicit (tabu list) | None | Population-based |
| **Escape from local optima** | Via tabu + aspiration | Via probabilistic acceptance | Via crossover/mutation |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic (given $x_0$) | Stochastic | Stochastic |
| **Parameter sensitivity** | Moderate (tenure $\theta$) | High (cooling schedule) | High (population, mutation rate) |
| **Best-use case** | Combinatorial with structure | Continuous, smooth | Black-box, parallel search |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Tabu Search Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Initialize x0, T empty, t=0"] --> B["Evaluate f(x0) and set x star to x0"]
    B --> C["Generate Neighborhood N(x)"]
    C --> D["Filter Tabu Moves using T"]
    D --> E{"Any Candidate Better than f star?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Apply Aspiration Criterion"]
    E -- "No" --> G["Select Best Admissible Move m star"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Move Current x to x next"]
    H --> I["Update x star and f star if Improved"]
    I --> J["Add Move m to Tabu List T"]
    J --> K{"List Size exceeds Tenure theta?"}
    K -- "Yes" --> L["Remove Oldest Entry FIFO"]
    K -- "No" --> M["Increment t"]
    L --> M
    M --> N{"Stopping Criterion Met?"}
    N -- "No" --> C
    N -- "Yes" --> O["Output x star and f star as Solution"]
```

### 4.2 Memory Architecture — Tabu List as a FIFO Queue

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TabuList["TABU LIST T  FIFO Queue"]
        direction LR
        E1["m at t minus theta plus 1  Oldest"] --> E2["m at t minus theta plus 2"] --> E3["m at t minus 1"] --> E4["m at t  Newly Added"]
    end
    E1 -. "Evicted after theta iterations" .-> EX["Discarded Move"]
    E4 -. "Just inserted" .-> TS["Now Forbidden for next theta iterations"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — How the Aspiration Criterion Interacts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["Candidate Move m"] --> Q1{"Is m in Tabu List T?"}
    Q1 -- "No" --> ACC["Accept m as Admissible"]
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Does f x plus m Beat Global Best f star?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> ASP["Aspiration Override  Accept m anyway"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> REJ["Reject m  Stay Tabu"]
    ACC --> SEL["Add to Candidate Pool"]
    ASP --> SEL
    REJ --> OUT["Excluded from This Iteration"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1a]**  
> **Q1a.** *Define NP-Complete problems. Why are they significant in optimization?* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
A decision problem is **NP-Complete** if (i) it belongs to class **NP** (verifiable in polynomial time) and (ii) every problem in NP is polynomial-time reducible to it (NP-Hard). They are significant because no polynomial-time algorithm is known to solve them exactly, making heuristic/metaheuristic methods like Tabu Search essential for large-scale instances. *(Word count ≈ 45)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1b]**  
> **Q1b.** *What is the role of the Tabu List in Tabu Search?* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The **Tabu List** $T$ is a short-term memory structure that stores recently applied moves as *forbidden* for a fixed number of iterations called the **tabu tenure** $\theta$. It prevents the search from **cycling** back to previously visited solutions, thereby forcing exploration of new regions and enabling the algorithm to escape **local optima** in NP-Complete problems.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q14a OR Q14b]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Full Tabu Search Derivation & TSP Application*

**Q14(a)** Explain the Basic Tabu Search algorithm in detail with its key components. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Basic Tabu Search is a metaheuristic introduced by Fred Glover (1986) for solving NP-Complete combinatorial problems. It enhances local search by introducing **memory** to escape local optima.

**Key Components:**
1. **Current solution** $x$ — the working point in solution space $X$.
2. **Neighborhood** $N(x)$ — set of solutions reachable by a single move.
3. **Move** $m$ — the transformation operator (e.g., 2-opt swap for TSP).
4. **Tabu List** $T$ — a FIFO queue of recently applied moves, length = tenure $\theta$.
5. **Aspiration Criterion** — overrides tabu status if a move yields a new global best.
6. **Stopping Rule** — typically max iterations $T_{\max}$ or convergence tolerance $\epsilon$.

**Working:** Starting from $x_0$, at each iteration, the algorithm examines all $x' \in N(x)$, filters out those whose generating move is in $T$, applies aspiration, and greedily moves to the best admissible solution. The chosen move enters $T$; the oldest entry is evicted when $|T| > \theta$. The best $x^*$ found overall is the output. *(~150 words)*

---

**Q14(b)** Apply Basic Tabu Search with tabu tenure $\theta = 2$ to the following 4-city TSP distance matrix starting from tour $1\text{-}2\text{-}3\text{-}4\text{-}1$, using 2-opt moves. Show three iterations. **[7 Marks]**

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 9 & 6 \\
5 & 0 & 7 & 3 \\
9 & 7 & 0 & 4 \\
6 & 3 & 4 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Initial:** $x_0 = (1\text{-}2\text{-}3\text{-}4\text{-}1)$  
$f(x_0) = 5 + 7 + 4 + 6 = 22$ → $x^* = x_0$, $f^* = 22$, $T = \emptyset$.

**Iteration 1 — Generate 2-opt neighbors of $x_0$:**

| Move | New Tour | $f$ |
|------|----------|-----|
| $m_1$ | 1-2-4-3-1 | $5+3+4+9 = 21$ |
| $m_2$ | 1-3-2-4-1 | $9+7+3+6 = 25$ |

Best → $m_1$, $x_1 = (1\text{-}2\text{-}4\text{-}3\text{-}1)$, $f = 21$.  
$21 < 22$ → $x^* \gets x_1$, $f^* = 21$.  
$T = \{m_1\}$.

**Iteration 2 — Neighbors of $x_1$:**

| Move | New Tour | $f$ | Tabu? |
|------|----------|-----|-------|
| $m_3$ | 1-2-3-4-1 | 22 | No |
| $m_4$ | 1-3-2-4-1 | 25 | No |

Best → $m_3$, $x_2 = (1\text{-}2\text{-}3\text{-}4\text{-}1)$, $f = 22$.  
$22 > 21$ → $x^*$ unchanged.  
$T = \{m_1, m_3\}$.

**Iteration 3 — Tenure expires for $m_1$; neighbors:**

| Move | New Tour | $f$ | Tabu? |
|------|----------|-----|-------|
| $m_5$ | 1-3-4-2-1 | $9+4+3+5 = 21$ | No |
| $m_6$ | 1-2-3-4-1 | 22 | Tabu ($m_3 \in T$) |

Best admissible → $m_5$, $f = 21$, **aspiration** since equal to $f^*$.  
$x_3 = (1\text{-}3\text{-}4\text{-}2\text{-}1)$, $f = 21$.  
$T = \{m_3, m_5\}$ (evict $m_1$).

**Result after 3 iterations:** $f^* = 21$, $x^* = (1\text{-}2\text{-}4\text{-}3\text{-}1)$ (or equivalent 1-3-4-2-1). 

> The optimal tour is $(1\text{-}2\text{-}4\text{-}3\text{-}1)$ with $f = 21$, which Tabu Search has **discovered** within 3 iterations. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q14(a)** Compare the Basic Tabu Search algorithm with Simulated Annealing in terms of memory use, exploration strategy, and convergence behavior. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Tabu Search | Simulated Annealing |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Memory** | Uses explicit *Tabu List* (short-term memory of forbidden moves) | No memory; stateless Markov chain |
| **Exploration** | Deterministic — always picks best admissible move | Stochastic — accepts worse moves with probability $e^{-\Delta f / T}$ |
| **Escape from local optima** | By forbidding revisiting recent solutions | By probabilistic uphill moves at high temperature |
| **Parameter sensitivity** | Tabu tenure $\theta$ | Cooling schedule $T_k$ |
| **Convergence** | Empirically fast to good solutions; not provable | Asymptotic convergence to global optimum (under slow cooling) |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic given $x_0$ | Probabilistic |

Tabu Search leverages **structure** in previously visited solutions, while SA relies on **random perturbation**. TS is generally preferred for combinatorial problems (TSP, scheduling) where move definitions are clear; SA excels in continuous optimization.

---

**Q14(b)** Define the following terms with one-line examples: (i) Tabu Tenure, (ii) Aspiration Criterion, (iii) Neighborhood, (iv) Move, (v) Short-term Memory, (vi) Diversification, (vii) Intensification. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (one-mark style answers):**

1. **Tabu Tenure ($\theta$):** Number of iterations a move remains forbidden after being added to the tabu list. *Example: $\theta = 7$ means the move is tabu for 7 iterations.*
2. **Aspiration Criterion:** A rule that overrides tabu status if a move produces a solution better than the current global best. *Example: Allowing a tabu move if $f(x') < f(x^*)$.*
3. **Neighborhood ($N(x)$):** Set of all solutions obtainable from $x$ by applying a single move. *Example: For TSP with 2-opt, $N(x)$ contains all tours differing by one edge swap.*
4. **Move ($m$):** An operator transforming $x$ into $x' \in N(x)$. *Example: 2-opt edge swap in TSP.*
5. **Short-term Memory:** Memory of the most recent moves/visits to prevent immediate cycling. *Example: The tabu list itself.*
6. **Diversification:** Strategy to drive the search toward *unvisited* regions of the solution space. *Example: Restarting from a random solution when search stagnates.*
7. **Intensification:** Strategy to focus the search around *high-quality* regions already discovered. *Example: Returning to the best-known solution and re-exploring its neighborhood.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Tabu" with "Taboo" as something permanently forbidden.** It is *temporary* — the tenure $\theta$ dictates how long.
> 2. **Forgetting the Aspiration Criterion.** Tabu Search without aspiration can get *trapped* ignoring the global best. Always mention it.
> 3. **Stating TS *guarantees* the global optimum.** It does NOT — it provides a *high-quality approximation*. Saying "TS finds the optimal solution" loses marks.
> 4. **Mixing up "Move" and "Solution."** A *move* is the operator; a *solution* is the result. Tabu List stores *moves*, not solutions.
> 5. **Skipping the update of the Tabu List** in numerical problems — this is the most common procedural error in KTU answer sheets.
> 6. **Forgetting FIFO eviction** — when $|T| = \theta$, the oldest move must be removed; otherwise, the list grows unboundedly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: NP-Complete & Tabu Search**

- **NP-Complete** = NP ∩ NP-Hard; verified in polynomial time; no known polynomial-time exact algorithm.
- **Examples:** TSP, Knapsack, Graph Coloring, SAT, Hamiltonian Cycle, Job-Shop Scheduling.
- **Tabu Search** = Metaheuristic by **Fred Glover (1986)** using *memory-based* local search escape.
- **Five Pillars:** Current solution, Neighborhood, Move, Tabu List, Aspiration Criterion.
- **Tabu List $T$:** FIFO queue of forbidden moves; length = **Tabu Tenure $\theta$**.
- **Aspiration Criterion:** Override tabu status if $f(x') < f(x^*)$ (global best improvement).
- **Tabu Search does NOT guarantee global optimum** — it gives a high-quality *approximation*.
- **Parameter to tune:** Tabu Tenure $\theta$ (small = frequent cycling; large = restricted search).
- **Stopping Criteria:** Max iterations $T_{\max}$, runtime limit, or convergence $\vert f(x_t) - f^* \vert < \epsilon$.
- **vs Simulated Annealing:** TS uses *deterministic memory*; SA uses *probabilistic acceptance* with cooling.
- **vs Genetic Algorithm:** TS works on *one solution* improving it; GA works on a *population* evolving it.
- **Engineering Applications:** Routing, scheduling, VLSI design, feature selection, portfolio optimization.
- **Basic TS limitation:** Pure short-term memory may still get stuck — solved by Intermediate (Frequency) and Long-term (Diversification) memory in advanced TS.
- **Algorithm steps (memorize in order):** Initialize → Generate Neighbors → Filter Tabu → Apply Aspiration → Select Best Move → Update $x^*$ → Update $T$ → Check Stop.
- **Move types for TSP:** 2-opt (edge swap), 3-opt (3 edges), or-opt (relocation), inversion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neighborhood
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT757 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Neighborhood # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Neighborhood (N(s)):** In combinatorial optimization and metaheuristic search, the **neighborhood** of a candidate solution `s ∈ S` (where `S` is the solution space) is formally defined as the set of all feasible solutions `s' ∈ S` that can be obtained from `s` by applying exactly one elementary transformation called a **move** (or **neighborhood operator/structure**). Mathematically, the neighborhood is expressed as: $$N(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid s' = m(s), \; m \in M \}$$ Where: - `S` = the discrete (finite or countably infinite) search/solution space - `M` = the set of permissible **move operators** (also called the neighborhood structure or `N-structure`) - `m(s)` = the application of a single move `m` to solution `s` - A solution `s* ∈ N(s)` is called a **neighbor** of `s` > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** The **neighborhood structure** `N` is the *function/rule* defining which solutions are adjacent, while `N(s)` is the *concrete set* of neighbors of solution `s`. KTU examiners frequently test this precise difference. ## 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are standing in the middle of a **chessboard**. Your current position is your current solution `s`. The neighborhood `N(s)` consists of **all squares a knight can legally jump to in one move**. Each such square is a *neighbor*, and the *rule* "knight's L-shaped move" is the *neighborhood structure*. A local search algorithm would evaluate all these neighboring squares and pick the best one to step into, mimicking how a hill-climbing optimizer explores the landscape. In **practical terms**: - If `s` is a delivery truck's current route visiting 10 cities, its neighborhood may include all routes obtained by **swapping any two cities** in the route. - If `s` is a Sudoku configuration, its neighborhood may include all configurations differing by **one digit placement**. ## 1.3 Why Neighborhoods Matter in Optimization Neighborhoods are the **engine of local search** and all trajectory-based metaheuristics. Without a well-designed neighborhood, an optimizer cannot: 1. **Explore** the solution space iteratively. 2. **Transition** from a current solution to a better one. 3. **Escape** local optima (when paired with metaheuristic acceptance rules like Simulated Annealing or Tabu Search). The **size** and **structure** of a neighborhood directly determine the **trade-off between solution quality and computational effort**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric — Neighborhood Size:** The cardinality `|N(s)|` is the most critical performance parameter. For an `n`-city TSP with **2-opt** moves, the standard size is **n(n−1)/2** and for **swap** moves it is **n(n−1)/2** as well, while for **insertion** it is **(n−1)(n−2)**. These are high-yield KTU facts. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Components of a Neighborhood A complete neighborhood design in optimization comprises **four interacting components**, each of which KTU questions may isolate: 1. **Current Solution `s`** — the incumbent candidate solution under evaluation. 2. **Move Operator `m ∈ M`** — the atomic transformation (e.g., swap, insert, invert, 2-opt). 3. **Feasibility Filter `F`** — ensures the generated neighbor is valid in `S` (e.g., respects capacity constraints in VRP). 4. **Evaluation Function `f(s')`** — computes the objective cost, used to rank neighbors. ## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' of Neighborhood Operations ### Why define a neighborhood at all? Because in **combinatorial/discrete optimization**, the search space `S` is non-continuous (no calculus-based derivatives). The neighborhood **imposes a discrete topology** on `S`, enabling a gradient-like traversal using only **local information**. ### How is a move applied? A move `m` acts on the encoding (representation) of `s` and produces `s'` such that `s'` differs from `s` in a **minimal, well-defined way**. This minimality is what makes the neighborhood *local*. ## 2.3 Taxonomy of Common Neighborhood Structures > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield for KTU:** Memorize the move types, their resulting neighbor counts, and their typical use-cases. ### A. **Swap (Transposition) Neighborhood** - **Operation:** Exchange the positions of two elements. - **Use-case:** Scheduling, assignment problems. - **Size:** `n(n-1)/2` for an `n`-element solution. ### B. **Insertion (Shift) Neighborhood** - **Operation:** Remove an element and re-insert it at a different position. - **Use-case:** TSP, vehicle routing. - **Size:** `(n-1)(n-2)`. ### C. **2-opt Neighborhood** - **Operation:** Reverse a sub-sequence (segment) of the solution. - **Use-case:** TSP, sequence ordering. - **Size:** `n(n-1)/2`. ### D. **Inversion (Block Reverse) Neighborhood** - **Operation:** Reverse the order of a contiguous block. - **Size:** `n(n-1)/2`. ### E. **Or-opt Neighborhood** - **Operation:** Move a chain of 1, 2, or 3 consecutive cities to another position. - **Use-case:** TSP refinement. ### F. **k-opt Neighborhood** - **Operation:** Replace up to `k` edges in a tour. - **Size:** Grows combinatorially: `O(n^k)`. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Symbol / Notation | Meaning | KTU Highlight | |---|---|---| | `N(s)` | Set of neighbors of solution `s` | Core definition | | `\vert N(s) \vert` | Cardinality (size) of the neighborhood | Determines search effort | | `S` | Full discrete solution space | The universe of solutions | | `M` | Set of all move operators | Defines the structure | | `m(s)` | Resulting solution after applying move `m` to `s` | Atomic transition | | `f(s)` | Objective (cost) function value at `s` | Ranking metric | | `s*` | Local optimum in `N(s)` | Termination signal | | `d(s, s')` | Distance metric between `s` and `s'` | Used in adaptive neighborhoods | | Swap size | `n(n-1)/2` | Universal for 2-element swap | | 2-opt size | `n(n-1)/2` | Universal for 2-opt | | Insert size | `(n-1)(n-2)` | For sequence repositioning | | k-opt complexity | `O(n^k)` | Expensive for large `k` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Table Rule:** Note the use of `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) instead of the pipe character `|` to prevent breaking the markdown table renderer. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Logistics & Supply Chain:** `2-opt` and `Or-opt` neighborhoods drive UPS, FedEx, and Amazon route optimization. - **VLSI Chip Design:** Neighborhoods over cell placements minimize wire length. - **Telecommunications:** Frequency assignment uses swap neighborhoods. - **Manufacturing Scheduling:** Job-shop scheduling uses insertion neighborhoods to minimize makespan. - **Bio-informatics:** DNA sequence alignment uses edit-distance neighborhoods. ## 2.6 Properties of a 'Good' Neighborhood A well-designed neighborhood should ideally satisfy: 1. **Reachability/Connectivity:** For every pair `s_a, s_b ∈ S`, there must exist a finite path of moves from `s_a` to `s_b`. Otherwise, the search is trapped in a disconnected region. 2. **Bounded Size:** `\vert N(s) \vert` should be polynomial in `n` to keep enumeration tractable. 3. **Local Improvement Exists:** At least one `s' ∈ N(s)` should often be better than `s`, or the search stalls. 4. **Efficient Evaluation:** The cost difference `Δf = f(s') − f(s)` should be computable in `O(1)` (incremental evaluation), not `O(n)`. > [!NOTE] > **Incremental Evaluation (Δ-evaluation):** KTU examiners love asking about this. For TSP with 2-opt, swapping edges `(i, i+1)` and `(j, j+1)` with `(i, j)` and `(i+1, j+1)` gives a cost delta computable in **O(1)** instead of O(n) recomputation. This is a *key efficiency trick*. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cardinality of the Swap Neighborhood **Problem:** Given a permutation solution of `n` distinct elements, how many unique pairwise swaps exist? **Step 1 — Set up the counting:** A swap exchanges 2 elements chosen from `n`. The number of unordered pairs is given by the binomial coefficient. $$|N_{\text{swap}}(s)| = \binom{n}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Expand the binomial:** $$|N_{\text{swap}}(s)| = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2!(n-2)!}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify:** $$|N_{\text{swap}}(s)| = \frac{n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2)!}{2 \cdot (n-2)!}$$ **Step 4 — Cancel `(n-2)!`:** $$|N_{\text{swap}}(s)| = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{|N_{\text{swap}}(s)| = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}}$$ > **Logic:** We divide by 2 because swapping element `i` with element `j` produces the *same* neighbor as swapping `j` with `i`. Order does not matter. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cardinality of the Insertion Neighborhood **Problem:** How many unique insertion moves exist for a permutation of `n` elements? **Step 1 — Frame the move:** To insert an element, we must: - Choose **1 element to remove** from its current position: `n` choices. - Choose **1 new position** to insert it into (excluding its current position): `(n − 1)` choices. **Step 2 — Multiply the independent choices:** $$|N_{\text{insert}}(s)| = n \times (n-1)$$ **Step 3 — Correct for overcounting:** Inserting element `x` between positions `k` and `k+1` is **the same** as inserting it between `k+1` and `k+2` *only when considered as a final permutation state?* No — for permutations, these are distinct because the resulting sequence differs. Hence **no overcounting** correction is needed. **Final Result:** $$\boxed{|N_{\text{insert}}(s)| = n(n-1) = (n-1) \cdot n}$$ > **Note:** Some textbooks quote `(n-1)(n-2)` for the *single-step* insertion (after first deleting, you have `n-1` positions for the gap, minus 1 for the vacated spot = `n-2`). Both are accepted; KTU accepts `n(n-1)`. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: 2-opt Cost Delta (Incremental Evaluation) **Scenario:** A TSP tour is `T = (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n, v_1)` with Euclidean edge costs. A 2-opt move removes edges `(v_i, v_{i+1})` and `(v_j, v_{j+1})` and reconnects with `(v_i, v_j)` and `(v_{i+1}, v_{j+1})`. **Step 1 — Original cost contribution:** $$C_{\text{old}} = d(v_i, v_{i+1}) + d(v_j, v_{j+1})$$ **Step 2 — New cost contribution after 2-opt:** $$C_{\text{new}} = d(v_i, v_j) + d(v_{i+1}, v_{j+1})$$ **Step 3 — Cost delta:** $$\Delta = C_{\text{new}} - C_{\text{old}} = d(v_i, v_j) + d(v_{i+1}, v_{j+1}) - d(v_i, v_{i+1}) - d(v_j, v_{j+1})$$ **Step 4 — Acceptance rule:** The move is **improving** if: $$\Delta < 0$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\Delta_{2\text{-opt}} = d(v_i, v_j) + d(v_{i+1}, v_{j+1}) - d(v_i, v_{i+1}) - d(v_j, v_{j+1})}$$ > **Logic:** This delta is computed in **O(1)** by retrieving 4 pre-stored edge distances. This is the cornerstone of fast TSP local search. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Neighborhood Selection Trade-off | Neighborhood Type | Size (Big-O) | Best Move Selection | Risk of Local Optimum | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | Swap | `O(n^2)` | Steepest / First-Improvement | High | General permutation problems | | Insertion | `O(n^2)` | Steepest | High | TSP, sequence ordering | | 2-opt | `O(n^2)` | Or-opt (variable `k`) | Medium | TSP | | 3-opt | `O(n^3)` | LK heuristic | Lower | Large TSP | | Adaptive / Variable | `O(n^2)` to `O(n^3)` | Dynamic `k` | Lowest | Modern metaheuristics | > **Engineering Insight:** Smaller neighborhoods (`O(n^2)`) evaluate faster but get trapped more easily. Larger neighborhoods (`O(n^3)`) escape local optima better but consume more CPU per iteration. The **LKH (Lin-Kernighan)** heuristic cleverly combines `k`-opt moves of variable `k` to balance both. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Local Search via Neighborhood Exploration ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Start: Initial Solution s0] --> evalA[Compute f s0] evalA --> genA[Generate Neighbors N s0] genA --> rankA[Rank Neighbors by f] rankA --> decA{Best Neighbor
Better than s0?} decA -- Yes --> acceptA[Set s1 = best neighbor] decA -- No --> termA[Terminate: Local Optimum s*] acceptA --> evalA style startA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style termA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 style decA fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 style acceptA fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009933 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Neighborhood Architecture in Metaheuristics ```mermaid flowchart LR solA[Current Solution s] --> moveA[Apply Move Operator m] moveA --> filtA[Feasibility Filter F] filtA --> validA{Valid
Neighbor?} validA -- Yes --> neighA[Neighbor s' added to N s] validA -- No --> rejA[Discarded] neighA --> evalB[Evaluate f s'] evalB --> poolA[Neighbor Pool] poolA --> acceptA{Acceptance
Criterion} acceptA --> nextA[Next Iteration Solution] style solA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style neighA fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009933 style rejA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 style poolA fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Neighborhood Topology for an 8-City TSP ```mermaid flowchart TD cur1[Current Tour T1] --> sw1[Swap Move] cur1 --> ins1[Insertion Move] cur1 --> opt1[2-opt Move] sw1 --> n1[Neighbor T2] sw1 --> n2[Neighbor T3] sw1 --> n3[Neighbor T4] ins1 --> n4[Neighbor T5] opt1 --> n5[Neighbor T6] opt1 --> n6[Neighbor T7] n1 --> nex1[Next Iteration Candidate] n2 --> nex1 n3 --> nex1 n4 --> nex1 n5 --> nex1 n6 --> nex1 style cur1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style nex1 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009933 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The current tour `T1` is the *center* of the neighborhood. Each move type generates a distinct set of neighbors. The next-iteration candidate is the best among them (or accepted per the metaheuristic rule). ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Adaptive Neighborhood (Variable k-opt) ```mermaid flowchart TD s0[Solution s] --> ksel[k-Selector Module] ksel --> k1[Choose k=2: 2-opt] ksel --> k2[Choose k=3: 3-opt] ksel --> k3[Choose k=4: 4-opt] k1 --> n2opt[2-opt Neighbors] k2 --> n3opt[3-opt Neighbors] k3 --> n4opt[4-opt Neighbors] n2opt --> merge[Merge Candidate Pool] n3opt --> merge n4opt --> merge merge --> sel[Select Best Candidate] sel --> out[Output s'] style s0 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style sel fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 style out fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009933 ``` > **Engineering Use:** The Lin-Kernighan-Helsgott (LKH) algorithm uses this adaptive variable-`k` neighborhood to solve million-city TSPs to near-optimality in seconds. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** > **Define a neighborhood in combinatorial optimization. How does it differ from the neighborhood structure?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **neighborhood** `N(s)` of a solution `s` is the set of all solutions reachable from `s` by applying a single move operator. The **neighborhood structure** is the *rule or set of move operators* `M` that defines which solutions are considered adjacent. In other words, `N(s)` is a *concrete set*, while the neighborhood structure is the *abstract function* generating it. The size `\vert N(s) \vert` is called the **neighborhood size** and is a critical performance parameter. *(Award: 1 mark for definition of N(s), 1 mark for distinction with structure, 1 mark for mentioning size parameter.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** > **List any three common types of neighborhood move operators used in local search algorithms. State the neighborhood size for each when applied to a permutation of `n` elements.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Swap (Transposition):** Exchanges two elements. Size = `n(n-1)/2`. 2. **Insertion (Shift):** Removes one element and reinserts at a new position. Size = `n(n-1)`. 3. **2-opt:** Reverses a subsequence by removing and reconnecting two edges. Size = `n(n-1)/2`. *(Award: 1 mark per correct pair of move type and size.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) > **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice, 14 Marks]** > **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of a neighborhood in local search optimization algorithms. Discuss in detail the **swap, insertion, and 2-opt** neighborhood structures with suitable examples. **(a) Model Solution (7 marks):** A **neighborhood** in local search is the set of solutions generated by applying one elementary move to a current solution. Local search iterates by moving from `s` to a better `s' ∈ N(s)`. **1. Swap Neighborhood (2 marks):** - **Operation:** Exchange the positions of two elements. - **Example:** For the permutation `[3, 1, 4, 2]`, swapping positions 1 and 3 gives `[4, 1, 3, 2]`. - **Size:** `n(n-1)/2`. - **Use:** Assignment, scheduling. **2. Insertion Neighborhood (2 marks):** - **Operation:** Remove one element and place it at a different position. - **Example:** For `[3, 1, 4, 2]`, removing `4` and inserting after `2` gives `[3, 1, 2, 4]`. - **Size:** `n(n-1)`. - **Use:** TSP, vehicle routing. **3. 2-opt Neighborhood (3 marks):** - **Operation:** Pick two non-adjacent edges `(v_i, v_{i+1})` and `(v_j, v_{j+1})` in a tour. Remove them and reconnect as `(v_i, v_j)` and `(v_{i+1}, v_{j+1})`. This effectively **reverses the segment** between the two edges. - **Example:** Tour `1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 1`. Apply 2-opt on edges `(2,3)` and `(5,1)`: new tour `1 → 5 → 4 → 3 → 2 → 1` (segment 2→3→4→5 reversed). - **Cost delta:** `Δ = d(v_i, v_j) + d(v_{i+1}, v_{j+1}) - d(v_i, v_{i+1}) - d(v_j, v_{j+1})`. - **Size:** `n(n-1)/2`. - **Use:** TSP, sequence ordering. --- > **(b)** [7 Marks] For a **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** with 10 cities, calculate the size of the **(i) swap, (ii) insertion, and (iii) 2-opt** neighborhoods. If a local search algorithm evaluates all neighbors of the current solution, how many total tour evaluations are required per iteration? **(b) Model Solution (7 marks):** Given `n = 10` cities. **(i) Swap neighborhood size (2 marks):** $$|N_{\text{swap}}| = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45$$ **(ii) Insertion neighborhood size (2 marks):** $$|N_{\text{insert}}| = n(n-1) = 10 \times 9 = 90$$ **(iii) 2-opt neighborhood size (2 marks):** $$|N_{2\text{-opt}}| = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45$$ **Total tour evaluations per iteration (1 mark):** For a steepest-descent local search that examines all three neighborhood types: $$T_{\text{total}} = 45 + 90 + 45 = 180 \text{ evaluations per iteration}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q3):** Students frequently confuse the **insertion size** as `(n-1)(n-2)` instead of `n(n-1)`. KTU accepts both formulas if the derivation is shown. **Also:** do not forget the factor of `1/2` in swap and 2-opt sizes — overcounting is a common 1-mark loss. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) > **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice, 14 Marks]** > **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **role of neighborhoods in metaheuristic algorithms** like Simulated Annealing and Tabu Search. Explain how the choice of neighborhood size affects the balance between **exploration and exploitation**. **(a) Model Solution (7 marks):** **Role of Neighborhoods in Metaheuristics (4 marks):** In **Simulated Annealing (SA)**, the neighborhood `N(s)` defines the candidate set from which the next solution `s'` is probabilistically selected. SA may accept *worse* neighbors with probability: $$P(\text{accept}) = e^{-\Delta f / T}$$ where `Δf > 0` is the cost increase and `T` is the current temperature. A well-designed neighborhood ensures both feasible and informative transitions. In **Tabu Search (TS)**, the neighborhood `N(s)` is restricted by a **tabu list** of recently visited solutions/moves. Only **non-tabu** neighbors are considered, which forces the search to explore new regions and avoid cycling. **Exploration vs. Exploitation Trade-off (3 marks):** | Neighborhood Property | Effect on Exploitation | Effect on Exploration | |---|---|---| | **Small size** (e.g., 2-opt) | High — focuses on local improvement | Low — easy to get stuck | | **Large size** (e.g., 3-opt, 4-opt) | Lower — may skip good local moves | High — escapes local optima | | **Adaptive / Variable** | Balanced | Balanced | > **Conclusion:** The choice of `N(s)` is the most critical design decision. Modern algorithms like LKH use **adaptive variable-`k` opt** neighborhoods to dynamically balance both objectives. --- > **(b)** [7 Marks] Consider the **incremental cost evaluation** in a 2-opt move. Given a TSP with Euclidean distances, derive the cost delta `Δ` for the 2-opt move and show that it can be computed in **O(1)** time. Why is this important for large-scale TSP instances? **(b) Model Solution (7 marks):** **Derivation (5 marks):** Consider a tour `T = (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_i, v_{i+1}, \ldots, v_j, v_{j+1}, \ldots, v_n, v_1)`. A **2-opt move** replaces edges `(v_i, v_{i+1})` and `(v_j, v_{j+1})` with `(v_i, v_j)` and `(v_{i+1}, v_{j+1})`. **Original cost (2 edges):** $$C_{\text{old}} = d(v_i, v_{i+1}) + d(v_j, v_{j+1})$$ **New cost (2 edges):** $$C_{\text{new}} = d(v_i, v_j) + d(v_{i+1}, v_{j+1})$$ **Cost delta:** $$\Delta = C_{\text{new}} - C_{\text{old}} = d(v_i, v_j) + d(v_{i+1}, v_{j+1}) - d(v_i, v_{i+1}) - d(v_j, v_{j+1})$$ The move is **improving** iff `Δ < 0`. **O(1) Computation (1 mark):** Since `Δ` requires only the lookup of 4 precomputed edge distances, it is computed in constant time. No need to recompute the entire tour cost. **Importance for Large-Scale TSP (1 mark):** For `n = 1,000,000` cities, recomputing the full tour cost is `O(n)` per move — too slow for the ~`10^{11}` candidate moves. With O(1) delta evaluation, the entire search becomes **practical**, enabling modern solvers (LKH, Concorde) to handle million-city instances. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q4):** For part (a), students often describe SA/TS *without explicitly linking them to N(s)* — losing 2 marks. Always anchor your explanation to the neighborhood concept. For part (b), omitting the `O(1)` justification loses 1 mark; the derivation alone is not enough. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam** - **Definition:** `N(s)` = set of all solutions reachable by **one move** from `s`. The **neighborhood structure** is the rule defining the move set `M`. - **Three Pillars of Neighborhood Design:** (1) connectivity, (2) bounded size, (3) efficient evaluation. - **Common Moves:** Swap, Insertion, 2-opt, Inversion, Or-opt, k-opt. - **Size Formulas (memorize):** - Swap: `n(n-1)/2` - Insertion: `n(n-1)` - 2-opt: `n(n-1)/2` - k-opt: `O(n^k)` - **2-opt Cost Delta:** `Δ = d(v_i, v_j) + d(v_{i+1}, v_{j+1}) − d(v_i, v_{i+1}) − d(v_j, v_{j+1})`. - **Acceptance Rule:** Move accepted if `Δ < 0` (improving); in SA, worse moves accepted with probability `e^{-Δf/T}`. - **Incremental Evaluation:** Computing `Δ` in `O(1)` is what makes large-scale TSP tractable. - **Metaheuristic Linkage:** - **Simulated Annealing** uses neighborhoods with probabilistic acceptance. - **Tabu Search** uses neighborhoods filtered by a tabu list. - **Genetic Algorithms** use crossover/mutation as *neighborhood-like* operators on a population. - **Exploration vs. Exploitation:** Smaller `N(s)` → more exploitation; larger `N(s)` → more exploration. - **Adaptive Neighborhoods (LKH):** Variable `k` balances both automatically. - **Examiner's Traps:** Confusing `N(s)` with the structure `N`; miscounting neighborhood size; forgetting the `1/2` factor; failing to mention `O(1)` incremental evaluation. - **Real-World Domains:** Logistics, VLSI, scheduling, bioinformatics, telecommunications.

topics.title: Candidate List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT757 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Candidate List ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Candidate List (CL):** In the **Branch and Bound (B&B)** algorithm applied to Integer Linear Programming (ILP) and mixed-integer problems, the *Candidate List* (also called the *Active List* or *Live List*) is the dynamic data structure that stores all **active (pending) subproblems** — i.e., subproblems that have been generated by the *branching* operation but have not yet been *solved* or *fathomed*. At every iteration of the B&B algorithm, a subproblem is *selected* from the Candidate List, its **LP relaxation** is solved, and based on the outcome, the subproblem is either **fathomed** or **branched**, with the resulting new subproblems re-injected into the Candidate List. **Mathematical Representation:** Let $S_0$ denote the original ILP/LP Relaxation problem. The Candidate List at iteration $k$ is: $$ \mathcal{C}_k = \{ S_i \;\vert\; S_i \text{ is generated but not yet explored or fathomed at iteration } k \} $$ The algorithm terminates when $\mathcal{C}_k = \varnothing$ (empty set). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Detective's Suspect Board":** > Imagine a detective solving a complex case. They start with one big lead (the original problem). When they investigate, they discover multiple "branches" of inquiry. They write each new lead on a sticky note and pin it to a **board** (the Candidate List). At every moment, the detective picks the **most promising** sticky note, investigates it, and either: > - **Closes the case** (fathom — solution found, infeasible, or worse than the best case found so far), or > - **Splits it into 2 new sticky notes** (branching). > > The board keeps shrinking or growing until every lead is closed. The board itself **is** the Candidate List. ### 1.3 Why is the Candidate List Critical? - It enables the **divide-and-conquer** nature of B&B by remembering all open branches. - It allows **selective exploration** of the search tree (we only solve a *fraction* of all $2^n$ possible subproblems in the worst case). - The **selection rule** applied to the Candidate List dramatically affects algorithm efficiency. - It is the **bookkeeping engine** of the entire B&B procedure. ### 1.4 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | **Subproblem ($S_i$)** | A relaxed LP obtained by adding cuts to the parent problem | | **LP Relaxation** | Original problem with integrality constraints dropped | | **Bound ($\bar{Z}_i$)** | Optimal value of the LP relaxation of $S_i$ | | **Incumbent ($\bar{Z}^*$)** | Best integer-feasible solution found so far | | **Fathoming** | Removing a subproblem from further consideration | | **Branching** | Splitting $S_i$ into 2 subproblems ($S_i^{\le}$ and $S_i^{\ge}$) | | **Active Node** | Subproblem currently in the Candidate List | | **E-node** | Subproblem selected from the CL for exploration | ### 1.5 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in **bold** - The size of the Candidate List is bounded by the depth of the search tree: at most **$2^n$** nodes in the worst case, where $n$ = number of integer variables. - The number of LP solves equals the number of times a subproblem is *selected* from the CL. - The **Bounding Function** $g(S_i) = \bar{Z}_i$ (LP optimum of $S_i$) is the key metric used in selection. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Mechanics of the Candidate List The Candidate List operates in a **5-stage cyclical pipeline**: #### Stage 1 — Initialization - $\mathcal{C}_0 \leftarrow \{S_0\}$ where $S_0$ is the LP relaxation of the original ILP. - Set $\bar{Z}^* = -\infty$ (incumbent value). #### Stage 2 — Selection (Choose an E-node) - Pick $S_j \in \mathcal{C}_k$ using a **selection rule**: - **FIFO (First-In First-Out):** Oldest subproblem first. - **LIFO (Last-In First-Out):** Newest subproblem first. - **LC / Best-Bound (Most Common):** $S_j = \arg\max_{S_i \in \mathcal{C}_k} \bar{Z}_i$ (for maximization). - Remove $S_j$ from $\mathcal{C}_k$. #### Stage 3 — Bounding (Solve LP Relaxation) - Solve the LP relaxation of $S_j$ to obtain the bound $\bar{Z}_j$ and the relaxed solution $x^{(j)}$. #### Stage 4 — Fathoming Test (Three Fathoming Criteria) A subproblem $S_j$ is **fathomed** if **any one** of the following holds: 1. **Infeasibility Fathom:** The LP relaxation of $S_j$ has **no feasible region** (empty feasible set). $$\mathcal{F}(S_j) = \varnothing \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{fathom}$$ 2. **Optimality Fathom (Integer Feasible):** The LP solution $x^{(j)}$ already satisfies the integrality constraints. $$x^{(j)} \in \mathbb{Z}^n \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{update incumbent } \bar{Z}^* \leftarrow \bar{Z}_j, \text{ then fathom}$$ 3. **Bound Fathom (Bound Cutoff):** The LP bound is **worse** than the current incumbent (for max). $$\bar{Z}_j \le \bar{Z}^* \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{fathom}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on the Bound Fathom Criterion:** Since $\mathcal{C}_k$ contains only unfathomed nodes, all surviving nodes satisfy $\bar{Z}_i > \bar{Z}^*$. This is what guarantees optimality of the incumbent at termination. #### Stage 5 — Branching - If $S_j$ is *not* fathomed, identify a fractional variable $x_r^{(j)} \notin \mathbb{Z}$ and create: $$S_j^{\le}: \quad S_j \cup \{x_r \le \lfloor x_r^{(j)} \rfloor\}$$ $$S_j^{\ge}: \quad S_j \cup \{x_r \ge \lceil x_r^{(j)} \rceil\}$$ - Insert both new subproblems into the Candidate List: $\mathcal{C}_{k+1} \leftarrow \mathcal{C}_k \cup \{S_j^{\le}, S_j^{\ge}\}$. #### Termination - If $\mathcal{C}_k = \varnothing$, **STOP**. The current incumbent $\bar{Z}^*$ is the optimal integer solution. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Candidate List | $\mathcal{C}_k = \{S_i \mid S_i \text{ active at iteration } k\}$ | Dynamic set | | 2 | LP Bound (Max) | $\bar{Z}_i = \max \{c^T x \mid x \in \mathcal{F}(S_i)\}$ | Always $\ge$ integer optimum | | 3 | LP Bound (Min) | $\underline{Z}_i = \min \{c^T x \mid x \in \mathcal{F}(S_i)\}$ | Always $\le$ integer optimum | | 4 | Fathom 1 (Infeas) | $\mathcal{F}(S_j) = \varnothing$ | Empty feasible region | | 5 | Fathom 2 (Integer) | $x^{(j)} \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ | Integrality satisfied | | 6 | Fathom 3 (Bound, Max) | $\bar{Z}_j \le \bar{Z}^*$ | Worse than incumbent | | 7 | Fathom 3 (Bound, Min) | $\underline{Z}_j \ge \underline{Z}^*$ | Worse than incumbent | | 8 | Branching (fractional $x_r$) | $S_j^{\le}: x_r \le \lfloor x_r^{(j)} \rfloor$, $S_j^{\ge}: x_r \ge \lceil x_r^{(j)} \rceil$ | Round both ways | | 9 | Selection — FIFO/LIFO | Pick by insertion order | Stack/Queue discipline | | 10 | Selection — LC (Max) | $S_j = \arg\max_{S_i \in \mathcal{C}_k} \bar{Z}_i$ | Highest bound first | | 11 | Selection — LC (Min) | $S_j = \arg\min_{S_i \in \mathcal{C}_k} \underline{Z}_i$ | Lowest bound first | | 12 | Tree size upper bound | $\vert \mathcal{C}_{\max} \vert \le 2^n$ | $n$ = integer variables | | 13 | Incumbent update | $\bar{Z}^* \leftarrow \bar{Z}_j$, $x^* \leftarrow x^{(j)}$ | Only on integer fathoms | | 14 | Termination | $\mathcal{C}_k = \varnothing$ | Empty candidate list | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Candidate List strategy is foundational in solving: - **Vehicle Routing & Scheduling** (OR-Tools, logistics) - **Network Design** (router/switch placement, fiber topology) - **Capital Budgeting** (binary yes/no project selection) - **Production Planning** (number of batches must be integer) - **VLSI Chip Design** (cell placement with integer constraints) - **Crew Scheduling** in airlines and railways (integer staff assignments) **Why it matters:** Without the Candidate List, the B&B algorithm would either lose track of unexplored subproblems or have to explore them in an inefficient (and often non-terminating) order. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example with Candidate List Tracking ### 3.1 Problem Statement (Standard KTU-Style ILP) > **Solve using Branch and Bound. Track the Candidate List at every step.** > > **Maximize** $\;Z = 3x_1 + x_2$ > > **Subject to:** > - $3x_1 + 2x_2 \le 6$ > - $x_1 + 2x_2 \le 4$ > - $x_1,\, x_2 \ge 0$ and **integer** ### 3.2 Step 0 — Initialization $$\mathcal{C}_0 = \{S_0\}, \quad \bar{Z}^* = -\infty$$ ### 3.3 Step 1 — Solve $S_0$ (LP Relaxation) The LP relaxation ignores integrality. Using the binding-constraint intersection method: $$3x_1 + 2x_2 = 6 \quad \text{(Constraint 1 as equality)}$$ $$x_1 + 2x_2 = 4 \quad \text{(Constraint 2 as equality)}$$ Subtracting (1) − (2): $$(3x_1 + 2x_2) - (x_1 + 2x_2) = 6 - 4$$ $$2x_1 = 2 \;\Longrightarrow\; x_1 = 1$$ Substituting into (2): $$1 + 2x_2 = 4 \;\Longrightarrow\; 2x_2 = 3 \;\Longrightarrow\; x_2 = 1.5$$ So the LP solution is $x^{(0)} = (1,\; 1.5)$. The objective value: $$Z^{(0)} = 3(1) + 1(1.5) = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5$$ **Verdict for $S_0$:** $x_2^{(0)} = 1.5 \notin \mathbb{Z}$ — *not* integer feasible. Not fathomed. **Branch on $x_2$.** **Branching:** - $S_1:\; S_0 \cup \{x_2 \le 1\}$ - $S_2:\; S_0 \cup \{x_2 \ge 2\}$ $$\mathcal{C}_1 = \{S_1,\; S_2\}$$ ### 3.4 Step 2 — Select $S_1$ (Using LC rule) We need the bounds of $S_1$ and $S_2$ to use LC. Solve both: **Solving $S_1$ (with $x_2 \le 1$):** At $x_2 = 1$: $$3x_1 + 2(1) = 6 \;\Longrightarrow\; x_1 = 4/3$$ $$x_1 + 2(1) = 4 \;\Longrightarrow\; x_1 = 2$$ Binding: $x_1 = 4/3,\; x_2 = 1$. Bound: $$\bar{Z}_1 = 3(4/3) + 1(1) = 4 + 1 = 5$$ **Solving $S_2$ (with $x_2 \ge 2$):** At $x_2 = 2$: $$3x_1 + 2(2) = 6 \;\Longrightarrow\; x_1 = 2/3$$ $$x_1 + 2(2) = 4 \;\Longrightarrow\; x_1 = 0$$ Binding: $x_1 = 0,\; x_2 = 2$. Bound: $$\bar{Z}_2 = 3(0) + 1(2) = 2$$ **LC selection (max):** $\bar{Z}_1 = 5 > \bar{Z}_2 = 2$ ⟹ **Select $S_1$.** ### 3.5 Step 3 — Process $S_1$ (Branch on $x_1$) LP solution: $x^{(1)} = (4/3,\; 1)$. $x_1 = 4/3 \notin \mathbb{Z}$. **Branch on $x_1$.** - $S_3:\; S_1 \cup \{x_1 \le 1\}$ - $S_4:\; S_1 \cup \{x_1 \ge 2\}$ $$\mathcal{C}_2 = \{S_2,\; S_3,\; S_4\}$$ ### 3.6 Step 4 — Solve and Fathom Subproblems **Solve $S_3$ (with $x_1 \le 1,\; x_2 \le 1$):** At $x_1 = 1,\; x_2 = 1$: - Check: $3(1) + 2(1) = 5 \le 6$ ✓ - Check: $1 + 2(1) = 3 \le 4$ ✓ - $x^{(3)} = (1,\; 1) \in \mathbb{Z}^2$ ✓ Integer feasible! $\bar{Z}_3 = 3(1) + 1(1) = 4$. **Update incumbent:** $$\bar{Z}^* = 4, \quad x^* = (1,\; 1)$$ **Fathom $S_3$** (integer feasibility). **Solve $S_4$ (with $x_1 \ge 2,\; x_2 \le 1$):** At $x_1 = 2,\; x_2 = 1$: - Check: $3(2) + 2(1) = 8 > 6$ ✗ **Infeasible!** **Fathom $S_4$** (infeasibility). ### 3.7 Step 5 — Bound Test on $S_2$ Recall $\bar{Z}_2 = 2$. Compare with incumbent $\bar{Z}^* = 4$: $$\bar{Z}_2 = 2 \le 4 = \bar{Z}^* \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Fathom } S_2 \text{ (bound cutoff).}$$ ### 3.8 Termination $$\mathcal{C}_k = \varnothing \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{STOP}$$ ### 3.9 Final Answer $$\boxed{Z^* = 4, \quad x^* = (x_1,\, x_2) = (1,\, 1)}$$ ### 3.10 Complete Candidate List Evolution Table | Iteration $k$ | Selected (E-node) | Action | Candidate List $\mathcal{C}_k$ | Incumbent $\bar{Z}^*$ | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | | 0 | — | Initialize | $\{S_0\}$ | $-\infty$ | | 1 | $S_0$ | Branch ($x_2$) | $\{S_1, S_2\}$ | $-\infty$ | | 2 | $S_1$ (LC: $\bar{Z}=5$) | Branch ($x_1$) | $\{S_2, S_3, S_4\}$ | $-\infty$ | | 3a | $S_3$ | Integer — Fathom | $\{S_2, S_4\}$ | **4** | | 3b | $S_4$ | Infeasible — Fathom | $\{S_2\}$ | 4 | | 4 | $S_2$ | $\bar{Z}_2=2 \le 4$ — Fathom | $\varnothing$ | 4 | | 5 | — | **STOP** | $\varnothing$ | 4 | > **Optimal integer solution:** $Z^* = 4$ at $x^* = (1,\; 1)$. ### 3.11 Algebraic Derivation of the LP Relaxation Bound The bounding function $g(S_i) = \bar{Z}_i$ for a maximization ILP is a **relaxation** of the integer optimum $Z^*_{ILP}$: $$ Z^*_{ILP} = \max\{c^T x \mid x \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{ILP}}\} \;\le\; \max\{c^T x \mid x \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{LP}}\} = \bar{Z}_i $$ where $\mathcal{F}_{\text{ILP}} \subseteq \mathcal{F}_{\text{LP}}$ (integrality constraints are dropped). This inequality is what makes **bound-based fathoming** valid: if the *best possible* integer value in $S_i$ is $\le \bar{Z}^*$, then $S_i$ cannot improve the incumbent. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — B&B Engine with Candidate List ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Start B and B Algorithm]) --> init[Initialize CL0 = S0, Zstar = neg infinity] init --> chkempty{CLk empty?} chkempty -- Yes --> stop[STOP: Report incumbent as optimal] chkempty -- No --> sel[Select subproblem Sj from CLk using selection rule] sel --> rem[Remove Sj from CLk] rem --> solve[Solve LP relaxation of Sj to get Zj and xj] solve --> test1{LP infeasible?} test1 -- Yes --> fath1[Fathom: Infeasibility] test1 -- No --> test2{Solution integer feasible?} test2 -- Yes --> upd[Update incumbent Zstar and xstar] upd --> fath2[Fathom: Integer Feasible] test2 -- No --> test3{Bound worse than incumbent?} test3 -- Yes --> fath3[Fathom: Bound Cutoff] test3 -- No --> branch[Branch on fractional variable xr to create Sjle and Sjge] branch --> add[Add Sjle and Sjge to CLk] fath1 --> chkempty fath2 --> chkempty fath3 --> chkempty add --> chkempty stop --> endNode([End: Optimal Zstar achieved]) ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Tree Diagram — Search Tree for the Worked Example ```mermaid flowchart TD P0["S0: LP Relaxation
x = 1, 1.5
Z = 4.5"] --> P1["S1: x2 le 1
x = 4/3, 1
Z = 5"] P0 --> P2["S2: x2 ge 2
x = 0, 2
Z = 2"] P1 --> P3["S3: x1 le 1, x2 le 1
x = 1, 1
Z = 4 INTEGER"] P1 --> P4["S4: x1 ge 2, x2 le 1
INFEASIBLE"] P2 --> P5["S2 FATHOMED by bound
Z2 = 2 le Zstar = 4"] P3 --> P3F["FATHOMED: integer
Update Zstar = 4"] P4 --> P4F["FATHOMED: infeasible"] P5 --> P5F["FATHOMED: bound cutoff"] P3F --> RESULT["OPTIMAL: x star = 1, 1
Z star = 4"] P4F --> RESULT P5F --> RESULT ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Candidate List Manager ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"] ilp["Original ILP
Problem Statement"] end subgraph CoreEngine["B and B Core Engine"] lpsolver["LP Relaxation
Solver Module"] brancher["Branching
Module"] fathomer["Fathoming
Module"] selector["Selection Rule
Module: FIFO, LIFO, LC"] end subgraph DataStructures["Data Structures"] candlist[("Candidate List CLk
Active Subproblems")] incumbent[("Incumbent Solution
Z star, x star")] end subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"] optimal["Optimal Integer
Solution and Z star"] end ilp --> candlist candlist --> selector selector --> lpsolver lpsolver --> fathomer fathomer -->|Fathomed| incumbent fathomer -->|Not Fathomed| brancher brancher --> candlist incumbent --> optimal lpsolver --> incumbent ``` > **Note on Diagram:** Node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `P0`, `P1`, `candlist`) and labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting inside the quoted labels) to comply with Mermaid's parser safeguards. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define the **Candidate List** in the context of the Branch and Bound algorithm. What is its role? > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Candidate List (CL) is the data structure that maintains all **active (unexplored and unfathomed) subproblems** in a Branch and Bound algorithm. > **Role:** > 1. It serves as the *working memory* of the B&B algorithm, ensuring no generated subproblem is lost. > 2. It enables **selective exploration** of the search tree by allowing the algorithm to pick the most promising subproblem at each step (via selection rules like FIFO, LIFO, or LC). > 3. The B&B algorithm terminates precisely when the Candidate List becomes **empty**, guaranteeing that no better integer solution exists. > *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for role.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State and explain the **three fathoming criteria** used in Branch and Bound. Why is fathoming essential for the algorithm's efficiency? > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A subproblem $S_j$ is fathomed (removed from the Candidate List) if **any one** holds: > 1. **Infeasibility Fathom:** The LP relaxation of $S_j$ has no feasible region ($\mathcal{F}(S_j) = \varnothing$). > 2. **Integer Feasibility Fathom:** The LP solution $x^{(j)}$ already satisfies all integrality constraints. The incumbent $\bar{Z}^*$ is updated. > 3. **Bound Fathom (Cutoff):** The LP bound $\bar{Z}_j$ is worse than the current incumbent ($\bar{Z}_j \le \bar{Z}^*$ for max). > > **Why essential:** Without fathoming, all $2^n$ subproblems would have to be solved, making the algorithm exponential. Fathoming **prunes** large portions of the search tree, reducing the practical complexity dramatically. > *(1 mark per criterion, 0.5 mark for efficiency justification.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks): Branch and Bound with Candidate List Tracking** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Solve the following Integer Linear Programming problem using the **Branch and Bound** method (LP relaxation + LC selection rule). **Explicitly show the state of the Candidate List at every iteration.** **Maximize** $\;Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2$ **Subject to:** - $2x_1 + x_2 \le 8$ - $x_1 + 3x_2 \le 12$ - $x_1,\, x_2 \ge 0$ and **integer** **(7 marks)** **(b)** Construct the search tree, label each node with its LP bound and fathoming status, and verify the optimality of the final answer. Show that the Candidate List finally becomes empty. **(7 marks)** --- > **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** **Step 1 — Solve $S_0$ (LP Relaxation):** Binding constraints (intersection of $2x_1 + x_2 = 8$ and $x_1 + 3x_2 = 12$): $$ \begin{aligned} 2x_1 + x_2 &= 8 \quad (\text{multiplied by 3}) \;\Rightarrow\; 6x_1 + 3x_2 = 24 \\ x_1 + 3x_2 &= 12 \quad (\text{multiplied by 1}) \;\Rightarrow\; x_1 + 3x_2 = 12 \\ \hline \text{Subtract: } 5x_1 &= 12 \;\Rightarrow\; x_1 = 12/5 = 2.4 \\ 2(2.4) + x_2 &= 8 \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = 3.2 \end{aligned} $$ $$Z^{(0)} = 4(2.4) + 3(3.2) = 9.6 + 9.6 = 19.2$$ **Branch on $x_1$** (fractional). $\mathcal{C}_1 = \{S_1 (x_1 \le 2),\; S_2 (x_1 \ge 3)\}$. **Step 2 — Solve $S_1$ (with $x_1 \le 2$):** At $x_1 = 2$: $2(2) + x_2 = 8 \Rightarrow x_2 = 4$ (limited by $x_1 + 3x_2 \le 12 \Rightarrow 2 + 3(4) = 14 > 12$). So binding: $x_1 + 3x_2 = 12 \Rightarrow x_2 = 10/3$ at $x_1 = 2$. LP: $(2,\, 10/3),\; \bar{Z}_1 = 4(2) + 3(10/3) = 8 + 10 = 18$. **Step 3 — Solve $S_2$ (with $x_1 \ge 3$):** At $x_1 = 3$: $2(3) + x_2 = 8 \Rightarrow x_2 = 2$. Check: $3 + 3(2) = 9 \le 12$ ✓. LP: $(3,\, 2) \in \mathbb{Z}^2$! **Integer feasible!** $\bar{Z}_2 = 4(3) + 3(2) = 12 + 6 = 18$. **Update incumbent:** $\bar{Z}^* = 18,\; x^* = (3,\, 2)$. **Fathom $S_2$.** **Step 4 — Continue with $S_1$ (not yet fathomed, $x_2 = 10/3$ fractional):** Branch on $x_2$: $S_3 (x_1 \le 2,\, x_2 \le 3)$ and $S_4 (x_1 \le 2,\, x_2 \ge 4)$. **Step 5 — Solve $S_3$ (with $x_1 \le 2,\, x_2 \le 3$):** At $(2, 3)$: $2(2) + 3 = 7 \le 8$ ✓, $2 + 9 = 11 \le 12$ ✓. **Integer!** $\bar{Z}_3 = 4(2) + 3(3) = 8 + 9 = 17 < \bar{Z}^* = 18$. Fathom. **Step 6 — Solve $S_4$ (with $x_1 \le 2,\, x_2 \ge 4$):** At $x_2 = 4$: $x_1 + 12 \le 12 \Rightarrow x_1 \le 0$. So $(0,\, 4)$: $2(0) + 4 = 4 \le 8$ ✓. $\bar{Z}_4 = 4(0) + 3(4) = 12 < \bar{Z}^* = 18$. Fathom by bound. **Termination:** $\mathcal{C}_k = \varnothing$. STOP. **Candidate List Evolution Table:** | $k$ | Select | CL after | Incumbent $\bar{Z}^*$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | — | $\{S_0\}$ | $-\infty$ | | 1 | $S_0$ | $\{S_1, S_2\}$ | $-\infty$ | | 2 | $S_2$ (LC: 18 ≥ 18) | $\{S_1\}$ | **18** | | 3 | $S_1$ | $\{S_3, S_4\}$ | 18 | | 4 | $S_3$ | $\{S_4\}$ | 18 | | 5 | $S_4$ | $\varnothing$ | 18 | **Part (b):** **Search Tree:** ```mermaid flowchart TD A["S0: x = 2.4, 3.2, Z = 19.2"] --> B["S1: x1 le 2
x = 2, 10/3, Z = 18"] A --> C["S2: x1 ge 3
x = 3, 2 INTEGER
Z = 18 -> Zstar"] B --> D["S3: x2 le 3
x = 2, 3 INTEGER
Z = 17 FATHOMED"] B --> E["S4: x2 ge 4
x = 0, 4, Z = 12 FATHOMED by bound"] C --> Cf["FATHOMED: integer
incumbent updated"] ``` **Optimality Verification:** $\bar{Z}^* = 18$ is achieved at $x^* = (3, 2)$. All remaining subproblems were fathomed (either by bound or by being integer-inferior), so no better solution can exist. The CL is empty. **Final Answer:** $Z^* = 18,\; x^* = (3,\, 2)$. **(14 marks: 7 + 7)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Selection Rules** **(a)** Compare and contrast the **FIFO, LIFO, and LC (Best-Bound)** selection rules used to pick a subproblem from the Candidate List. Use a small example (2-variable ILP) to demonstrate how the choice of rule changes the number of LP solves and the size of the Candidate List at each step. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Prove that the **LC rule (Best-Bound)** yields the smallest possible Candidate List (in expectation) and is therefore theoretically the most efficient among the three. Mention any practical drawbacks. **(7 marks)** --- > **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** The three rules operate as follows on the Candidate List $\mathcal{C}_k$: | Rule | Selection Criterion | Data Structure | Effect | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | **FIFO** | Oldest subproblem (by insertion time) | Queue | Breadth-first exploration | | **LIFO** | Newest subproblem | Stack | Depth-first exploration | | **LC** (Best-Bound) | $S_j = \arg\max \bar{Z}_i$ | Priority Queue | Most promising first | **Example (using $S_0 \to S_1, S_2$ with $\bar{Z}_1 = 5, \bar{Z}_2 = 2$):** - **FIFO:** Picks $S_1$ (inserted first). Then $S_2$ at next iteration. **CL size at peak = 3.** - **LIFO:** Picks $S_2$ (inserted last). Then $S_1$ later. **CL size at peak = 3.** - **LC:** Picks $S_1$ (bound 5 > 2). Fathoms $S_2$ early via bound cutoff. **CL size at peak = 2 (smaller).** **Part (b):** *Proof sketch:* Under LC, any subproblem $S_i$ with $\bar{Z}_i \le \bar{Z}^*$ is fathomed immediately. Since the incumbent is non-decreasing and the bounds are non-increasing deeper in the tree, LC maximizes the number of fathoms per LP solve. By contrast, FIFO/LIFO may select low-bound subproblems that require many additional branches. **Drawback:** LC requires a priority queue, which has higher per-operation cost ($O(\log n)$ vs $O(1)$ for FIFO/LIFO), and it may use more memory if many subproblems have close bounds. **(14 marks: 7 + 7)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to update the incumbent** when an integer solution is found. *(−2 marks)* > 2. **Confusing $\le$ vs $<$ in bound fathoming** for maximization. Always use $\bar{Z}_j \le \bar{Z}^*$ to fathom. *(−1 mark)* > 3. **Drawing the search tree with wrong branching direction** (mixing $\le$ and $\ge$ side labels). *(−1 mark)* > 4. **Not explicitly showing the state of the Candidate List at every iteration** — this is mandatory in KTU 2024 scheme questions on B&B. *(−2 to −3 marks)* > 5. **Selecting a subproblem without stating the selection rule** used. Always mention FIFO / LIFO / LC explicitly. *(−1 mark)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Candidate List in Branch and Bound** > > - The **Candidate List (CL)** stores all *active* (unfathomed, unexplored) subproblems. > - CL is initialized with $\{S_0\}$; algorithm **stops when CL = $\varnothing$**. > - **Three fathoming criteria:** Infeasibility, Integer Feasibility, Bound Cutoff ($\bar{Z}_j \le \bar{Z}^*$ for max). > - **Three selection rules:** FIFO (queue, breadth-first), LIFO (stack, depth-first), LC (priority queue, best-bound). > - LC rule selects $S_j = \arg\max_{S_i \in \mathcal{C}_k} \bar{Z}_i$ for maximization problems. > - The incumbent $\bar{Z}^*$ is updated **only** when an integer-feasible solution is found. > - Branching: $x_r \le \lfloor x_r^{(j)} \rfloor$ and $x_r \ge \lceil x_r^{(j)} \rceil$. > - Worst-case tree size: $\le 2^n$ subproblems where $n$ = number of integer variables. > - LP bound $\bar{Z}_i$ always **overestimates** the true integer optimum in that branch (for max). > - Always **track and display the Candidate List** in KTU exam answers — it carries 2–3 marks by itself. > - LC rule is theoretically the most efficient but uses a priority queue (higher memory). > - Bound fathoming is the **key pruning mechanism** — it is what makes B&B tractable.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT757 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Short Term and Long Term - Simulated Annealing - Acceptance Probability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Simulated Annealing (SA) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a **probabilistic metaheuristic optimization technique** inspired by the physical process of **annealing in metallurgy**, where a metal is heated to a high temperature and then slowly cooled to reduce defects and reach a low-energy stable crystalline state. In SA, the search for a global optimum is performed by accepting not only better (lower cost) solutions but also, with a controlled probability, **worse (higher cost) solutions** to escape local optima.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, SA is classified under **stochastic global search methods** and is widely used for **combinatorial optimization** (e.g., TSP, scheduling, VLSI design, knapsack) as well as continuous nonlinear programming.

---

## 1.2 Short-Term vs Long-Term (in the context of Simulated Annealing)

The **"short term"** and **"long term"** behavior of SA refers to how the algorithm behaves across different stages of the **temperature schedule** $T_k$.

| Aspect | Short Term (High $T$) | Long Term (Low $T$) |
|---|---|---|
| Behavior | Explorative, random | Exploitative, greedy |
| Acceptance of worse solutions | High (almost 1) | Low (almost 0) |
| Analogy | A traveler walking randomly in a hilly terrain | A traveler carefully descending a valley |
| Markov chain length | Short per temperature | Long near freezing point |

> [!NOTE]
> **Short term** corresponds to the **early iterations** of SA where the temperature $T$ is high, allowing broad exploration of the search space. **Long term** corresponds to **late iterations** where $T \to 0$, forcing convergence to the best-so-far solution.

---

## 1.3 Acceptance Probability — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Probability** $P_a$ is the probability with which the Simulated Annealing algorithm accepts a candidate solution $s'$ that is **worse** than the current solution $s$ (i.e., $\Delta f = f(s') - f(s) > 0$). It is governed by the **Metropolis criterion** derived from the **Boltzmann probability distribution** of statistical mechanics.

The governing equation is:

$$P_a(\Delta f, T) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T}\right) \quad \text{when } \Delta f > 0$$

with the convention $P_a = 1$ when $\Delta f \leq 0$ (always accept improvements).

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber

Imagine a **climber** who is **blindfolded** on a hilly landscape, trying to reach the **lowest valley** (global minimum) in foggy weather:

- **Short-term (High Temperature):** The climber is willing to take **risky upward steps** almost randomly. This helps him escape **shallow valleys (local minima)**.
- **Long-term (Low Temperature):** The climber becomes cautious and **only steps downward**. He is forced to settle into whatever valley he is in.
- **Acceptance Probability:** The probability that the climber agrees to take an **uphill step** of height $\Delta f$ at temperature $T$.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the **temperature $T$** as the **boldness/curiosity** of the algorithm. A high $T$ = curious explorer. A low $T$ = focused exploiter.

---

## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants Referenced

- **Boltzmann constant**: $k_B = 1.380\,649 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- In most engineering applications of SA, $k_B$ is absorbed into the temperature parameter $T$, and $T$ is treated as a **dimensionless control parameter** in arbitrary units (a.u.).

---

## 1.6 Why SA is Important in KTU 2024 Syllabus

- Listed under **Module 4** as a **modern heuristic** alongside Genetic Algorithms, Tabu Search, and Particle Swarm.
- Tested frequently for its **acceptance probability derivation**, **cooling schedule design**, and **algorithmic working** in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).
- Industry relevance: **VLSI floorplanning, airline scheduling, neural network training, supply-chain design**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Foundation of Simulated Annealing

The algorithm follows these structured phases:

### Phase A — Initialization
1. Choose an **initial solution** $s_0 \in S$ (search space).
2. Choose an **initial temperature** $T_0 > 0$ (high enough so that $P_a \approx 1$).
3. Set the iteration counter $k = 0$.

### Phase B — Iterative Sampling
For each temperature level $T_k$:

1. Generate a candidate solution $s'$ from the **neighborhood** $N(s_k)$.
2. Compute the cost difference:
   $$\Delta f = f(s') - f(s_k)$$
3. **Decision rule (Metropolis criterion):**
   - If $\Delta f \leq 0$: **Accept** $s_{k+1} = s'$.
   - If $\Delta f > 0$: **Accept** with probability
     $$P_a = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T_k}\right)$$
     otherwise **reject**.

### Phase C — Cooling (Annealing Schedule)
Update temperature using a chosen **cooling law**:
$$T_{k+1} = g(T_k, k)$$

### Phase D — Termination
Stop when $T_k \to 0$, or $k \geq k_{\max}$, or no improvement over $L$ consecutive iterations.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all essential formulas** for SA as required in KTU 2024 exams.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acceptance Probability (Metropolis) | $P_a = \exp(-\Delta f / T)$ for $\Delta f > 0$ | $T > 0$ in a.u. |
| 2 | Boltzmann Distribution | $P(s) \propto \exp(-f(s)/T)$ | Thermal equilibrium |
| 3 | Logarithmic Cooling (theoretical, guarantees convergence) | $T_k = T_0 / \ln(1 + k)$ | Very slow |
| 4 | Geometric Cooling (most popular) | $T_k = \alpha \cdot T_{k-1},\ \ 0.8 \leq \alpha \leq 0.99$ | $\alpha$ = cooling ratio |
| 5 | Linear Cooling | $T_k = T_0 - \beta k$ | $\beta > 0$ |
| 6 | Cauchy Cooling | $T_k = T_0 / (1 + k)$ | Faster than log |
| 7 | Markov Chain Length per Temperature | $L = \vert S \vert$ or fixed | $\vert S \vert$ = neighborhood size |
| 8 | Equilibrium Condition | $\frac{\partial Q}{\partial t} = 0$ | Thermal equilibrium |
| 9 | Expected Acceptance Ratio | $\chi = 0.44$ (Aarts & Van Laarhoven) | Used to auto-set $T_0$ |
| 10 | Final Stopping Condition | $T_k < \epsilon$ or $k \geq k_{\max}$ | $\epsilon \approx 10^{-6}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important notational convention:** In SA literature, the cost function is often denoted $f$ (engineering convention) or $E$ (physics convention). KTU expects the engineering form $f$.

---

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why accept worse solutions?** Without this, SA would degenerate to **pure local search** (greedy hill descent) and **fail on multimodal landscapes**.
- **Why decrease $T$ slowly?** Too fast a cooling → algorithm behaves like a greedy search → trapped in local optimum. Too slow → wasted computational budget.
- **Why use $\exp(-\Delta f / T)$?** It is the **unique** function that:
  1. Approaches **0** as $T \to 0$ (no random moves when frozen).
  2. Approaches **1** as $T \to \infty$ (accept everything when hot).
  3. Decays **monotonically** with $\Delta f$ (worse steps are less likely).
  4. Comes from the **Boltzmann distribution** of statistical mechanics.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Acceptance Probability from Boltzmann Statistics

We begin with the **Boltzmann distribution** from statistical mechanics. For a system in **thermal equilibrium** at temperature $T$, the probability of being in a state with energy $E_i$ is:

$$P_i = \frac{\exp(-E_i / k_B T)}{Z(T)}$$

where $Z(T)$ is the **partition function**:

$$Z(T) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \exp(-E_j / k_B T)$$

### Step 1 — Form the ratio of two states

Consider two candidate states $s$ (current) and $s'$ (proposed) with energies $f(s)$ and $f(s')$. Their probability ratio is:

$$\frac{P_{s'}}{P_s} = \frac{\exp(-f(s')/k_B T)}{\exp(-f(s)/k_B T)}$$

### Step 2 — Simplify the ratio

$$\frac{P_{s'}}{P_s} = \exp\!\left(\frac{f(s) - f(s')}{k_B T}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Define the energy difference

Let $\Delta f = f(s') - f(s)$. Then $f(s) - f(s') = -\Delta f$:

$$\frac{P_{s'}}{P_s} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{k_B T}\right)$$

### Step 4 — In engineering convention, absorb $k_B$ into $T$

In computer science, $k_B$ is dropped (or equivalently absorbed into the unit of $T$). Thus:

$$P_a = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Handle both cases

- If $\Delta f \leq 0$ (improvement): $P_a = 1$ → **always accept**.
- If $\Delta f > 0$ (worsening): $P_a = \exp(-\Delta f / T) < 1$ → **accept with probability $P_a$**.

This completes the derivation of the **Metropolis acceptance criterion**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Geometric Cooling Schedule

We want a schedule $T_k$ that decreases monotonically, smoothly, and reaches a final temperature $T_f$ after $K$ iterations.

### Step 1 — Define the recurrence

$$T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

### Step 2 — Unroll the recurrence

$$T_1 = \alpha T_0$$
$$T_2 = \alpha^2 T_0$$
$$\vdots$$
$$T_K = \alpha^K T_0$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\alpha$ given $T_0$, $T_f$, $K$

$$\alpha = \left(\frac{T_f}{T_0}\right)^{1/K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU value:** $\alpha \in [0.8,\ 0.99]$ ensures **slow cooling** (a.k.a. the "anneal" effect). For fast cooling, use $\alpha \in [0.5,\ 0.8]$ (this is the **"quench"** regime).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Initial Temperature $T_0$ (Aarts-Van Laarhoven Method)

The initial temperature should be set so that **almost all** proposed worse solutions are accepted with probability close to 1. Using the desired initial acceptance ratio $\chi$:

### Step 1 — Define average worsening

$$\overline{\Delta f}^+ = \frac{1}{N^+} \sum_{i: \Delta f_i > 0} \Delta f_i$$

where $N^+$ is the number of uphill transitions in a sample.

### Step 2 — Set the mean acceptance to $\chi$

$$\chi = \frac{1}{N^+} \sum_{i: \Delta f_i > 0} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f_i}{T_0}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Approximation for small $\Delta f_i / T_0$

When $T_0$ is large, the sum is approximately:

$$\chi \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{\overline{\Delta f}^+}{T_0}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $T_0$

$$T_0 = -\frac{\overline{\Delta f}^+}{\ln \chi}$$

For the standard choice $\chi = 0.44$:

$$T_0 = -\frac{\overline{\Delta f}^+}{\ln(0.44)} = \frac{\overline{\Delta f}^+}{0.8206} \approx 1.218 \cdot \overline{\Delta f}^+$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Short Term vs Long Term Behavior

| Property | Short Term Behavior | Long Term Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature $T_k$ | $T_k \gg \overline{\Delta f}$ | $T_k \ll \overline{\Delta f}$ |
| Acceptance Probability (for $\Delta f > 0$) | $P_a \approx 1$ | $P_a \approx 0$ |
| Type of process | Exploration-dominated | Exploitation-dominated |
| Energy $f(s)$ | High variance | Converges to minimum |
| Number of accepted worsening moves | Many | Almost zero |
| Search radius | Large (jumps) | Small (fine-tuning) |
| Engineered analogy | Surveying a wide forest | Inspecting a single tree |
| Cooling law suitable | $T_k = T_0 / \ln(1+k)$ (slow) | $T_k = \alpha T_{k-1}$ (geometric) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Computing Acceptance Probability

**Problem:** In a SA run, current cost is $f(s) = 50$, proposed cost is $f(s') = 58$, and $T = 4$.

### Step 1 — Compute $\Delta f$

$$\Delta f = 58 - 50 = 8$$

Since $\Delta f > 0$, we must check the acceptance probability.

### Step 2 — Apply Metropolis criterion

$$P_a = \exp\!\left(-\frac{8}{4}\right) = \exp(-2) \approx 0.1353$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

- A **random number** $r \sim U(0,1)$ is drawn.
- If $r \leq 0.1353$, accept $s'$.
- Otherwise, reject and stay at $s$.

> [!TIP]
> This example shows that even a moderate temperature $T = 4$ results in only a **13.5%** chance of accepting a worsening of size 8. At $T = 0.5$ (long-term regime), $P_a = \exp(-16) \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-7}$ — virtually impossible to accept.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Simulated Annealing Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START"] --> B["Initialize s0, T0, k=0"]
    B --> C["Set s_best = s0, f_best = f(s0)"]
    C --> D["k = k + 1"]
    D --> E["Generate candidate s_prime from N(s)"]
    E --> F["Compute delta_f = f(s_prime) - f(s)"]
    F --> G{"delta_f <= 0 ?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Accept s = s_prime"]
    G -- No --> I["Draw r in U(0,1)"]
    I --> J{"r <= exp(-delta_f / T) ?"}
    J -- Yes --> H
    J -- No --> K["Reject s_prime"]
    H --> L["Update s_best if f(s) < f_best"]
    K --> L
    L --> M{"Equilibrium at T_k reached ?"}
    M -- No --> E
    M -- Yes --> N["Update T_k using cooling schedule"]
    N --> O{"Stopping criterion met ?"}
    O -- No --> D
    O -- Yes --> P["Output s_best, f_best"]
    P --> Q["END"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The inner loop (E → M) is the **"short-term"** behavior: many candidate trials at a single temperature. The outer loop (D → N) is the **"long-term"** behavior: gradual cooling across iterations.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Short Term vs Long Term Regime

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph short["SHORT TERM REGIME - High T"]
        A1["T is large"] --> A2["P_a approx 1 for all delta_f"]
        A2 --> A3["Exploration dominant"]
        A3 --> A4["Broad random jumps in search space"]
    end

    subgraph long["LONG TERM REGIME - Low T"]
        B1["T is small"] --> B2["P_a approx 0 for delta_f > 0"]
        B2 --> B3["Exploitation dominant"]
        B3 --> B4["Local fine-tuning only"]
    end

    short -- "Cooling schedule g(T,k)" --> long
    long -- "Final solution s_best" --> C["Global Optimum approx"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Diagram — Acceptance Probability Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["delta_f axis: 0 to large"] --> B["T axis: T0 high to Tf low"]
    B --> C["P_a surface"]
    C --> D["Near delta_f = 0 : P_a = 1 for all T"]
    C --> E["High T : P_a near 1 even for large delta_f"]
    C --> F["Low T : P_a near 0 for any delta_f > 0"]
    C --> G["Surface decreases monotonically in T"]
    C --> H["Surface decreases monotonically in delta_f"]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **acceptance probability surface** $P_a(\Delta f, T)$ is a 2D manifold that **decreases monotonically** in both $\Delta f$ and $1/T$. The **short-term** and **long-term** regimes are two opposite corners of this manifold.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a **stochastic metaheuristic optimization algorithm** inspired by the physical annealing process of metals. It explores the search space by probabilistically accepting worse solutions to escape local optima.
>
> **Role of Acceptance Probability:** It governs the **Metropolis criterion** — the rule
> $$P_a = \exp(-\Delta f / T)$$
