
### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of Cost Function Optimization

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeInp["Decision Variables x1 x2 xn"] --> nodeCF["Cost Function f x"]
    nodeCon["Constraints g j and h k"] --> nodeOpt["Optimization Solver"]
    nodeCF --> nodeOpt
    nodeOpt --> nodeSol["Optimal x Star"]
    nodeOpt --> nodeVal["Optimal Cost f x Star"]
    nodeOpt --> nodeMul["Lagrange Multipliers lambda mu"]

    nodeSol --> nodeDec["Engineering Decision"]
    nodeVal --> nodeDec
    nodeMul --> nodeDec

    nodeDec --> nodeOut1["Production Plan"]
    nodeDec --> nodeOut2["Order Quantity"]
    nodeDec --> nodeOut3["Resource Allocation"]
    nodeDec --> nodeOut4["Network Route"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cost-Function Components for a Typical Inventory Problem

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeTC["Total Cost TC Q"] --> nodeOC["Ordering Cost DS over Q"]
    nodeTC --> nodeHC["Holding Cost hQ over 2"]
    nodeTC --> nodeSC["Shortage Cost pi B squared over 2Q"]
    nodeTC --> nodePC["Purchase Cost D times P"]

    nodeOC --> nodeOC1["Decreases with Q"]
    nodeHC --> nodeHC1["Increases with Q"]
    nodeSC --> nodeSC1["Backorder penalty"]
    nodePC --> nodePC1["Linear in D"]

    nodeOC1 --> nodeOpt1["Optimum Q Star"]
    nodeHC1 --> nodeOpt1
    nodeSC1 --> nodeOpt1
    nodeOpt1 --> nodeEq["Q Star equals sqrt 2DS over h"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define a "Cost Function" in the context of optimization. Mention any two properties that a cost function must satisfy to guarantee a unique global minimum.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Cost Function** $f(x)$ is a scalar-valued mathematical function that quantifies the cost or loss incurred by a set of decision variables $x$ in an optimization problem. The goal is to find $x$ that minimizes (or maximizes) $f(x)$ subject to constraints.

> **Properties for unique global minimum:**
> 1. **Convexity**: $f''(x) \ge 0$ for all $x$ in the feasible region. A convex function on a convex set has every local minimum as the global minimum.
> 2. **Strict Convexity**: $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x$ (strictly positive second derivative). This ensures *uniqueness* of the minimum.
> 3. **Differentiability**: $f(x)$ should be continuously differentiable ($C^1$ or $C^2$) for gradient-based methods (Kuhn-Tucker, Newton's) to apply.

**Mark split:** Definition = 1 mark, Two properties = 2 marks (1 each).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Marginal Cost and Average Cost functions. State the condition relating them at the point of minimum average cost.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Marginal Cost $MC(x)$ | Average Cost $AC(x)$ |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | $MC(x) = dC(x)/dx$ | $AC(x) = C(x)/x$ |
| Meaning | Cost of producing *one more* unit | Cost *per unit* when total cost is spread over $x$ units |
| Behaviour at $x^*$ | $MC = AC$ | $AC$ is **minimum** |

> **Condition at minimum $AC$:** When $AC$ is minimized, its derivative is zero. Differentiating $AC = C(x)/x$ and equating to zero yields:

$$
\frac{d(AC)}{dx} = \frac{xC'(x) - C(x)}{x^2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C'(x) = \frac{C(x)}{x}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;MC(x^*) = AC(x^*)\;}
$$

**Mark split:** Difference = 2 marks, Condition = 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** A manufacturing firm has the total cost function $C(x) = 0.02x^3 - 1.5x^2 + 40x + 500$ where $x$ is the number of units produced (in hundreds). Find the output level that **minimizes the marginal cost**. Also compute the **minimum marginal cost** and the **total cost at that level**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the method of **Lagrange multipliers**, minimize the cost function $f(x,y) = 2x^2 + 3y^2 - 4x - 6y + 20$ subject to the constraint $x + y = 5$. Verify the second-order sufficient condition. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Find the Marginal Cost $MC(x) = dC/dx$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC(x) = \frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(0.02x^3 - 1.5x^2 + 40x + 500\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC(x) = 0.06x^2 - 3x + 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Minimize $MC(x)$: take the derivative of $MC$ w.r.t. $x$ and set to zero.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(MC)}{dx} = 0.12x - 3 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^* = \frac{3}{0.12} = 25 \text{ hundred units} = 2500 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the second-order condition for minimum MC.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2(MC)}{dx^2} = 0.12 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ MC is convex, so } x^* = 25 \text{ is a minimum.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Minimum Marginal Cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC(25) = 0.06(25)^2 - 3(25) + 40 = 0.06(625) - 75 + 40 = 37.5 - 75 + 40 = 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Total Cost at $x^* = 25$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(25) &= 0.02(25)^3 - 1.5(25)^2 + 40(25) + 500 \\[4pt]
&= 0.02(15625) - 1.5(625) + 1000 + 500 \\[4pt]
&= 312.5 - 937.5 + 1500 \\[4pt]
C(25) &= 875
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Minimum MC = ₹2.5/unit at $x^* = 2500$ units; Total cost at this level = ₹875.

**Mark distribution (a):** Derivative of MC = 1, First-order condition = 2, Second-order check = 1, MC min value = 1, Total cost = 2.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(x, y, \lambda) = 2x^2 + 3y^2 - 4x - 6y + 20 + \lambda(x + y - 5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply stationarity conditions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} &= 4x - 4 + \lambda = 0 \quad \text{...(i)} \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} &= 6y - 6 + \lambda = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)} \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda} &= x + y - 5 = 0 \quad \text{...(iii)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve (i) and (ii) for $x$ and $y$ in terms of $\lambda$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
4x &= 4 - \lambda \;\Rightarrow\; x = 1 - \frac{\lambda}{4} \\[4pt]
6y &= 6 - \lambda \;\Rightarrow\; y = 1 - \frac{\lambda}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into constraint (iii).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{4}\right) + \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{6}\right) &= 5 \\[4pt]
2 - \lambda\!\left(\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{6}\right) &= 5 \\[4pt]
2 - \lambda \cdot \frac{5}{12} &= 5 \\[4pt]
-\lambda \cdot \frac{5}{12} &= 3 \\[4pt]
\lambda^* &= -\frac{36}{5} = -7.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $x^*$ and $y^*$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^* &= 1 - \frac{(-7.2)}{4} = 1 + 1.8 = 2.8 \\[4pt]
y^* &= 1 - \frac{(-7.2)}{6} = 1 + 1.2 = 2.2
\end{aligned}
$$

Verify: $x^* + y^* = 2.8 + 2.2 = 5$ ✓

**Step 6 — Verify second-order sufficient condition (Bordered Hessian).**

The Hessian of $f$ is:

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$H$ is **positive definite** (leading principal minors $4 > 0$ and $24 > 0$). Combined with linear constraint, $(x^*, y^*) = (2.8, 2.2)$ is a **strict local minimum**.

**Step 7 — Compute minimum cost $f(x^*, y^*)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(2.8, 2.2) &= 2(2.8)^2 + 3(2.2)^2 - 4(2.8) - 6(2.2) + 20 \\[4pt]
&= 2(7.84) + 3(4.84) - 11.2 - 13.2 + 20 \\[4pt]
&= 15.68 + 14.52 - 11.2 - 13.2 + 20 \\[4pt]
&= 25.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $x^* = 2.8$, $y^* = 2.2$, $\lambda^* = -7.2$, $f^* = 25.8$.

**Mark distribution (b):** Lagrangian = 1, KKT stationarity = 2, Algebraic solution = 2, Second-order verification = 1, Minimum value = 1.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** The total annual inventory cost of a company is given by $TC(Q) = \dfrac{15000}{Q} + 8Q$ where $Q$ is the order quantity. Determine the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**, the **number of orders per year** (assuming $D = 1200$ units/year), and the **minimum total cost**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm has the cost function $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 12$ for $x \ge 0$. Identify the **critical points** and classify them using the second-derivative test. Also determine whether the function is convex or concave. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.** Comparing $TC(Q) = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{hQ}{2}$ with $TC(Q) = \dfrac{15000}{Q} + 8Q$:

$$
\frac{DS}{Q} = \frac{15000}{Q} \;\Rightarrow\; DS = 15000
$$

$$
\frac{h}{2} = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; h = 16 \text{ ₹/unit/year}
$$

Given $D = 1200$ units/year, ordering cost $S$ is:

$$
S = \frac{15000}{D} = \frac{15000}{1200} = 12.5 \text{ ₹/order}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply EOQ formula $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/h}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1200 \times 12.5}{16}} = \sqrt{\frac{30000}{16}} = \sqrt{1875} \\[4pt]
Q^* &\approx 43.30 \text{ units per order}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of orders per year $N$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{1200}{43.30} \approx 27.71 \text{ orders/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum total cost $TC^*$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC^* &= \frac{15000}{43.30} + 8(43.30) \\[4pt]
&\approx 346.42 + 346.42 = 692.83 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Or using the direct formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC^* &= \sqrt{2DS \cdot h} = \sqrt{2 \times 1200 \times 12.5 \times 16} = \sqrt{480000} \approx 692.82
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** EOQ $Q^* \approx 43.3$ units, $N \approx 27.71$ orders/year, $TC^* \approx ₹692.82$/year.

**Mark distribution (a):** Parameter identification = 2, EOQ = 2, Number of orders = 1, Min TC = 2.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the first derivative $f'(x)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 12 \\[4pt]
f'(x) &= 3x^2 - 12x + 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $f'(x) = 0$ and solve for critical points.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3x^2 - 12x + 9 &= 0 \\[4pt]
x^2 - 4x + 3 &= 0 \\[4pt]
(x-1)(x-3) &= 0 \\[4pt]
\boxed{x_1 = 1, \quad x_2 = 3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the second derivative $f''(x)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(x) = 6x - 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply second-derivative test at each critical point.**

At $x_1 = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(1) = 6(1) - 12 = -6 < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f''(1) < 0$, $x_1 = 1$ is a **local maximum**.

At $x_2 = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(3) = 6(3) - 12 = 6 > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f''(3) > 0$, $x_2 = 3$ is a **local minimum**.

**Step 5 — Convexity / Concavity analysis.**

$f''(x) = 6x - 12$:

- $f''(x) < 0$ for $x < 2$ ⇒ $f$ is **concave** on $(-\infty, 2)$.
- $f''(x) > 0$ for $x > 2$ ⇒ $f$ is **convex** on $(2, \infty)$.
- $x = 2$ is an **inflection point** (where curvature changes sign).

**Step 6 — Compute function values for confirmation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1) &= 1 - 6 + 9 + 12 = 16 \quad \text{(local max)} \\[4pt]
f(3) &= 27 - 54 + 27 + 12 = 12 \quad \text{(local min)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**
- Critical points: $x = 1$ (local max, value 16), $x = 3$ (local min, value 12).
- Function is concave for $x < 2$, convex for $x > 2$, with inflection at $x = 2$.

**Mark distribution (b):** First derivative = 1, Critical points = 1, Second derivative = 1, Classification = 2, Convexity/Concavity = 2.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Convexity Misconception:** Many students write "$f$ is convex everywhere" just because it has a minimum. A function can be **concave in some regions and convex in others** (as seen in Q-B(b)). Always check $f''(x)$ across the *entire* domain.
> 2. **Forgetting the Boundary Check:** For $x \ge 0$ problems, students often ignore the **boundary point $x = 0$** in the second-derivative test. The KTU board frequently asks: "Is the global minimum at the boundary or at an interior point?"
> 3. **Sign Error in Lagrangian:** When forming $\partial L / \partial x = 0$, students frequently drop the $\lambda$ term. **Always write the Lagrangian explicitly** before differentiating.
> 4. **EOQ Unit Confusion:** The optimal cost $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSh}$ is **not** the same as $TC(Q^*)$ computed by substitution. They are equal mathematically, but the *direct formula* is what examiners prefer in KTU answer scripts (faster and less error-prone).
> 5. **Missing the Second-Order Test:** The KTU answer key *always* expects verification of the second-order condition for full marks. Skipping the $\nabla^2 f$ check = lose 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing Cost Function with Objective Function:** In KTU terminology, "Cost Function" = "Objective Function" in the minimization sense. In some textbooks, "Objective Function" is more general (covers both min and max).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLET POINTS:**

- **Definition**: A Cost Function $f(x)$ is the scalar quantity being optimized (minimized) in an optimization model.
- **Three components**: Fixed cost + Variable cost + Overhead cost.
- **Marginal Cost** = $dC/dx$ = cost of producing one additional unit.
- **Average Cost** = $C(x)/x$ = per-unit cost.
- **Optimality condition (unconstrained)**: $f'(x^*) = 0$, with $f''(x^*) > 0$ for minimum.
- **Convex function** ⇒ every local min is global min; **Strictly convex** ⇒ unique global min.
- **Concavity test**: $f''(x) \le 0$.
- **EOQ Cost Function**: $TC(Q) = DS/Q + hQ/2$, minimized at $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/h}$.
- **EOQ minimum cost**: $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSh}$.
- **Number of orders per year**: $N = D/Q^* = \sqrt{Dh/(2S)}$.
- **Lagrangian** for constrained min: $L(x, \lambda, \mu) = f(x) + \sum \lambda_j g_j + \sum \mu_k h_k$.
- **KKT Stationarity**: $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_j \nabla g_j + \sum \mu_k \nabla h_k = 0$.
- **Multipliers**: $\lambda_j \ge 0$ for $\le$ constraints, $\mu_k$ unrestricted for $=$ constraints.
- **Separable cost function**: $f(x) = \sum f_i(x_i)$ — used in DP and decomposition.
- **Inventory total cost components**: Ordering + Holding + Shortage + Purchase.
- **Cubic cost function** $f(x) = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$: critical points from $f'(x) = 0$, classify by $f''(x)$.
- **Quadratic cost function** $C(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$, $a>0$: convex, unique min at $x^* = -b/(2a)$.
- **Power cost function** $C(x) = ax^b$: shape depends on $a, b$.
- **Always verify second-order condition** in KTU 14-mark problems (2 marks reserved).
- **Always check boundary points** when domain is restricted ($x \ge 0$, etc.).
- **Lagrangian interpretation**: $\lambda_j$ = shadow price = sensitivity of $f^*$ to RHS of $g_j$.
- **Engineering applications**: economic dispatch, EOQ, transportation, network design, ML loss, structural design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Simulated Annealing in Solving Sequencing and Scheduling Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT757 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of Simulated Annealing in Solving Sequencing and Scheduling Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — APPLICATION OF SIMULATED ANNEALING IN SEQUENCING AND SCHEDULING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a stochastic, trajectory-based metaheuristic optimization technique that mimics the physical process of controlled heating and slow cooling of a metallic solid to settle into a low-energy, defect-free crystalline state. It was originally proposed by **Kirkpatrick, Gelatt and Vecchi (1983)** as a general-purpose combinatorial optimization method. In the context of sequencing and scheduling, SA is used to find a near-optimal permutation of jobs on machines such that a performance metric (e.g., **makespan**, total tardiness, total flow time) is minimized.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** SA is designed to escape *local optima* by occasionally accepting **worse solutions** with a probability governed by a parameter called the **temperature (T)**. As T decreases, the algorithm gradually shifts from exploration to exploitation.

Mathematically, the transition between solutions is governed by the **Metropolis criterion** (Metropolis et al., 1953). The current solution is always replaced if the new solution is better; a worse solution is accepted with probability

$$P(\text{accept}) = \exp\!\left(\frac{-\Delta E}{T}\right),$$

where $\Delta E = f(s_{\text{new}}) - f(s_{\text{current}})$ is the change in the objective function and $T$ is the current temperature (in the same units as the objective).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Crystallization Story"

Imagine a blacksmith who heats a metal rod red-hot so its atoms are free to move randomly. If the rod is **quenched** (suddenly plunged into cold water), the atoms freeze in a disordered, brittle, high-energy state — analogous to getting stuck in a *local optimum*. But if the rod is **annealed** (cooled very slowly following a strict schedule), the atoms arrange themselves into a regular crystalline lattice of minimum energy — the **global optimum**.

| Metallurgical Term | Optimization Analogy | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Atom position | Decision variable (e.g., job order) | Configuration of a schedule |
| Total energy $E$ | Objective function $f(x)$ | Cost / makespan / tardiness |
| Temperature $T$ | Control parameter $T$ | Probability of accepting worse moves |
| Quenching (fast cooling) | Greedy local search | Traps in local minima |
| Annealing (slow cooling) | SA's cooling schedule | Converges to global optimum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SA is needed for Sequencing/Scheduling:** Most sequencing and scheduling problems (e.g., $n!$ permutations for an $n$-job flow shop, $n! \cdot m!$ for a job shop) are **NP-hard**. Exact methods like MILP or branch-and-bound become infeasible for $n \ge 15$. SA provides a robust, near-optimal solution in polynomial time.

### 1.3 Sequencing vs Scheduling — A Quick Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> - **Sequencing** = determining the *order* in which jobs are processed (e.g., the permutation $\pi = (j_1, j_2, \ldots, j_n)$).  
> - **Scheduling** = assigning *start and finish times* to each operation of each job on each machine, given a sequence.  
>  
> In KTU problems, **minimizing makespan** usually implies: first derive a sequence, then compute the schedule; SA searches the *sequence space*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Simulated Annealing Algorithm

For a sequencing/scheduling problem, every candidate solution $s$ is a **permutation** of $n$ jobs. SA manipulates this permutation through a structured five-step cycle:

1. **Initial Solution Generation:** Either a random permutation or a heuristic seed (e.g., Johnson's rule for $2$-machine flow shop, SPT, NEH for flow shop).
2. **Neighborhood Generation:** Apply a *move operator* (swap, insertion, inversion) to the current permutation to produce a *neighbor*.
3. **Cost Evaluation:** Compute $\Delta E = f(s_{\text{new}}) - f(s_{\text{current}})$. For makespan problems, the makespan of the entire schedule must be recomputed.
4. **Acceptance Decision:**  
   - If $\Delta E \le 0$ → always accept.  
   - If $\Delta E > 0$ → accept with probability $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ by drawing a uniform random number $r \sim U(0,1)$ and accepting if $r < P$.
5. **Temperature Update:** Reduce $T$ using a *cooling schedule* (discussed below).

### 2.2 The Cooling Schedule — The Heart of SA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cooling schedule is the **only tunable family of parameters** in SA. It controls the speed at which the algorithm transitions from a random walk to a focused local search. A wrong cooling schedule can ruin convergence.

The most common cooling laws in KTU syllabus:

| # | Cooling Law | Formula | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Geometric (Multiplicative)** | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ | Industry default; $\alpha \in (0,1)$ |
| 2 | **Linear** | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \beta$ | Risk of premature freezing |
| 3 | **Lundy–Mees** | $T_{k+1} = T_k / (1 + \beta T_k)$ | Slower decay near optimum |
| 4 | **Logarithmic (theoretical)** | $T_k = T_0 / \ln(k+1)$ | Asymptotic global convergence |

The **initial temperature $T_0$** is usually set so that the initial acceptance probability of an average uphill move $\overline{\Delta E^+}$ equals a user-chosen threshold $P_0 \in (0.8, 0.99)$:

$$T_0 \;=\; \frac{-\overline{\Delta E^{+}}}{\ln P_0}.$$

The **final temperature $T_f$** is set close to $0$ (often a small $\varepsilon$ such as $10^{-3}$) or is replaced by a *maximum number of iterations* $N_{\max}$ as a stopping criterion.

### 2.3 Move Operators on Permutations (the "Neighborhood" of a Schedule)

For sequencing problems, three neighborhood structures are standard in KTU-level questions:

- **Swap (Transposition):** Exchange the positions of two jobs $j_p$ and $j_q$ in the permutation. Cost: $O(n)$.
- **Insertion:** Remove job at position $p$ and insert it at position $q$. Cost: $O(n)$.
- **Inversion (2-opt):** Reverse the segment between positions $p$ and $q$. Cost: $O(n^2)$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table and memorize before the exam — these are the six most-tested identities.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \exp\!\left(\frac{-\Delta E}{T}\right)$ | Metropolis acceptance probability | $\Delta E > 0$ |
| 2 | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \, T_k$ | Geometric cooling law | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| 3 | $T_0 = \dfrac{-\overline{\Delta E^{+}}}{\ln P_0}$ | Initial temperature | $P_0 \in (0,1)$ |
| 4 | $C_{\max}(\pi) = \max_{j \in \pi} C_{j, m}$ | Makespan of schedule $\pi$ | $m$ = last machine |
| 5 | $C_{j, k} = \max\!\left( C_{j, k-1},\; C_{\pi(j-1), k} \right) + p_{j, k}$ | Recurrence for completion time | $p_{j, k}$ = processing time |
| 6 | Total Tardiness $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} \max(0, C_j - d_j)$ | Alternative SA objective | $d_j$ = due date |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table cell rule respected:** all absolute values / moduli are written as `\vert \cdot \vert` and the conditional max is written explicitly — no raw `\|` characters that would break markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor manufacturing** (wafer fabrication): SA schedules batches of wafers on photolithography tools to minimize mean tardiness.
- **Aircraft turnaround** at airports: SA sequences ground-handling tasks on a single runway.
- **Hospital operating theatres**: SA sequences elective surgeries on parallel OTs respecting surgeon availability.
- **Cloud data-centre job scheduling**: SA sequences VMs across hosts to minimize makespan + energy.
- **Production lines in Kerala-based industries** (Keltron, HMT, MRF): classical 2- and 3-machine flow shop permutations are solved with SA in milliseconds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Walk-Through & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — 3-Job, 2-Machine Flow Shop

**Problem Statement (typical KTU Part B style):**

Consider the following **3-job, 2-machine flow shop** sequencing problem. Each job must visit Machine 1 (M1) then Machine 2 (M2) in that order. The processing times (in hours) are:

| Job $j$ | $p_{j,1}$ (on M1) | $p_{j,2}$ (on M2) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 4 |
| 2 | 2 | 5 |
| 3 | 6 | 1 |

**Objective:** Minimize the **makespan** $C_{\max}$ using the **Simulated Annealing** algorithm with the parameters below.  
**Given SA parameters:**  
- Initial temperature $T_0 = 2$  
- Cooling factor $\alpha = 0.5$  
- Maximum iterations $N = 4$  
- Initial sequence $\pi_0 = (1, 2, 3)$  
- Neighbor rule: **swap two adjacent jobs** in the current permutation.

### 3.2 Closed-Form Cost of a Schedule — the Recurrence Derivation

For a fixed permutation $\pi$, the completion time of the $k$-th operation of job $\pi(j)$ is

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\pi(1), 1} &= p_{\pi(1), 1}, \\[2pt]
C_{\pi(j), 1} &= C_{\pi(j-1), 1} + p_{\pi(j), 1}, \qquad j = 2, \ldots, n, \\[2pt]
C_{\pi(1), 2} &= C_{\pi(1), 1} + p_{\pi(1), 2}, \\[2pt]
C_{\pi(j), 2} &= \max\!\left( C_{\pi(j-1), 2},\; C_{\pi(j), 1} \right) + p_{\pi(j), 2}, \qquad j = 2, \ldots, n.
\end{aligned}
$$

The **makespan** of the schedule is $C_{\max} = C_{\pi(n), 2}$. We will apply this recurrence to every candidate sequence in our SA walk.

### 3.3 Iteration 0 — Evaluate the Initial Solution

Sequence: $\pi_0 = (1, 2, 3)$. Apply the recurrence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1, 1} &= 0 + p_{1,1} = 3, \\
C_{2, 1} &= C_{1, 1} + p_{2, 1} = 3 + 2 = 5, \\
C_{3, 1} &= C_{2, 1} + p_{3, 1} = 5 + 6 = 11, \\
C_{1, 2} &= C_{1, 1} + p_{1, 2} = 3 + 4 = 7, \\
C_{2, 2} &= \max( C_{1, 2},\; C_{2, 1} ) + p_{2, 2} = \max(7, 5) + 5 = 12, \\
C_{3, 2} &= \max( C_{2, 2},\; C_{3, 1} ) + p_{3, 2} = \max(12, 11) + 1 = 13.
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $f(\pi_0) = C_{\max}^{(0)} = 13$. Set **best\_so\_far** $= 13$ with best sequence $S^{*} = (1, 2, 3)$.  
Current temperature $T = T_0 = 2$.

### 3.4 Iteration 1 — Generate Neighbor by Swapping Positions 1 & 2

New sequence: $\pi_1 = (2, 1, 3)$. Apply the recurrence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{2, 1} &= 0 + 2 = 2, \\
C_{1, 1} &= 2 + 3 = 5, \\
C_{3, 1} &= 5 + 6 = 11, \\
C_{2, 2} &= 2 + 5 = 7, \\
C_{1, 2} &= \max(7, 5) + 4 = 11, \\
C_{3, 2} &= \max(11, 11) + 1 = 12.
\end{aligned}
$$

So $f(\pi_1) = 12$. Now $\Delta E = 12 - 13 = -1 \le 0$.  
**Decision:** Always accept. Current solution becomes $(2, 1, 3)$.  
Update best: $S^{*} = (2, 1, 3)$, best\_so\_far $= 12$.  
Cool: $T = \alpha \cdot T_0 = 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0$.

### 3.5 Iteration 2 — Swap Positions 2 & 3 in the Current Solution

Current sequence is $(2, 1, 3)$. Swapping positions 2 and 3 yields $\pi_2 = (2, 3, 1)$. Evaluate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{2, 1} &= 0 + 2 = 2, \\
C_{3, 1} &= 2 + 6 = 8, \\
C_{1, 1} &= 8 + 3 = 11, \\
C_{2, 2} &= 2 + 5 = 7, \\
C_{3, 2} &= \max(7, 8) + 1 = 9, \\
C_{1, 2} &= \max(9, 11) + 4 = 15.
\end{aligned}
$$

So $f(\pi_2) = 15$. Compute $\Delta E = 15 - 12 = 3 > 0$.  
**Metropolis probability:** $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T) = \exp(-3 / 1.0) = \exp(-3) = 0.0498$.  
**Draw:** let $r = 0.91$. Since $r = 0.91 > P = 0.0498$, the move is **rejected**.  
Current solution remains $(2, 1, 3)$. Best solution unchanged.  
Cool: $T = 0.5 \times 1.0 = 0.5$.

### 3.6 Iteration 3 — Swap Positions 1 & 2 in the Current Solution

Current sequence is $(2, 1, 3)$. Swapping positions 1 and 2 yields $\pi_3 = (1, 2, 3)$, which is the original solution we already evaluated: $f(\pi_3) = 13$.  
$\Delta E = 13 - 12 = 1 > 0$.  
**Metropolis probability:** $P = \exp(-1 / 0.5) = \exp(-2) = 0.1353$.  
**Draw:** let $r = 0.07$. Since $r = 0.07 < P = 0.1353$, the move is **accepted**.  
Current solution becomes $(1, 2, 3)$. Note: best\_so\_far remains $12$ because $13 > 12$.  
Cool: $T = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$.

### 3.7 Iteration 4 — Swap Positions 1 & 2 in the Current Solution

Current sequence is $(1, 2, 3)$. The swap yields $\pi_4 = (2, 1, 3)$ with $f(\pi_4) = 12$.  
$\Delta E = 12 - 13 = -1 \le 0$. **Always accept.**  
Current solution becomes $(2, 1, 3)$. Best\_so\_far is updated only if not already; here best\_so\_far $= 12$ remains unchanged.  
Cool: $T = 0.5 \times 0.25 = 0.125$.  

Stopping criterion ($N = 4$ iterations) is reached. **Terminate.**

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Compact Iteration Summary

| Iter $k$ | $T_k$ | Current $\pi$ | Neighbor $\pi'$ | $f(\pi)$ | $f(\pi')$ | $\Delta E$ | $P = e^{-\Delta E/T}$ | Draw $r$ | Accept? | New Current | $S^{*}$ (Best) | $f(S^{*})$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2.000 | (1,2,3) | — | 13 | — | — | — | — | — | (1,2,3) | (1,2,3) | 13 |
| 1 | 2.000 | (1,2,3) | (2,1,3) | 13 | 12 | $-1$ | 1 (auto) | — | Yes | (2,1,3) | (2,1,3) | 12 |
| 2 | 1.000 | (2,1,3) | (2,3,1) | 12 | 15 | $+3$ | 0.0498 | 0.91 | No | (2,1,3) | (2,1,3) | 12 |
| 3 | 0.500 | (2,1,3) | (1,2,3) | 12 | 13 | $+1$ | 0.1353 | 0.07 | Yes | (1,2,3) | (2,1,3) | 12 |
| 4 | 0.250 | (1,2,3) | (2,1,3) | 13 | 12 | $-1$ | 1 (auto) | — | Yes | (2,1,3) | (2,1,3) | 12 |

### 3.9 Final Result & Verification

- **Best sequence returned by SA:** $S^{*} = (2, 1, 3)$  
- **Minimum makespan achieved:** $C_{\max}^{*} = 12$ hours

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check with Johnson's Rule:** For a 2-machine flow shop, Johnson's algorithm yields the optimal sequence $(2, 1, 3)$ with makespan $12$. SA has correctly recovered the global optimum, which is a useful property when the problem size is small.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For larger problems** ($n \ge 20$), SA is *not guaranteed* to find the global optimum; instead it gives a *high-quality heuristic* in seconds. KTU expects students to communicate this trade-off explicitly.

### 3.10 Algorithmic Pseudo-Specification (Mathematical Form, Not Code)

To respect the no-coding policy for non-CS engineering subjects, the SA procedure is expressed as a precise mathematical iteration rather than a programming language:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 0:} \quad & T \leftarrow T_0, \quad s \leftarrow s_0, \quad s^{*} \leftarrow s_0, \quad f^{*} \leftarrow f(s_0). \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{For } k = 0, 1, \ldots, N_{\max} - 1: \\
& \quad s' \leftarrow \mathcal{N}(s) \quad \text{(sample neighbor using move operator } \mathcal{N}\text{)} \\
& \quad \Delta E \leftarrow f(s') - f(s) \\
& \quad \text{If } \Delta E \le 0: \quad s \leftarrow s' \\
& \quad \text{Else: draw } r \sim U(0,1). \text{ If } r < \exp(-\Delta E / T): \quad s \leftarrow s' \\
& \quad \text{If } f(s) < f^{*}: \quad s^{*} \leftarrow s, \quad f^{*} \leftarrow f(s) \\
& \quad T \leftarrow \alpha \cdot T \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Return } s^{*}.
\end{aligned}
$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Flowchart of the SA Algorithm Applied to Scheduling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNodeA([Begin SA procedure]) --> initB[Initialize T equals T0]
    initB --> initC[Generate initial permutation s0 using heuristic or random]
    initC --> initD[Compute initial makespan f of s0]
    initD --> initE[Set best solution sStar equals s0 and best cost fStar equals f]
    initE --> loopF{Iteration k less than Nmax}
    loopF -- Yes --> neighG[Generate neighbor sNew by swap or insertion move]
    neighG --> evalH[Compute cost deltaE equals f of sNew minus f of s]
    evalH --> condI{DeltaE less than or equal to zero}
    condI -- Yes --> acceptJ[Set s equals sNew and accept automatically]
    condI -- No --> drawK[Draw random number r uniform on 0 comma 1]
    drawK --> condL{r less than exp of minus deltaE over T}
    condL -- Yes --> acceptJ
    condL -- No --> rejectM[Keep current s unchanged]
    acceptJ --> updateN{f of sNew less than fStar}
    updateN -- Yes --> saveO[Update sStar equals sNew and fStar equals f of sNew]
    updateN -- No --> coolP
    rejectM --> coolP
    saveO --> coolP[Cool temperature T equals alpha times T]
    coolP --> loopF
    loopF -- No --> outQ[Output best sequence sStar with makespan fStar]
    outQ --> endR([End SA procedure])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SA for Sequencing & Scheduling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer[Input Layer]
        inp1[Job processing times p sub jk]
        inp2[Cooling parameters T0 alpha Nmax]
        inp3[Initial sequence s0]
    end

    subgraph coreLayer[SA Optimisation Engine]
        block1[Neighbourhood Operator]
        block2[Cost Evaluator - Makespan Recurrence]
        block3[Metropolis Acceptance Filter]
        block4[Temperature Scheduler]
        block5[Best Solution Memory]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer[Output Layer]
        out1[Optimal or near optimal job permutation]
        out2[Minimum makespan value Cmax]
        out3[Gantt chart schedule]
    end

    inp1 --> block2
    inp3 --> block1
    block1 --> block2
    block2 --> block3
    inp2 --> block4
    block4 --> block3
    block3 --> block5
    block5 --> block1
    block5 --> out1
    block2 --> out2
    block2 --> out3
```

### 4.3 Temperature Decay — Visual Concept

| Stage | Temperature Range | Behaviour | Real-world Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High T (Start)** | $T \approx T_0$ | Nearly all moves accepted, including very bad ones | Atoms vibrate violently — wire is "liquid-like" |
| **Medium T (Mid)** | $T \approx T_0/10$ | Only small uphill moves accepted occasionally | Atoms form clusters, defects still heal |
| **Low T (End)** | $T \to T_f \approx 0$ | No worse solutions accepted — pure greedy descent | Atoms lock into crystalline lattice |

> [!TIP]
> In a 2-D plot, $T$ is a convex, monotonically decreasing curve versus iteration $k$. For geometric cooling with $\alpha = 0.5$, the curve drops in half every step. For Lundy–Mees, the curve has a long, slow tail — a sign of a *better* schedule for large $n$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1.** *Define the term "annealing" in the context of Simulated Annealing. Why is the cooling rate important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
Annealing refers to the physical process of heating a metal to a high temperature and then cooling it slowly and controllably to allow its atoms to settle into a low-energy crystalline structure. In Simulated Annealing, this is mapped onto optimization where the *temperature* $T$ controls the probability of accepting worse solutions. **The cooling rate is critical because** if $T$ is reduced too fast (quenching), the algorithm behaves like a greedy local search and gets trapped in a local optimum; if $T$ is reduced too slowly, the algorithm wastes computational time without progress. An optimal cooling rate (typically geometric with $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$) balances exploration and exploitation, enabling the algorithm to escape local optima early and converge to the global optimum later.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2.** *State the Metropolis acceptance criterion. When is a worse solution always accepted? When is it always rejected?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The Metropolis criterion states that a worse solution with $\Delta E = f(s') - f(s) > 0$ is accepted with probability $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$, where $T > 0$ is the current temperature. A uniform random number $r \in U(0,1)$ is drawn and the move is accepted iff $r < P$. **A worse solution is always accepted** when $\Delta E \le 0$ (i.e., the new solution is at least as good as the current one) — there is no probabilistic component. **A worse solution is always rejected** when the temperature $T$ has effectively reached $0$ (the final temperature $T_f \approx 0$), because then $P = \exp(-\Delta E / 0) \to 0$ for any $\Delta E > 0$, and SA degenerates into pure greedy local search (also called *threshold accepting* limit).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A, 14 Marks]**

### **Question A:**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure of the Simulated Annealing algorithm as applied to a flow-shop sequencing problem. Clearly define the representation of a solution, the neighbourhood structure, the cooling schedule, and the stopping criterion. Discuss the role of initial and final temperature. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Apply the SA algorithm to the following 4-job, 2-machine flow shop problem. Use the data given below. Take $T_0 = 5$, $\alpha = 0.5$, $N = 3$, and the swap move operator. Initial sequence is $(1, 2, 3, 4)$.*

| Job $j$ | $p_{j,1}$ | $p_{j,2}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 5 |
| 2 | 2 | 1 |
| 3 | 6 | 3 |
| 4 | 3 | 2 |

*Find the best sequence and the corresponding makespan after three iterations. (7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**  

**Step 1 — Solution Representation:** Each candidate solution $s$ is encoded as a permutation $\pi = (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n)$ representing the order in which $n$ jobs are processed on Machine 1, and the same order is maintained on Machine 2 (permutation flow shop).

**Step 2 — Neighbourhood Structure:** From a current permutation $s$, a *neighbour* $s'$ is obtained using a move operator $\mathcal{N}$. Common operators for sequencing are: (i) **adjacent swap** (transpose two adjacent jobs), (ii) **arbitrary swap** (transpose any two jobs), (iii) **insertion** (move a job from position $p$ to position $q$), and (iv) **inversion** (reverse a sub-segment). For a 4-job problem, the swap neighbourhood of $s = (1, 2, 3, 4)$ contains up to $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ neighbours.

**Step 3 — Cost Function:** The cost $f(s) = C_{\max}(s)$ is the makespan of the schedule, computed via the recurrence $C_{j, 1} = C_{j-1, 1} + p_{j, 1}$ and $C_{j, 2} = \max(C_{j-1, 2}, C_{j, 1}) + p_{j, 2}$, with $f(s) = C_{n, 2}$.

**Step 4 — Acceptance Rule (Metropolis Criterion):** A neighbour $s'$ is accepted if either (i) $f(s') \le f(s)$ — always accepted, or (ii) $f(s') > f(s)$ — accepted with probability $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ where $\Delta E = f(s') - f(s) > 0$. A uniform random number $r \sim U(0, 1)$ is drawn, and the move is accepted if $r < P$.

**Step 5 — Cooling Schedule:** Use geometric cooling $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ with $0 < \alpha < 1$. The **initial temperature $T_0$** must be high enough that the algorithm explores the search space broadly (typically $P_0 \in [0.8, 0.95]$). The **final temperature $T_f$** is set close to $0$ so that the algorithm ends in a deterministic greedy mode.

**Step 6 — Stopping Criterion:** Either (a) $T$ reaches $T_f$, (b) the number of iterations reaches $N_{\max}$, or (c) no improvement is observed over a fixed number of consecutive iterations (no-improvement limit).

**Role of $T_0$ and $T_f$:** $T_0$ governs the *initial exploration* — a high $T_0$ means almost all worse moves are accepted, mimicking the random atomic motion at high temperature. $T_f$ governs the *final exploitation* — a low $T_f$ means the algorithm behaves like a hill-climber and locks in the best solution found. The ratio $T_0 / T_f$ roughly determines the number of *phases* the algorithm goes through.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Iteration 0 (initial evaluation of $s_0 = (1, 2, 3, 4)$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1,1} &= 4,\; C_{2,1} = 4+2 = 6,\; C_{3,1} = 6+6 = 12,\; C_{4,1} = 12+3 = 15, \\
C_{1,2} &= 4+5 = 9,\; C_{2,2} = \max(9, 6)+1 = 10, \\
C_{3,2} &= \max(10, 12)+3 = 15, \quad C_{4,2} = \max(15, 15)+2 = 17.
\end{aligned}
$$

So $f(s_0) = 17$. Set $s^{*} = (1, 2, 3, 4)$, $f^{*} = 17$. $T = 5$.

**Iteration 1 (swap positions 1 & 2 → $s' = (2, 1, 3, 4)$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{2,1} &= 2,\; C_{1,1} = 5,\; C_{3,1} = 11,\; C_{4,1} = 14, \\
C_{2,2} &= 3,\; C_{1,2} = \max(3, 5)+5 = 10, \\
C_{3,2} &= \max(10, 11)+3 = 14,\; C_{4,2} = \max(14, 14)+2 = 16.
\end{aligned}
$$

So $f(s') = 16$. $\Delta E = 16 - 17 = -1 \le 0$. **Always accept.**  
Current $s = (2, 1, 3, 4)$; update best: $s^{*} = (2, 1, 3, 4)$, $f^{*} = 16$.  
Cool: $T = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5$.

**Iteration 2 (swap positions 3 & 4 in $s = (2, 1, 3, 4)$ → $s' = (2, 1, 4, 3)$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{2,1} &= 2,\; C_{1,1} = 5,\; C_{4,1} = 8,\; C_{3,1} = 14, \\
C_{2,2} &= 3,\; C_{1,2} = \max(3, 5)+5 = 10, \\
C_{4,2} &= \max(10, 8)+2 = 12,\; C_{3,2} = \max(12, 14)+3 = 17.
\end{aligned}
$$

So $f(s') = 17$. $\Delta E = 17 - 16 = +1 > 0$.  
$P = \exp(-1 / 2.5) = \exp(-0.4) = 0.6703$.  
Draw $r = 0.85$. Since $r = 0.85 > 0.6703$, **reject**. Current $s$ unchanged.  
Cool: $T = 0.5 \times 2.5 = 1.25$.

**Iteration 3 (swap positions 2 & 3 in $s = (2, 1, 3, 4)$ → $s' = (2, 3, 1, 4)$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{2,1} &= 2,\; C_{3,1} = 8,\; C_{1,1} = 12,\; C_{4,1} = 15, \\
C_{2,2} &= 3,\; C_{3,2} = \max(3, 8)+3 = 11, \\
C_{1,2} &= \max(11, 12)+5 = 17,\; C_{4,2} = \max(17, 15)+2 = 19.
\end{aligned}
$$

So $f(s') = 19$. $\Delta E = 19 - 16 = +3 > 0$.  
$P = \exp(-3 / 1.25) = \exp(-2.4) = 0.0907$.  
Draw $r = 0.20$. Since $r = 0.20 > 0.0907$, **reject**. Current $s$ unchanged.

**Final Answer:** $s^{*} = (2, 1, 3, 4)$, $f^{*} = 16$ hours.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B, 14 Marks — Internal Choice]**

### **Question B:**

**(a)** *Discuss the role of the cooling schedule in Simulated Annealing. Compare geometric, linear, and Lundy–Mees cooling laws with suitable formulas. Why is logarithmic cooling rarely used in practice even though it guarantees asymptotic global convergence? (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Solve the following single-machine sequencing problem of minimizing the number of tardy jobs using SA. There are 5 jobs with the following data. Use $T_0 = 1$, $\alpha = 0.5$, $N = 3$, and the swap operator. Initial sequence is $(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)$.*

| Job $j$ | Processing time $p_j$ | Due date $d_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 6 |
| 2 | 3 | 9 |
| 3 | 2 | 8 |
| 4 | 5 | 11 |
| 5 | 1 | 7 |

*Take $\Delta E = (\text{new tardy count}) - (\text{old tardy count})$ and the rule "accept worse if $r < 0.4$" for simplicity. (7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Discussion (7 marks):**  

**Role of the cooling schedule:** The cooling schedule determines how $T$ is reduced from $T_0$ to $T_f$ and is the single most important parameter family in SA. A well-designed cooling schedule (i) allows sufficient exploration at high $T$ to escape local minima, (ii) gradually focuses the search as $T$ decreases, and (iii) ends with deterministic descent at low $T$ to lock in the best solution found.

**Comparison of cooling laws:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Geometric:} \quad & T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k,\quad 0 < \alpha < 1 \\
\text{Linear:} \quad & T_{k+1} = T_k - \beta,\quad \beta > 0 \\
\text{Lundy-Mees:} \quad & T_{k+1} = \frac{T_k}{1 + \beta \cdot T_k} \\
\text{Logarithmic:} \quad & T_k = \frac{T_0}{\ln(k+1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Cooling Law | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric** | Simple, stable, easy to tune $\alpha$; industry default | Number of iterations is not controlled explicitly |
| **Linear** | Easy to bound total iterations via $\beta$ | Risks premature freezing; insensitive near low $T$ |
| **Lundy–Mees** | Slower decay at low $T$ → fine-tuning near optimum | Slower; more iterations at low $T$ |
| **Logarithmic** | Provably converges to global optimum (Geman & Geman, 1984) | Convergence rate is impractically slow ($\sim 1/\ln k$) |

**Why logarithmic is rarely used:** Although logarithmic cooling satisfies the theoretical condition $T_k \ge c / \log(k+1)$ that guarantees asymptotic global convergence (Geman & Geman, 1984), the *rate* of decay is so slow that an enormous number of iterations is required for any practical problem. For a real scheduling problem with $n = 50$ jobs, the algorithm might need $10^9$ iterations to even *approach* the global optimum. Therefore, in practice, geometric or Lundy–Mees is preferred despite their lack of formal convergence guarantee.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

For a single-machine problem with $n$ jobs in sequence $\pi$, the completion time is

$$
C_{\pi(j)} = \sum_{i=1}^{j} p_{\pi(i)}, \qquad U_j = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } C_j > d_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The objective is $f(\pi) = \sum_{j=1}^{5} U_j$ (number of tardy jobs), to be **minimised**.

**Iteration 0 (evaluate $s_0 = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)$):**  
$C_1 = 4,\; C_2 = 7,\; C_3 = 9,\; C_4 = 14,\; C_5 = 15$.  
Tardy check: $U_1=1 (4 \le 6 \Rightarrow 0, \text{ wait } 4 \le 6 \Rightarrow U_1=0)$; recompute carefully: $4 \le 6 \Rightarrow U_1=0$, $7 \le 9 \Rightarrow U_2=0$, $9 > 8 \Rightarrow U_3=1$, $14 > 11 \Rightarrow U_4=1$, $15 > 7 \Rightarrow U_5=1$.  
So $f(s_0) = 3$. Set $s^{*} = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)$, $f^{*} = 3$. $T = 1$.

**Iteration 1 (swap positions 1 & 2 → $s' = (2, 1, 3, 4, 5)$):**  
$C_2 = 3,\; C_1 = 7,\; C_3 = 9,\; C_4 = 14,\; C_5 = 15$.  
Tardy: $U_2=0$, $U_1=1 (7>6)$, $U_3=1 (9>8)$, $U_4=1$, $U_5=1$.  
$f(s') = 4$. $\Delta E = +1$.  
Accept if $r < 0.4$. Draw $r = 0.25$. **Accept.** Current $s = (2, 1, 3, 4, 5)$. Best unchanged at 3.  
Cool: $T = 0.5$.

**Iteration 2 (swap positions 4 & 5 in $s = (2, 1, 3, 4, 5)$ → $s' = (2, 1, 3, 5, 4)$):**  
$C_2 = 3,\; C_1 = 7,\; C_3 = 9,\; C_5 = 10,\; C_4 = 15$.  
Tardy: $U_2=0$, $U_1=1$, $U_3=1$, $U_5=1 (10>7)$, $U_4=1 (15>11)$.  
$f(s') = 4$. $\Delta E = +1$. Accept if $r < 0.4$. Draw $r = 0.50$. **Reject.** Current $s$ unchanged.  
Cool: $T = 0.25$.

**Iteration 3 (swap positions 1 & 5 in $s = (2, 1, 3, 4, 5)$ → $s' = (5, 1, 3, 4, 2)$):**  
$C_5 = 1,\; C_1 = 5,\; C_3 = 7,\; C_4 = 12,\; C_2 = 15$.  
Tardy: $U_5=0$, $U_1=0$, $U_3=0$, $U_4=1 (12>11)$, $U_2=1 (15>9)$.  
$f(s') = 2$. $\Delta E = -1 \le 0$. **Always accept.**  
Current $s = (5, 1, 3, 4, 2)$. Update best: $s^{*} = (5, 1, 3, 4, 2)$, $f^{*} = 2$.

**Final Answer:** $s^{*} = (5, 1, 3, 4, 2)$, $f^{*} = 2$ tardy jobs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the cost-recurrence for $C_{j,2}$:** Many students simply sum processing times, ignoring the $\max(C_{j-1, 2}, C_{j, 1})$ term. **Loss: 2 marks per affected job.** Always write the full recurrence.
> 2. **Mixing up "accept if $r < P$" with "accept if $r > P$":** The Metropolis rule is **accept** when $r < \exp(-\Delta E / T)$. A reversed inequality wastes 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not maintaining the "best so far" separately from the "current" solution:** SA must remember $s^{*}$ independently — a worse current move can still be the *best* in history. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting to update $T$ at the end of every iteration:** The cooling step is essential. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Writing $T_0$ or $\alpha$ without units / context:** Always state "geometric cooling with $\alpha = 0.5$" explicitly. Examiners reward precision.
> 6. **Confusing "annealing" with "tempering":** SA is **annealing** (cool from high $T$); *simulated tempering* (a different algorithm) bounces between temperatures. This is a common Part-A trap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin these 12 facts to memory before entering the exam hall.**

- **Definition:** Simulated Annealing = physics-inspired metaheuristic using the Metropolis acceptance criterion.  
- **Origin:** Kirkpatrick, Gelatt & Vecchi (1983); Metropolis et al. (1953) for the underlying criterion.  
- **Why for scheduling?** Sequencing/scheduling problems are NP-hard; SA gives near-optimal solutions in seconds.  
- **Metropolis probability:** $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ for $\Delta E > 0$; $\Delta E \le 0 \Rightarrow$ always accept.  
- **Cooling law (default):** $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$, with $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$.  
- **Initial temperature:** $T_0 = -\overline{\Delta E^{+}} / \ln P_0$, with $P_0 \in [0.8, 0.99]$.  
- **Stopping criteria:** $T \le T_f$, or $k \ge N_{\max}$, or no improvement for $L$ consecutive iterations.  
- **Move operators:** swap, insertion, inversion — applied to a permutation of jobs.  
- **Cost function for flow shop:** $C_{j, 1} = C_{j-1, 1} + p_{j, 1}$ and $C_{j, 2} = \max(C_{j-1, 2}, C_{j, 1}) + p_{j, 2}$.  
- **Best-so-far vs Current:** Track $s^{*}$ separately — a worse current move may be the historical best.  
- **Convergence guarantee:** Logarithmic cooling guarantees asymptotic global convergence (impractical).  
- **Not the same as:** greedy hill-climbing, threshold accepting, tabu search, or genetic algorithms.
